Files: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh (lines 31-58, 93-96), .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats (line 1270 assertion).

title: .beads/interactions.jsonl committed as runtime file in source control
description: .beads/interactions.jsonl is tracked as a committed empty file, but is not listed in .beads/.gitignore. This is a runtime file that accumulates LLM interaction history during beads sessions. Committing it — even empty — means every subsequent session will produce an untracked-change diff, polluting worktree state and causing spurious dirty-tree detections. Fix: add 'interactions.jsonl' to .beads/.gitignore (alongside the already-excluded workspace.db and lock files). The file at .beads/interactions.jsonl should be gitignored, not tracked.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: git PARTIAL cleanup has no error handling on git worktree remove
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 344, the git PARTIAL cleanup instruction is:

  git: `git worktree remove ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name>`

This has no error handling. git worktree remove will fail if the branch is checked out or if there are modified files in the worktree (which is possible for a PARTIAL result — partial changes may have been committed). When this fails silently, the worktree directory and git metadata remain, creating an orphan. No bead comment is added, no WARNING is emitted, and the orchestrator has no indication of the leak.

This contrasts with how worktree-remove.sh handles it (checks exit code, falls back to git worktree prune, emits WARNING). Suggested fix: add explicit error handling and fallback:
  git worktree remove --force ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name> || { echo 'WARNING: git worktree remove failed — running prune' >&2; git worktree prune; }

Note: the jj PARTIAL cleanup (line 345) handles jj workspace forget failure with a WARNING echo, but rm -rf failure is still unhandled (see related finding).

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Fixed in same commit as gl4.9 — git PARTIAL cleanup already had error handling from round 2 fix (line 344).

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj workspace list grep check can miss workspace if name contains special format chars
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 150–156, the workspace existence check uses:

  grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:" <<< "$_jj_ws_list"

The grep -qF (fixed-string) check requires the workspace entry to appear as 'worktree-<name>:' (with a colon immediately after the name). If jj ever changes its output format — e.g., adds spaces before the colon, changes delimiter — the grep returns non-zero even when the workspace exists, and _attempt_forget is set to false (line 155). The workspace is then silently skipped: no forget is attempted, jj_forget_failed remains false, the script exits 0, and the workspace metadata is leaked. The fallback on lines 151–153 catches 'missing colon' but NOT other format variations.

The script comment acknowledges this depends on the jj workspace list format, but the silent-success case (skip forget, exit 0 with leaked metadata) is the unsafe outcome. Suggested fix: when grep returns non-zero AND jj workspace list succeeded, default _attempt_forget=true (attempt forget and let jj report definitively) rather than silently skipping. jj workspace forget is idempotent for non-existent workspaces in recent jj versions.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: jj conflict-check code block is incomplete — missing closing elif body
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md lines 381–390, the conflict detection code block ends with an unclosed elif branch:

  ```bash
  elif echo "$_conflict_check" | grep -q 'CONFLICT'; then
  ```

The block has no closing fi, no body for the elif, and no indication to the model of what to do when a conflict IS detected inside this code block. The prose that follows (lines 392+) explains what to do verbally, but it is disconnected from the code block. An LLM agent reading this code block as an executable snippet will have syntactically invalid shell that fails at runtime, OR will skip the conflict handling entirely because the code block appears incomplete.

The intent (run jj undo on conflict) is documented in prose below the code block but the code block itself is a silent failure: it detects the conflict condition but provides no action. Suggested fix: complete the code block with the jj undo invocation and fi, or remove the code block entirely and rely on the prose instructions.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: rm -rf of PARTIAL jj worktree has no error handling
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, the PARTIAL cleanup section (line 345) instructs the orchestrator to run:

  jj: `jj workspace forget worktree-<name> || echo "WARNING: jj workspace forget failed" >&2; rm -rf ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name>`

The rm -rf has no error handling — if it fails (e.g., permission issue, name mangling), there is no warning, no bead comment, and no indication of the orphaned directory. This is a silent failure. The worktree-remove.sh hook handles this correctly (checks rm -rf exit code and emits ERROR + exits 1), but the inline cleanup guidance here does not.

Similarly the step 5 workspace cleanup (lines 444–449) says 'If jj workspace forget fails, log a warning but proceed with rm -rf' but the rm -rf instruction has no || handler shown either.

Suggested fix: Add explicit error handling to the rm -rf instructions:
  rm -rf ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name> || echo 'ERROR: failed to remove worktree directory — manual cleanup required' >&2
Or better: prefer calling the worktree-remove hook which handles both VCS deregistration and directory removal atomically.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Review gate FAIL (attempt 1): jj cleanup line has shell grouping bug — || after jj workspace forget only guards echo, not rm -rf. Also missed step 5 workspace cleanup at lines 444-449.

title: jj-init BATS verification fragment test has vacuous success assertion
description:
test_jj_init.bats line 112 ('verification: succeeds when jj st and jj log work') asserts:
  [ -z "$output" ]
This assertion is vacuous: the VERIFICATION_FRAGMENT by construction emits nothing on success (it only emits when jj_rc != 0). So the assertion [ -z "$output" ] can never fail when $status -eq 0, making it a non-test.

The useful assertion would be verifying that NEITHER 'WARNING' NOR any error text appears in the output — but the current assertion just confirms the tautology that an empty-output command produced empty output.

This matters because if the VERIFICATION_FRAGMENT were accidentally changed to emit output on success (e.g., a spurious echo), the test would still pass as long as status=0. The test should instead assert specific negative conditions (no WARNING, no 'jj git init --colocate may have failed') rather than the tautological [ -z "$output" ].

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats, test 'verification: succeeds when jj st and jj log work', criticality 7/10.

title: No test for jj workspace list output with workspace name but non-standard colon format triggering WARNING branch
description:
worktree-remove.sh contains a secondary grep branch (line ~120):
  elif grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}" <<< "$_jj_ws_list"; then
    echo "WARNING: workspace ... format differs from expected (missing colon separator) — attempting forget anyway"
This covers the case where the workspace name appears in 'jj workspace list' output but without the expected trailing colon.

test_worktree_remove.bats does have a test 'jj workspace list without colon format emits warning and attempts forget' (line 712) that exercises exactly this path. So this path IS tested. However, the WARNING assertion in that test only checks [[ "$output" == *"WARNING"* ]] and [[ "$output" == *"format differs from expected"* ]] but does NOT assert that jj workspace forget was actually called (i.e., there is no forget-arg.log check). If a future refactor broke the 'attempt forget anyway' part while keeping the warning, the test would not catch it.

Suggested fix: use create_logging_jj_mock or add a forget-called marker so the test asserts forget was invoked in addition to the warning being emitted.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, test 'jj workspace list without colon format emits warning and attempts forget'.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Already covered by existing test 'jj workspace list without colon format emits warning and attempts forget' at line 707.

title: jj-init BATS tests only cover gitignore and verification fragments; steps 1-3 have no shell-level tests
description:
test_jj_init.bats explicitly acknowledges it only covers GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT (step 4) and VERIFICATION_FRAGMENT (step 5), deferring steps 1-3 to behavioral evals. Steps 1-3 implement: (1) command -v jj check, (2) git rev-parse check, and (3) test -d .jj guard. These are the most critical guard rails — if jj is not installed and the command still proceeds, it causes a bad user experience. The behavioral evals (JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT5) cover these as model-behavior tests, not as executable shell tests.

The gap: the gitignore fragment is explicitly tested as a bash string in BATS (proving the shell logic is correct), but the prerequisite checks are only evaluated via behavioral evals which test model response quality — not shell correctness. Since jj-init is a slash command (not a shell script), this is partially acceptable, but a BATS test for the step 4 shell fragment that tests read-only .gitignore error message is missing the case where the parent directory is also read-only (preventing file creation).

More importantly: the verification fragment test at line 113 asserts [ -z "$output" ] on the success case, but the verification fragment itself does not emit any stdout on success — this assertion cannot fail in a meaningful way and may give false confidence.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats, test 'verification: succeeds when jj st and jj log work'.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: won't-fix — steps 1-3 are agent instructions, not extractable shell. Test file documents this scope (lines 7-10). Behavioral evals cover them.

title: PARTIAL cleanup for jj repos uses && operator — rm -rf skipped if workspace forget fails
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, the PARTIAL handling cleanup for jj repos instructs:
  jj workspace forget worktree-<name> && rm -rf ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name>
Using && means rm -rf is skipped if jj workspace forget fails. A PARTIAL result already indicates an incomplete fix — if workspace forget also fails (e.g., jj metadata corruption, workspace not fully registered), the worktree directory is leaked with no cleanup and no error message. Contrast with the FIXED path's jj cleanup (Step 5) which explicitly says 'If jj workspace forget fails, log a warning but proceed with rm -rf.' The PARTIAL path lacks this fallback logic entirely. Suggested fix: change the PARTIAL cleanup instruction to: 'jj workspace forget worktree-<name> || echo WARNING: ...; rm -rf ...<path>' so directory cleanup always runs regardless of workspace forget outcome, consistent with the FIXED path's Step 5 cleanup behavior.

title: VCS inference fallback in address-findings silently fails when worktree already removed
description: In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4 step 5 handles a missing VCS field from fix-worker via a multi-step inference procedure. Step 3 for jj repos reads: 'cd <worktree-path> && jj log -r @- --no-graph -T change_id.short(8) — reads the committed fix (parent of working copy). If it fails, mark FAILED.' However, the preceding Step 0 only checks 'test -d <worktree-path>' and falls through to inference 'if the path is accessible.' But if the worktree was cleaned up by the PARTIAL handling path (which removes the worktree directory), this Step 3 jj log command will fail. The instruction 'If it fails, mark FAILED' does not require surfacing WHY it failed (worktree removed vs. jj error), making post-mortem debugging harder. Additionally, the inference procedure runs VCS detection (step 1) on the main repo while trying to infer from the worktree — for a jj repo the change_id inference requires the worktree still to exist, creating a race condition if PARTIAL cleanup ran first. Suggested fix: explicitly check whether the worktree directory still exists before attempting step 3, and emit a distinct error message for 'worktree already removed during VCS inference' vs. 'jj log failed'.

title: jj workspace list output format dependence silently skips forget
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the workspace presence check uses grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:" on jj workspace list output. The trailing colon assumes jj always formats workspace list entries as 'workspace-name: ...'. If jj changes output format (drops colon, uses tabs, adds spacing), the grep silently fails to match and _attempt_forget is set to false — jj workspace forget is skipped with only an INFO log message, indistinguishable from the legitimate 'workspace not registered' path. The test file itself documents this as a known risk ('@test jj workspace list without colon format skips forget'). Suggested fix: emit a WARNING-level message when grep fails to match but a workspace directory was present, or attempt forget anyway with a warning.

title: Evals lack assertions for fix-worker CHANGE_ID vs WORKTREE_BRANCH output format distinction
description: RETRACTED: pr-review/evals/evals.json does contain B-FW1 and related evals (B-VCS-GIT1 pattern) that test fix-worker CHANGE_ID vs WORKTREE_BRANCH output. Confirmed coverage is present.

title: jj workspace list output format change would silently skip forget with exit 0 — no test guards against format regression
description: worktree-remove.sh uses grep -qF 'worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:' to detect if the workspace is listed before calling jj workspace forget. The trailing colon anchors the match. However, test_worktree_remove.bats line 677 ('jj workspace list without colon format skips forget') explicitly documents that if jj changes its output format to omit the colon, workspace forget is silently SKIPPED — the test asserts exit 0 and 'skipping forget'. This test documents the behavior but the behavior itself is a silent data-loss risk: if jj changes its output format, workspaces will never be cleaned up without any error. The correct behavior in a format-change scenario should be to either: (a) attempt forget anyway with a warning, or (b) exit 1 with an error. Currently a jj version upgrade that changes workspace list format would silently leak workspaces with no indication to the user. There should be a stricter test asserting that the script emits at minimum an INFO message when format parsing produces no matches, AND ideally that a real jj binary's workspace list output is sampled in CI. File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh

title: OR-syntax in contains assertions is never satisfiable — B-VCS-NONE1 and B-WC-COLLISION automated checks always fail
description:
pr-review/evals/evals.json, evals B-VCS-NONE1 and B-WC-COLLISION, introduced in this PR.

The evals.schema.json defines the 'contains' assertion type as a literal string match (no OR/regex support). Two new evals added in this PR use OR-syntax in their check fields:

  B-VCS-NONE1 cleanup-worktree assertion:
    type: contains, check: 'clean up OR remove OR delete'

  B-WC-COLLISION surfaces-error-to-user assertion:
    type: contains, check: 'already exists OR worktree path OR collision'

  B-WC-COLLISION suggests-cleanup assertion:
    type: contains, check: 'clean up OR remove OR delete OR rm -rf'

The string 'clean up OR remove OR delete' will never literally appear in any model response, so these automated assertions will always fail when run even for a correct model response. The pre-existing B1 evals have the same pattern but are out of scope.

Fix: Replace each OR-syntax check with the single most likely string a correct response will contain. For B-VCS-NONE1 cleanup-worktree use 'rm -rf'. For B-WC-COLLISION surfaces-error-to-user use 'already exists'. For B-WC-COLLISION suggests-cleanup use 'rm -rf'.

title: address-findings jj checkout: missing per-step error handling allows silent continuation after jj git fetch or gh pr view failure
description:
File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 1 step 3 (diff line ~9135-9146)

The jj checkout sequence in address-findings has no per-command error handling:

  jj git fetch
  BOOKMARK=$(gh pr view <number> --json headRefName --jq .headRefName)
  jj edit $BOOKMARK

Three silent failure paths:
1. If 'jj git fetch' fails (network error, auth failure), skill continues to 'jj edit' against stale/absent remote state with no warning.
2. If 'gh pr view' fails or returns empty string, $BOOKMARK is empty. 'jj edit $BOOKMARK' becomes 'jj edit ' which either fails with confusing jj error or operates on wrong revision. Unquoted $BOOKMARK also risks word-splitting if branch name has spaces.
3. The 'If checkout fails' prose handler below lists only git failure scenarios (PR not found, dirty working tree) — jj-specific failures (fetch failure, empty bookmark, jj edit failure) are not covered.

Suggested fix: Add error checks after each command:

  jj git fetch || { echo 'STATUS: FAILED — jj git fetch failed'; exit 1; }
  BOOKMARK=$(gh pr view <number> --json headRefName --jq .headRefName) || { echo 'STATUS: FAILED — could not determine PR branch'; exit 1; }
  [[ -n "$BOOKMARK" ]] || { echo 'STATUS: FAILED — headRefName was empty'; exit 1; }
  jj edit "$BOOKMARK" || { echo 'STATUS: FAILED — jj edit failed'; exit 1; }

Also update the 'If checkout fails' prose to cover jj-specific failure cases.

title: [code] jj checkout verification uses @- instead of @ after jj edit in address-findings
description: In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3, the jj verification command after 'jj edit <pr-bookmark>' is 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1'. This is wrong: after 'jj edit <bookmark>', @ becomes that exact commit. @- is the parent of that commit (e.g., the commit before the PR tip). So 'jj log -r @-' shows the commit BEFORE the PR, not the PR itself, and provides no confirmation that the edit landed correctly. The correct verification is 'jj log -r @ --no-graph -n 1' (no minus). Note: elsewhere in the SKILL.md the @- pattern is correctly used for inspection after 'jj commit' (where @ is an empty working-copy child and @- is the committed state) — but after 'jj edit' the semantics differ. File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 90

title: [code] jj/evals/evals.json references schema path that does not exist relative to file location
description: jj/evals/evals.json line 2 contains: "$schema": "../../pr-review/evals/evals.schema.json". From jj/evals/, two levels up is the repo root, so the resolved path is <repo-root>/pr-review/evals/evals.schema.json which does exist. However the relative path '../../pr-review/evals/evals.schema.json' resolves correctly only if the schema validator is run from jj/evals/. Some JSON schema validators resolve $schema relative to the file, others relative to CWD. The existing pr-review/evals/evals.json uses '$schema': './evals.schema.json' (same-directory relative path). Using a cross-directory relative $schema path is fragile and inconsistent with the sibling file. Suggested fix: either copy the schema to jj/evals/ and use './evals.schema.json', or use an absolute URI for the schema. File: jj/evals/evals.json line 2

title: [code] gh pr checkout in jj repo runs mutating git checkout in address-findings SKILL.md
description: In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3, the skill instructs running 'gh pr checkout <number>' unconditionally before branching on VCS type. In a colocated jj+git repo, gh pr checkout internally runs 'git checkout', which is a mutating git command that bypasses jj's HEAD management and corrupts jj's internal state (jj keeps git in detached HEAD and manages it itself). The jj VCS rule in CLAUDE.md explicitly prohibits mutating git commands when jj is present. The jj verification path that follows (jj git fetch + jj edit) is correct for jj, but it does not fix the damage done by the preceding gh pr checkout. Suggested fix: branch on VCS before the checkout step — for jj repos, skip gh pr checkout entirely and use 'jj git fetch && jj edit <pr-bookmark>' directly. For git repos, keep 'gh pr checkout <number>'. File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md lines 82-90

title: [tests] test_worktree_remove.bats POSIX fallback test may silently skip on CI (realpath available)
description:
test_worktree_remove.bats includes this test:
  @test "POSIX fallback: removes worktree when realpath unavailable" {
    command -v realpath &>/dev/null && skip "realpath is available"
    ...

The comment states this test 'ALWAYS self-skips on ubuntu-latest' where CI runs. The companion test 'POSIX fallback: removes worktree when realpath absent from PATH' (using a curated PATH) IS supposed to cover this in CI.

However, the curated-PATH test at line 2821 does NOT include 'jj' in the symlinked commands. The test works for the git path (no jj) but the jj POSIX fallback test at line 2853 DOES include jj via the clean_bin mock. The jj POSIX fallback test is well-constructed.

The issue: the self-skipping test documents accepted behavior but the skip message ('CI coverage note: this test ALWAYS self-skips on ubuntu-latest') embedded in a comment is not machine-verifiable. If CI ever changes platforms (macOS runner without realpath), this test would un-skip and potentially fail for unrelated reasons (different tmpdir behavior). The test should assert that the skip is expected rather than documenting it in a comment.

This is lower severity as the curated-PATH companion test provides equivalent coverage in CI, but the skip annotation creates a false sense that the test is exercised somewhere.

title: [tests] Parallel BATS execution risks symlink collision on shared BATS_TEST_TMPDIR/worktree-helpers.sh path
description:
Multiple tests in test_worktree_create.bats use the pattern:
  ln -sf BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../worktree-helpers.sh BATS_TEST_TMPDIR/worktree-helpers.sh

With bats --jobs 2 (the CI command in .github/workflows/check-skills.yml), tests within the same file share BATS_TEST_TMPDIR. Concurrent tests race to create the same symlink path. The RETURN trap also deletes patched scripts but not the shared symlink, creating a window where a parallel test could use a stale or missing symlink.

Affected: ~10 tests in .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats that use the patched-script pattern.

Fix: replace the shared symlink with per-test isolation using a mktemp -d subdirectory, placing both the patched script and the worktree-helpers.sh symlink inside it.

title: address-findings SKILL.md jj verification uses @- instead of @ after jj edit, checking wrong commit
description:
pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3: after running 'jj edit <pr-bookmark>', the verification step runs 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1'. This is semantically incorrect.

After 'jj edit <pr-bookmark>', @ IS the PR bookmark tip commit. @- is the parent of that commit (the commit before the PR). The verification would therefore confirm the parent commit is visible, not the PR commit itself.

The correct check after 'jj edit' is 'jj log -r @ --no-graph -n 1' (no minus sign), which shows the current working-copy commit (the PR branch tip).

Note: @- is correct in other contexts where a commit has just been made (e.g., after fix-worker commits, @ is an empty working-copy and @- is the committed fix). This is the inverse — after 'jj edit', no commit happened, so @ = the PR commit.

Suggested fix: Change 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1' to 'jj log -r @ --no-graph -n 1' on line 90 of address-findings/SKILL.md.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 90

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Resolved by h6b.12 fix — restructured checkout uses @ not @-

title: worktree-remove.sh exits 0 when jj workspace forget fails, masking VCS metadata leaks from callers
description:
.claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 194: when jj workspace forget fails (jj_forget_failed=true), the script still exits 0. The comment says this is intentional, but this means callers who check exit code will see success while workspace metadata was silently leaked. The ERROR is logged to stderr, so it is not completely silent, but any orchestration layer that checks exit code only will silently proceed without knowing the workspace entry is orphaned.

Risk: orphaned jj workspace entries accumulate over time, requiring manual 'jj workspace forget' cleanup with no automated signal.

Suggested fix: either (a) exit 1 when jj_forget_failed=true so callers see the failure, or (b) explicitly document that callers MUST check stderr for ERROR lines even on exit 0. The current comment justifies the behavior but does not document caller responsibility.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 190-194

title: worktree-remove.sh exits 0 when jj workspace forget fails, masking metadata leakage
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (final lines of the added block), the script does:

  if $jj_forget_failed || $git_prune_failed; then exit 0; fi

When jj workspace forget fails (jj_forget_failed=true), the script exits 0 after emitting ERROR to stderr. The directory was removed successfully, but the jj workspace metadata entry was not cleaned up. The hook caller (Claude Code framework) receives exit 0 and has no machine-readable signal that cleanup was incomplete — it must parse stderr to detect the failure.

The design is documented as mxm.1 in test comments ('exit 0 per mxm.1'), but worktree-remove.sh itself has no inline comment explaining why exit 0 is chosen. This creates a silent failure pattern: leaked jj workspace entries can cause confusing duplicate workspace names in subsequent jj workspace list output, with no indication to the framework that manual cleanup is needed.

Suggested fix: Add an inline comment explaining the design rationale (e.g., '# exit 0: directory removal succeeded (primary goal); jj metadata leak is recoverable via manual jj workspace forget'). Consider whether exit 1 would be more appropriate to signal partial failure to the caller.

title: test_worktree_create.bats: patched script tmp files left in hooks dir on test failure
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, multiple tests create patched copies of worktree-create.sh using mktemp in the hooks directory itself (not in BATS_TEST_TMPDIR):

  patched=$(mktemp "$BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../worktree-create-${BATS_TEST_NUMBER}-XXXXXX.sh")

This is done so that BASH_SOURCE[0]-based resolution of worktree-helpers.sh still works in the patched copy. The files are cleaned up with:
  trap 'rm -f "$patched"' RETURN
  rm -f "$patched"  # explicit removal after run

However, if a test is interrupted (e.g., by SIGTERM, a crash, or bats --no-cleanup), the RETURN trap fires on function return but the explicit rm -f after 'run' may not execute if the test is killed mid-run. More critically, the trap 'rm -f "$patched"' RETURN will clean up on normal RETURN but not on bats's internal test-process exit in all edge cases.

This means *.sh files matching 'worktree-create-*-XXXXXX.sh' could be left in .claude/hooks/ after test suite failures, and would then be picked up by the bats runner on the next run (bats .claude/hooks/tests/ runs all .bats files but not .sh files directly; however the file creation pattern is fragile for CI).

The CLEANUP_FAILED test at line ~1693 is particularly notable — it patches the script with 'exit 0' instead of 'false', creating a scenario where the cleanup rm -f may not run if the bats process itself exits abnormally.

Location: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, all tests using mktemp in BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../

Suggested fix: Use BATS_TEST_TMPDIR for the patched copies and replicate the necessary helper sourcing behavior by passing SCRIPT_DIR explicitly, or use a bats setup/teardown to track and clean temp files.

title: worktree-create.sh: mkdir -p for worktree parent fires before jj is verified available
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, mkdir -p for the worktree parent directory is called before the jj availability check. The sequence is:

  mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT" || { echo error; exit 1 }
  if [[ -d "${REPO_ROOT}/.jj" ]]; then
    if ! command -v jj &>/dev/null; then
      echo 'ERROR: .jj/ directory found but jj is not installed'
      exit 1   # <-- exits 1 here, EXIT trap fires cleanup_on_error

The problem: cleanup_on_error's 'else' branch for jj-not-installed emits a WARNING-level message (not ERROR), but the mkdir -p was already executed, meaning the parent directory was created. The cleanup_on_error function does clean it up via cleanup_empty_parent, but the test 'jj path: rejects when jj not installed' verifies [ ! -d "${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees" ] — this works because cleanup_on_error calls rm -rf on WORKTREE_PATH and then cleanup_empty_parent on WORKTREE_PARENT.

However there is a subtle correctness issue: the WARNING message says 'workspace metadata not cleaned; run jj workspace forget ... manually' — but the workspace was NEVER added (jj workspace add never ran), so this instruction is misleading. The WARNING at cleanup_on_error line ~3333 says 'run manually if needed' which is slightly better, but it still fires for the case where jj is not installed AND mkdir -p already ran — before any jj operation was attempted.

The more significant concern: mkdir -p fires before we know if jj is available. If the mkdir succeeds but jj isn't installed, cleanup_on_error must clean up a directory that was never intended to exist. The current code handles this correctly in tests, but it's fragile — the jj check should happen before mkdir -p to avoid creating partial state.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, the ordering of mkdir -p vs jj availability check.

Suggested fix: Move the jj availability check (command -v jj) to before the mkdir -p call, exiting early without creating any directories if jj is required but not present.

title: worktree-create: no test for jj workspace add partial-directory failure when cleanup rm also fails
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, cleanup_on_error (line 74) calls rm -rf WORKTREE_PATH and if that also fails, sets CLEANUP_FAILED=true and logs a WARNING. There is a test 'CLEANUP_FAILED promotes exit code from 0 to 1 when rm -rf fails after successful workspace add' (jj path, post-add failure). However, there is no test for the scenario where jj workspace add FAILS (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false) AND the subsequent rm -rf in cleanup_on_error also fails. In this case, _exit_code from the workspace add failure is non-zero, so CLEANUP_FAILED=true would NOT promote it (the promotion guard requires _exit_code==0). The WARNING about cleanup failure would still be emitted, but the test for this combined failure path is absent. If someone modifies the exit code promotion logic, this gap could hide a regression where the WARNING is silently dropped. File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 74-84. Criticality: 6/10 — affects operator visibility into leaked workspace directories.

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj workspace forget treated as hard failure but 'not found' patterns may not match all jj versions
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines ~155-160

The idempotent-forget check uses:
  if echo "$jj_err" | grep -qiE 'no workspace|unknown workspace|not found'; then

This regex attempts to match jj's 'workspace not registered' error messages. However jj's CLI error messages are not versioned/stable, and different jj versions may use different phrasing. If jj changes its 'workspace not found' error text (e.g. 'workspace does not exist', 'no such workspace'), the idempotency guard fails and the forget is treated as a hard error (ERROR: jj workspace forget failed... + jj_forget_failed=true + exit 1).

The consequence is that removing a worktree where the workspace was never registered (e.g. partial creation during a previous failed create) would emit a spurious ERROR and exit 1, even though the worktree directory is successfully deleted.

Suggested fix: broaden the pattern or accept any non-zero exit from forget as a warning when the workspace directory no longer exists (since the primary goal — removing the directory — has already succeeded). Alternatively, check 'jj workspace list' to confirm presence before calling forget, and skip forget entirely if absent.

title: worktree-remove.sh: exit 1 when jj absent silently makes directory removal look like a failure to the framework
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, last line (~line 196)

The script ends with:
  if $jj_forget_failed || $git_prune_failed; then exit 1; fi

When jj is not installed, jj_forget_failed is set to true (line ~139) and the script exits 1. However, by this point the worktree directory has been successfully removed (rm -rf succeeded at line ~175). The exit-1 from a metadata-only failure is indistinguishable from a hard failure to the Claude framework, which may retry or abort the whole operation.

The WARNING message correctly surfaces the problem, but the exit code conflates 'directory removed, metadata cleanup failed' with 'directory removal failed'. In environments where jj is uninstalled after workspaces were already created via this hook, every subsequent worktree-remove will fail with exit 1 even though the worktree is gone.

Suggested fix: treat metadata-only cleanup failures as warnings (exit 0) and rely solely on stderr output to surface them, or use a structured JSON response. The hard exit 1 should be reserved for cases where the worktree directory itself could not be removed.

title: worktree-create.sh: EXIT trap not disarmed on jj-not-installed early exit
description: RETRACTED: The EXIT trap firing when jj is not installed is intentional behavior, tested at test_worktree_create.bats:886-892. The test explicitly asserts the WARNING message from cleanup_on_error appears. The cleanup properly removes the parent directory. Not a bug.

title: worktree-create.sh: jj version detection grep not guarded against multi-line output
description: In worktree-create.sh lines 101-103, jj workspace add stderr is merged into stdout via 2>&1 and captured in jj_out. The old-jj detection grep uses grep -qiE with patterns like 'unexpected argument.*--name'. The dot (.) in POSIX ERE does not match newlines by default. If jj emits error output where 'unexpected argument' and '--name' appear on separate lines (e.g., a multi-line error message), the pattern would not match and the user would see a generic 'jj workspace add failed' error instead of the more helpful 'jj version too old' message. While current jj error format puts these on one line, this is brittle. The grep could add the -P flag with (?s) for POSIX systems that support PCRE, or restructure to grep for '--name' and 'unexpected\|unrecognized\|unknown' separately. File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh line 103.

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj workspace forget failure logged at ERROR but exit code depends on jj_forget_failed variable, not set on jj-not-installed path
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (diff lines 3518-3577)

When jj is not installed (command -v jj fails), the code sets _attempt_forget=false and emits a WARNING. jj_forget_failed remains false. The script then removes the directory successfully and exits 0. This is intentional per the test 'jj path: warns but still removes directory when jj not installed'.

However: the WARNING message in this path says 'workspace metadata not cleaned' which is a meaningful error — the jj workspace entry leaks. The caller receives exit 0 and no machine-parseable signal that cleanup was incomplete. The orchestrator in address-findings only checks exit code to decide whether to re-queue — it will treat this as clean success and not log the leaked workspace.

By contrast, when jj IS installed but forget fails with a non-'no workspace named' error, jj_forget_failed=true is set and the script exits 1. The inconsistency means jj-not-installed leaves a leaked workspace entry but reports success, while a jj forget error properly surfaces as failure.

Suggested fix: When jj is not installed but .jj/ exists (workspace metadata cannot be cleaned), consider setting jj_forget_failed=true so the exit code signals the incomplete cleanup. Alternatively, add a distinct exit code or structured JSON output on stderr so orchestrators can detect this condition. At minimum, the WARNING should include a machine-readable tag.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: jj checkout verification uses @ (working copy) not @- (committed state)
description:
File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md (diff around line 8845)

The jj PR checkout verification step reads:
  'Verify with jj log -r @ --no-graph -n 1'

In jj, @ is the working-copy commit which is typically an empty commit on top of the actual work. The correct revision to verify the checked-out PR bookmark state is @- (the parent of the working copy), not @. Using @ will show the empty working-copy commit and not confirm that the PR bookmark content is correctly checked out.

This is consistent with the known gotcha documented in CLAUDE.md: 'In jj, @ is the working-copy commit (typically empty). Use @- (parent) when verifying committed state.'

The vcs-detection-preamble.md itself also uses @- for verification (line in fix-worker step 8: 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1'). This is an inconsistency between address-findings Phase 1 step 3 and the fix-worker protocol.

Suggested fix: Change 'jj log -r @ --no-graph -n 1' to 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1' in address-findings Phase 1 step 3 checkout verification.

title: worktree-create.sh: mkdir -p failure path is dead code under set -e — EXIT trap fires instead of explicit handler
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh (diff lines 3240-3244)

The script arms the EXIT trap (cleanup_on_error) before the mkdir -p call:

  trap cleanup_on_error EXIT
  mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT" || {
    echo "ERROR: ..." >&2
    trap - EXIT
    exit 1
  }

Under set -euo pipefail, if mkdir fails, bash evaluates the || branch. The explicit 'trap - EXIT; exit 1' inside the || block correctly disarms the trap. This is fine.

However the real issue is more subtle: cleanup_on_error references $WORKTREE_PATH (to rm -rf) and $WORKSPACE_CREATED (to decide VCS cleanup). If a signal or unexpected early exit fires between the trap registration and the mkdir block, cleanup_on_error will run rm -rf on a path that may be legitimately unrelated. More importantly: the cleanup_empty_parent call in cleanup_on_error runs unconditionally even if WORKTREE_PARENT was never created, which is benign but wasteful and could mask real errors on some filesystems.

The more direct issue: if mkdir itself is interrupted (SIGINT during mkdir), set -e triggers the EXIT trap while the || body has not yet run, meaning the explicit 'trap - EXIT' is bypassed and cleanup_on_error runs with _exit_code \!= 0. This is a race: the explicit disarm is only reached via the || branch on non-zero exit from mkdir. Under SIGINT the || branch is not entered.

Suggested fix: Use an explicit if/then guard rather than || { ... } so the disarm is guaranteed regardless of how the branch is entered, or use trap - EXIT immediately before the mkdir and re-arm after.

title: eval B-JJ-ATMINUS1 not_contains assertion is a no-op due to literal \n in JSON string
description:
File: pr-review/evals/evals.json, eval id: B-JJ-ATMINUS1, assertion id: no-bare-at-for-verify

The assertion reads:
  { "id": "no-bare-at-for-verify", "type": "not_contains", "check": "jj log -r @\n" }

The \n in JSON is a literal backslash-n (two characters: \ and n), NOT a newline character. As a result the check string is "jj log -r @\n" — a string that would never appear verbatim in model output. The assertion can never fire, making it a permanent no-op that provides zero test coverage for the intended behavior (preventing bare `jj log -r @` for verification instead of `jj log -r @-`).

Fix: change the check value to "jj log -r @" (without the \n), since the goal is to detect any command using bare `@` as the revision specifier for verification purposes:
  { "id": "no-bare-at-for-verify", "type": "not_contains", "check": "jj log -r @" }

If trailing-newline sensitivity is genuinely needed, use regex mode or a separate test harness rather than embedding a literal \n in JSON.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text:
Closed: finding is stale — premise is factually incorrect. JSON 
 IS a proper newline escape (verified via Python repr()). The check string 'jj log -r @
' correctly matches 'jj log -r @' at end-of-line without false-matching 'jj log -r @-'. The 
 acts as an intentional word boundary.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: gh pr checkout in jj repos may leave inconsistent state with no error detection
description:
File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 1 step 3.

What is wrong:
The skill instructs the orchestrator to run 'gh pr checkout <number>' followed by jj-specific verification. However, 'gh pr checkout' uses 'git checkout' internally — in a colocated jj repo, this moves the git HEAD but does not update the jj working copy. The jj working copy continues to track its own @ commit, not the PR branch. The result is that 'git branch --show-current' (or even 'jj bookmark list') may show the PR bookmark, but 'jj st' and subsequent jj operations work on the old jj working copy state.

The verification step says: 'jj bookmark list | grep <pr-branch-name>'. If the PR bookmark is present (it was fetched by gh pr checkout), this grep succeeds — but the working copy may still be at the old commit, not the PR code. The next step 'jj new <pr-bookmark>' moves the working copy to the bookmark, but this is only attempted if the bookmark is NOT found. If the bookmark IS found (normal case), 'jj new' is skipped and the working copy is never updated.

This means: in a jj repo, the orchestrator proceeds on the wrong code base (its old working copy rather than the PR code) without any error being raised. fix-worker agents inherit this stale base.

Suggested fix:
For jj repos, always run 'jj git fetch' to fetch remotes, then explicitly run 'jj new <pr-bookmark>' (or 'jj edit <pr-bookmark>') to move the working copy to the PR code. Remove the 'if not found, fetch' conditional — always fetch and always move the working copy.

title: worktree-remove.sh: git worktree prune runs before rm -rf, so it cannot clean stale metadata when git worktree remove fails
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 164-177.

What is wrong:
When git worktree remove --force fails (line 164), the code falls through to git worktree prune (line 166). Prune returns exit 0 — so git_prune_failed stays false — but it has done nothing useful because the worktree directory still exists at the time prune runs. Git worktree prune only removes .git/worktrees/ entries where the directory is gone. Since rm -rf (line 177) runs AFTER prune, the directory is still present during prune, so prune skips the stale entry. After rm -rf removes the directory, the .git/worktrees/ entry is left orphaned with no subsequent cleanup attempt.

Result: The script exits 0 with no indication of the metadata leak. The git_prune_failed flag introduced in commit 790232d never fires because prune exits 0 — but prune's success is vacuous. The stale metadata persists in .git/worktrees/ until a future git worktree prune is run elsewhere.

Suggested fix:
Move git worktree prune to run AFTER rm -rf, so it actually operates on a directory-gone entry:

  git worktree remove --force $WORKTREE_PATH   # attempt graceful deregister+remove
  rm -rf $WORKTREE_PATH                         # ensure directory is gone
  git worktree prune                              # now prune can clean the orphaned entry

Or alternatively, when git worktree remove fails, skip prune before rm-rf, and run prune after rm-rf, setting git_prune_failed only if that post-rm prune also fails.

title: JJ-B-CLEANUP1 eval assertion 'jj rebase' check no longer matches rephrase fix
description: The JJ-B-CLEANUP1 eval in jj/evals/evals.json (line ~6744) describes a scenario where the worktree-create hook printed a cleanup error to stderr. The latest commit (72a08e7) rephrased this eval to test hook stderr surfacing—specifically replacing a 'Do NOT' assertion with a 'jj rebase' check. However, the CLEANUP1 scenario is about workspace forget errors from worktree-create.sh stderr, NOT about jj rebase. The 'jj rebase' check assertion ('tells-user-forget-command' → type:contains, check:'jj workspace forget') IS correct—but review of the commit message references fzymgc-house-skills-7ju.35 and mentions replacing a brittle 'Do NOT' assertion with a 'jj rebase' check. If the current eval file contains a 'jj rebase' check in JJ-B-CLEANUP1 rather than the 'jj workspace forget' check, that assertion would be wrong—a cleanup error from worktree-create.sh should prompt the user to run 'jj workspace forget', not 'jj rebase'. The eval assertions for JJ-B-CLEANUP1 show 'jj workspace forget' and 'worktree-my-feature' as checks, which are correct. However this should be verified against the committed eval file to confirm the turn-5 fix landed correctly.

title: worktree-remove.sh exits 0 for git metadata leak but exits 1 for jj workspace forget failure
description:
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills_worktrees/agent-ad5b4209/.claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh

Lines 157-165 (git path) vs line 180 (jj path).

What is wrong:
When jj workspace forget fails, worktree-remove.sh correctly sets jj_forget_failed=true and exits 1 at line 180. But when the git equivalent fails -- both git worktree remove AND git worktree prune fail (lines 159-163) -- the script still exits 0 after removing the directory. Stale entries in .git/worktrees/ are only surfaced as WARNING messages, not as a non-zero exit code.

This is an asymmetric contract: callers and orchestrators that check the exit code cannot distinguish 'git metadata leak' from 'clean success'. The jj case (workspace entry in jj state is data-integrity-critical) sets exit 1; git stale metadata (which can be pruned later) does not. While git metadata corruption is less severe than a jj workspace entry leak, the discrepancy means callers have no programmatic signal.

Suggested fix:
Add a git_prune_failed flag analogous to jj_forget_failed. When both git worktree remove and git worktree prune fail, set git_prune_failed=true and check it before exit 0. Then: if git_prune_failed; then exit 1; fi

Alternatively, document the intentional asymmetry in a comment if the design decision is that git prune failures are non-fatal by policy.

title: JJ-B-CLEANUP1 eval tests skill guidance, not hook behavior — cannot exercise actual worktree-create.sh error path
description: jj/evals/evals.json eval JJ-B-CLEANUP1 sets skill: 'jujutsu' and tests whether the skill tells the user to run 'jj workspace forget' after a worktree creation failure. However, what actually matters is whether the hook (worktree-create.sh) emits the ERROR on stderr so the agent can read and surface it — that behavior is already covered by the BATS test 'jj path: cleanup_on_error always attempts jj workspace forget even when workspace add fails'. The behavioral eval tests a downstream concern (what the skill *says* about the error) rather than verifying the integration path (does the hook emit the right message? does the skill/agent surface it to the user?). A more useful eval would verify that fix-worker or the orchestrating skill actually surfaces the cleanup ERROR from stderr to the user, not just that the jujutsu skill in isolation mentions 'jj workspace forget'. As written, the eval can pass even if the hook emits nothing useful, as long as the skill has generic recovery guidance. Consider rephrasing the eval as a fix-worker or address-findings behavioral eval where the input context includes the hook's stderr output and asserts the agent correctly identifies the manual recovery step.

title: cleanup_on_error trap attempts jj workspace forget before workspace is created
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, the EXIT trap is set immediately after validate_safe_name checks but BEFORE the jj workspace is created. The jj branch of cleanup_on_error always calls `jj workspace forget worktree-${NAME}` without checking whether WORKSPACE_CREATED==true (the git branch has this guard, jj does not). If the script exits early during `mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT"` or any other step before the jj workspace add succeeds, the trap fires and attempts to forget a workspace that never existed, producing a noisy ERROR message to the user. Additionally, `cleanup_empty_parent "$WORKTREE_PARENT"` is called unconditionally even when WORKTREE_PARENT was never created. Fix: add a WORKSPACE_CREATED guard to the jj branch of cleanup_on_error, mirroring the existing git guard. File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh in the cleanup_on_error function, jj branch.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — code at worktree-create.sh:31-83 already has WORKSPACE_CREATED guard (lines 44-48 differentiate ERROR vs WARNING). Comment at lines 40-42 explains intentional always-attempt-forget design.

title: jj-init.md step 4 gitignore fragment diverges from BATS test fixture
description: The .gitignore append command in jj/commands/jj-init.md step 4 uses a braced group with a fallback error message: `if \! append_err=($({ echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore; } 2>&1)); then ... ${append_err:-permission denied}`. The BATS test fixture in .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats hardcodes a different form: `if \! append_err=$(echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore 2>&1); then ... $append_err` (no braced group, no fallback). The BATS test validates the fragment character-for-character via GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT, so if the production command is ever edited to match the test or vice versa, both tests pass but the actual command file diverges. Currently the test fixture does NOT match the production fragment, meaning the BATS coverage does not verify the exact text Claude will execute. Fix: align the GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT constant in test_jj_init.bats with the verbatim fragment from jj-init.md step 4, or vice versa. File locations: jj/commands/jj-init.md line ~42 (step 4 block), .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT constant.

title: worktree-remove.sh exits 0 when jj workspace forget is silently skipped due to format mismatch
description:
In worktree-remove.sh, when jj workspace list output omits the trailing colon (e.g. a future jj version changes its format), grep -qF 'worktree-NAME:' at line 142 fails to match, _attempt_forget is set to false, and the script exits 0 with only a WARNING. The workspace metadata remains registered in jj's internal state with no error signal to the caller.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 142-144 and 181.

The test 'jj workspace list without colon format skips forget with warning' (test_worktree_remove.bats ~line 2745) explicitly documents and accepts this behavior, asserting status -eq 0. This means metadata leaks go unreported at the exit code level.

Suggested fix: distinguish between confirmed-absent (workspace name not present at all in jj workspace list output) versus format-mismatch (name present but without colon). For the latter case, fall through to attempt jj workspace forget anyway rather than skipping. Alternatively, use a looser grep pattern that does not require the colon, since the name is unique enough to identify the workspace.

title: jj workspace list format change silently breaks workspace forget in worktree-remove.sh
description:
The test 'jj workspace list without colon format skips forget with warning' (test_worktree_remove.bats line ~2745) correctly documents that the grep -qF 'worktree-NAME:' check in worktree-remove.sh depends on jj workspace list outputting a trailing colon after the workspace name.

The test documents the behavior but explicitly frames it as expected: if jj changes its output format to drop the colon, the workspace is silently skipped with a WARNING, leaving leaked workspace metadata. This is a real-world regression risk since jj is actively developed.

There is no corresponding test that verifies the skip-forget path surfaces a clear, actionable error message (rather than just a WARNING) when the format changes — the current WARNING message says 'not found in jj workspace list — skipping forget' which would be misleading if the format changed.

Suggested test addition: an integration test that verifies the output message is unambiguous and includes a manual recovery command when forget is skipped due to list parse failure. Alternatively, a comment in the source documenting the exact jj version where the colon format was confirmed would help calibrate the risk.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line ~3435 (grep -qF check), test at test_worktree_remove.bats line ~2745.

title: pr-review/evals: B-VCS-GIT1 eval referenced in test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats comment is missing from evals.json
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats line 14
File: pr-review/evals/evals.json

The lint test comment at line 14 of test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats reads:

  # Runtime invocation and correct branching behavior are covered by behavioral evals
  # (B-FW1, B-VCS-GIT1).

B-FW1 exists in pr-review/evals/evals.json. However, B-VCS-GIT1 does NOT appear anywhere in pr-review/evals/evals.json. A search of the file confirms no eval with id 'B-VCS-GIT1' exists.

This means the behavioral coverage claim for the git path of the VCS detection preamble (agents using git commands when .jj/ is absent) is not backed by an actual eval. The lint test correctly validates that agents reference the preamble by text presence, but the claimed runtime behavioral coverage for the git-only path is missing.

The gap: there is no eval verifying that fix-worker correctly detects a git-only repo (no .jj/) and uses git commit rather than jj commit, reporting VCS: git and WORKTREE_BRANCH (not CHANGE_ID).

Suggested fix: add a B-VCS-GIT1 eval to pr-review/evals/evals.json that tests fix-worker behavior in a git-only repo context, or update the test comment to remove the B-VCS-GIT1 reference if that eval was intentionally dropped.

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md: git path check uses git branch --show-current which returns empty in detached HEAD, causing false FAILED status
description: In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, the git worktree location check (step 2, git branch) runs: _branch=$(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null) || { echo 'STATUS: FAILED -- git branch check failed'; exit 1; }. In a git worktree that was created with a detached HEAD (no branch), git branch --show-current returns empty string with exit code 0. The subsequent case match 'worktree/*' fails on empty string, so the agent emits STATUS: FAILED and exits. git worktrees can legitimately be in detached HEAD state (e.g., when git worktree add <path> <commit> is used without -b). The error message 'On branch \'' (empty branch) is confusing and does not communicate the real issue. Suggested fix: after the git branch --show-current call, check if _branch is empty and emit a more specific error ('detached HEAD in git worktree — expected branch worktree/*') rather than falling through to the generic case match which produces a misleading empty branch name in the error.

title: worktree-remove.sh: _ws_list_err temp file leaks when jj workspace list fails (rm -f happens but EXIT trap references stale path)
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the elif branch at line 137 that handles 'jj workspace list failed' does rm -f "$_ws_list_err" (line 139) and then falls through without resetting _ws_list_err to empty. The EXIT trap at line 67 is: '[[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err"'. After the inline rm -f on line 139, _ws_list_err still holds the (now-deleted) path. When the EXIT trap fires at line 181 (trap -), it tries to rm -f the already-deleted path, which is harmless but can emit a spurious error if rm is strict. More critically, if the script exits abnormally before line 139 (e.g., due to set -e triggering inside the elif block), the file is cleaned up by the trap correctly. However if an error fires after line 139 removes it and before trap - is reached, the guard [[ -n $_ws_list_err ]] is true but the file is gone — harmless but indicates the design is fragile. The symmetrical cleanup in the success branch (line 142: rm -f "$_ws_list_err" + no reset) has the same issue. Suggested fix: after each inline rm -f "$_ws_list_err", set _ws_list_err='' so the EXIT trap guard correctly skips it.

title: mktemp failure in worktree-remove.sh jj branch silently marks jj_forget_failed without attempting workspace deregistration
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 128: _ws_list_err=$(mktemp 2>/dev/null) || true. When mktemp fails, _ws_list_err is empty string, the elif [[ -z "$_ws_list_err" ]] branch (line 133) fires, sets jj_forget_failed=true and skips the forget entirely. The script then exits 1 at line 182. Since jj workspace forget is idempotent for unregistered workspaces, attempting forget even without the pre-check list step would be safer than giving up. A mktemp resource-exhaustion failure causes the entire removal to exit 1 when the workspace deregistration might have succeeded. Suggested fix: when mktemp fails, skip the pre-check list but still attempt jj workspace forget directly; only set jj_forget_failed if forget itself fails.

title: jj git fetch / jj new failure not handled in address-findings checkout verification
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 1 step 3, the jj checkout verification path says: if the PR bookmark is not found after 'gh pr checkout', run 'jj git fetch' then 'jj new <pr-bookmark>'. No error handling is specified for these recovery commands. If 'jj git fetch' fails (network error, auth failure) or 'jj new' fails (bookmark still absent, detached state), the skill has no stop instruction — the agent would silently continue to Phase 2 on the wrong workspace, dispatching fix-workers against stale or incorrect code.

The downstream 'If checkout fails' handlers (PR not found, dirty tree, other error) explicitly cover 'gh pr checkout' failure but not the jj-specific recovery sequence.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 1 step 3 jj-specific verify block (around diff line 8604-8614).

Fix: Add explicit error handlers for the jj recovery path: 'If jj git fetch fails, stop: <error>. If jj new fails, stop: <error>.' before the downstream verify step.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: jj conflict-check code block falls through after _jj_log_failed in address-findings SKILL.md
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b jj repos section, step 3 (conflict check), the shell code block sets _jj_log_failed=true and then uses comment-only placeholders '# <cleanup step 5>' and '# continue to next finding' with no actual guard. Execution falls through to the next 'if echo "$_conflict_check" | grep -q CONFLICT' check even when _jj_log_failed is true. Since _conflict_check is empty/undefined on jj log failure, the CONFLICT grep will silently pass (no CONFLICT found), and the skill will proceed to the bookmark-set step (step 4) on a rebase whose state is unknown.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b jj repos step 3 (around line 254 in the diff, lines 8870-8879 in /tmp/pr31-diff.txt).

Fix: Add an explicit prose instruction to stop execution of steps 4 and 5 after logging the jj log failure. Replace the comment-only placeholders with a clear 'return/skip to next finding' directive, or restructure the code block so the CONFLICT-check only runs inside an else branch when _jj_log_failed is false.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: jj-init command uses grep without Bash tool permission in allowed-tools
description:
The jj-init command (jj/commands/jj-init.md) lists 'Bash(grep *)' in its allowed-tools. The CLAUDE.md project guidance states to use the Grep tool instead of raw grep. While this is a slash command (not a skill), it still instructs the model to run grep via Bash.

Additionally, the command also lists 'Bash(echo *)' in allowed-tools. For the .gitignore append operation (step 4), a Write tool call would be the idiomatic approach rather than echo redirection. The echo approach works but is more fragile (no error from the Write tool framework).

However, the more direct concern is that the Bash(grep *) allowed-tool conflicts with the project convention from CLAUDE.md which says 'MUST use [Grep/rg] never raw grep'. For a slash command running in a Claude Code session where the Grep tool is available, using Bash(grep) is a guideline violation.

Location: jj/commands/jj-init.md, YAML frontmatter allowed-tools section

Suggested fix: Remove 'Bash(grep *)' and 'Bash(echo *)' from allowed-tools. In the step 4 instruction, replace the echo redirection with Edit/Write tool instructions, and replace the grep -qxF check with a Read-then-check pattern or keep the Bash fragment but acknowledge the guideline exception with a comment.

title: jj workspace list failure silently leaks workspace metadata in worktree-remove.sh
description:
In worktree-remove.sh lines 133-136, when jj workspace list fails, the code emits a WARNING, skips jj workspace forget entirely, and proceeds to rm -rf the worktree directory. This results in an orphaned jj workspace registration: the directory is gone but jj still tracks the workspace in the op log.

The warning message includes a manual recovery command, but the workspace name used in the message is 'worktree-$WORKSPACE_NAME' (from the directory basename). If jj workspace list fails for reasons unrelated to workspace registration (e.g., a corrupt op store), the workspace IS registered and will remain as leaked metadata indefinitely.

More specifically: jj workspace list is used only as a health check, but when it fails, jj workspace forget is skipped without attempting the idempotent forget (which handles 'no workspace' gracefully as a non-error). The safer behavior would be to attempt jj workspace forget anyway when jj workspace list fails, since forget already handles the 'not registered' case.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 133-136

Current behavior:
  jj workspace list fails → skip forget → rm -rf directory → leaked metadata

Suggested fix: Replace the jj workspace list guard with a direct attempt at jj workspace forget (which already handles 'no workspace named' as non-error via the grep-qiE check at line 141). The workspace list check is defensive complexity that creates a worse failure mode.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: mktemp failure for _ws_list_err exits 0 despite leaked jj workspace metadata
description:
In worktree-remove.sh lines 131-132, when mktemp fails for _ws_list_err and jj IS installed, the script prints a WARNING and skips jj workspace forget. jj_forget_failed remains false. The script then proceeds to rm -rf the directory and exits 0 (line 172). The jj workspace metadata is permanently leaked in the repo, but the framework receives exit code 0 signaling complete success.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 128-132 and 172.

Fix: set jj_forget_failed=true in the mktemp-failure branch (lines 131-132) to propagate the failure, or document explicitly why exit 0 is correct when metadata is known leaked. Currently there is no signal to the caller that manual intervention is required.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: Idempotent jj workspace forget path in worktree-remove.sh is untested
description:
worktree-remove.sh lines 140-142 implement an idempotent-forget guard: when jj workspace forget exits non-zero but the error matches 'no workspace|unknown workspace|not found', the script emits a WARNING and treats the operation as success (jj_forget_failed stays false). This path is intentional and critical for idempotent teardown of orphaned worktrees.

No test in test_worktree_remove.bats exercises this branch. The existing 'handles workspace forget failure' test uses the error string 'Error: workspace forget failed for some reason', which does NOT match the idempotency regex, so jj_forget_failed=true fires correctly — but the WARNING-and-continue path is never hit.

To add coverage: create a mock jj that responds to workspace forget with exit 1 and stderr 'Error: No workspace named worktree-test-jj-wt'. Assert: status 0, directory removed, output contains 'not registered' or 'skipping', exit code is 0 (confirming jj_forget_failed did not propagate).

Production code: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 139-147
Test file: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj workspace list grep pattern uses undocumented ':' suffix — silent workspace leak if format changes
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 137

The code checks whether a jj workspace exists before calling 'jj workspace forget' by grepping for a colon-suffixed pattern in 'jj workspace list' output:

  elif \! echo "$_jj_ws_list" | grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:"; then
    echo "WARNING: workspace ... not found in jj workspace list — skipping forget" >&2

The ':' suffix is not part of the jj CLI contract. If a future jj version changes the format of 'jj workspace list' (removes the colon, adds color escape codes, changes the delimiter), grep finds no match and 'jj workspace forget' is silently skipped. The operator sees only a WARNING message indistinguishable from the genuine 'workspace does not exist' case, and the orphaned workspace entry persists in .jj/ requiring manual cleanup.

Additionally, the conditional 'elif [[ -n "$_ws_list_err" ]] && ...' at the start of this branch is redundant (the previous elif already handled the empty case), which suggests the guard was added defensively but actually makes the condition harder to read.

Suggested fix: Remove the pre-check entirely and call 'jj workspace forget' unconditionally. Treat exit codes indicating 'workspace not registered' as a non-error success case. This avoids the fragile output-format dependency:

  if \! jj_err=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace forget "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}" 2>&1); then
    # Accept 'no workspace named' as success (idempotent forget)
    if echo "$jj_err" | grep -qiE 'no workspace|unknown workspace|not found'; then
      echo "WARNING: workspace 'worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}' was not registered — skipping" >&2
    else
      echo "ERROR: jj workspace forget failed: $(sanitize_for_output "${jj_err:0:500}")"; jj_forget_failed=true
    fi
  fi

If jj does not provide a reliable error string for missing workspace, the pre-check can remain but should document the expected format and jj version floor.

title: Shell expansion in bd comments add pseudocode executes test -d .jj immediately
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4 step 5 VCS fallback, the following code block is shown as pseudocode to run:

bd comments add <work-bead-id> "STATUS: FAILED — VCS detection returned 'none'. Worktree directory removed. $(test -d .jj && echo 'WARNING: .jj/ exists — orphaned jj workspace metadata may remain. Run: jj workspace forget <name>')"

The $(test -d .jj && echo ...) command substitution inside the double-quoted string will expand immediately when the agent runs this shell command — it will run test -d .jj in the agent's current working directory at the time the bd command executes, not as a conditional interpolation. The <name> placeholder also reveals this is template pseudocode, creating confusion about whether agents should execute this verbatim.

Impact: An agent executing this code block would produce a comment with inconsistent content depending on whether .jj/ exists in CWD at the time bd runs. The correct fix is to either (a) remove the $() expansion and use a static warning message, or (b) split into a conditional shell block that builds the comment string before passing it to bd.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4 step 5 VCS fallback section.

title: address-findings: jj undo after conflict leaves workspace not cleaned up before re-queue
description: In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b Jj repos, step 3 (conflict handling): after a successful jj undo that restores pre-rebase state, the instruction says 'Clean up workspace (step 5). Mark FAILED, re-queue.' This correctly orders cleanup before re-queue. However, the conflict path at step 3 says to clean up workspace (step 5) BEFORE re-queuing — but step 5 is defined as: jj workspace forget + rm -rf. After a FAILED result that was re-queued, the fix-worker worktree still physically exists. The re-queued finding will dispatch a NEW fix-worker which creates a NEW worktree. The old worktree from the failed worker is cleaned up per the instructions, which is correct. BUT: in the step 3 instruction 'If _post_undo_tip \!= _pre_rebase_tip, STOP... Clean up workspace (step 5). Mark FAILED, re-queue.' — this escalation case says re-queue, but the previous sentence says STOP (escalate to user). There is an inconsistency: the _post_undo_tip verification failure says both STOP (escalate) and re-queue. These are contradictory. The conflict path (successful undo, tip matches) correctly says re-queue. The failed undo case (step 3 first branch) says STOP and escalate. The tip-mismatch case (step 3 second bullet) contradictorily says both STOP and re-queue. Fix: remove 're-queue' from the tip-mismatch escalation path — it should only say STOP and escalate to user.

title: jj integration: workspace forget uses WORKSPACE_NAME but orchestrator has only CHANGE_ID
description: In address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4b (Jj repos), step 5 instructs the orchestrator to run 'jj workspace forget worktree-<name>' and 'rm -rf ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name>'. Both operations require the WORKSPACE_NAME (the worktree directory basename), but fix-worker only reports CHANGE_ID for jj repos — not a workspace name. The orchestrator must derive the workspace name from the Task isolation metadata worktreePath (documented at Phase 4 step 5 in a different context), but Phase 4b step 5 does not explain this derivation — it jumps to 'jj workspace forget worktree-<name>' without specifying where <name> comes from. This gap is acute in the conflict/rollback path (step 3) and the PARTIAL cleanup path. The git path documents WORKTREE_BRANCH explicitly; the jj path should analogously document workspace name derivation. Without this, the orchestrator may attempt 'jj workspace forget worktree-<CHANGE_ID>' (wrong) or be unable to clean up. Suggested fix: in Phase 4b Jj repos step 5, add: 'Derive <name> from the worktreePath isolation metadata: basename <worktreePath>. The workspace name is worktree-<name>.'

title: CLEANUP_FAILED=true causes exit 1 even when original command succeeded — no bats test covers this path
description:
worktree-create.sh lines 79-81: when CLEANUP_FAILED is set but the original exit_code was 0, the script overrides the exit to 1. This protects against silent workspace leaks but there is no bats test that verifies this promotion logic fires.

The path requires: (1) a successful worktree add (so cleanup_on_error exits with 0 initially), then (2) cleanup itself partially fails setting CLEANUP_FAILED=true. The jj-gone-wt test (line 268) is the closest but it asserts status -ne 0 via the patched false-trigger — it does not verify the exit-code-promotion branch specifically (original exit 0 promoted to 1).

Without a test, a future refactor removing lines 79-81 would go undetected, allowing silent workspace leaks after a cleanup partial failure.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh:79

Suggested test: simulate cleanup_on_error firing from exit code 0 context (e.g., a mock jj that succeeds on workspace add, then use the patched script to make jj workspace forget fail AND rm -rf succeed so CLEANUP_FAILED=true but original _exit_code=0). Assert status -eq 1 to prove the promotion fires.

title: address-findings: silent failure when jj log -r <change-id> fails after rebase in conflict check
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b step 3 (jj conflict check), the conflict detection command is:

  jj log -r <change-id> --no-graph -T 'if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "OK")' 2>/dev/null | grep -q 'CONFLICT'

The `2>/dev/null` suppresses stderr. If `jj log -r <change-id>` fails entirely (e.g., the change-id is not found in the current repo view after rebase, or jj itself errors), the command exits non-zero but stderr is silenced and stdout is empty. The grep for 'CONFLICT' then returns non-zero (no match), so the `if` condition is false — the code treats the silent failure as "no conflict" and proceeds to `jj bookmark set` in potentially corrupt or undefined state.

This is a false-negative silent failure: an error in the diagnostic command causes the code to assume success.

Location: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b jj repos section, step 3 conflict check block.

Suggested fix: Capture the exit code of `jj log` separately before piping:

  _conflict_check_out=$(jj log -r <change-id> --no-graph -T 'if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "OK")' 2>&1)
  _conflict_check_rc=$?
  if [[ $_conflict_check_rc -ne 0 ]]; then
    # log command failed — treat as FAILED, run jj undo, re-queue
    ...
  elif echo "$_conflict_check_out" | grep -q 'CONFLICT'; then
    ...
  fi

Or at minimum, remove 2>/dev/null and let failures surface instead of being swallowed.

title: address-findings VCS inference fallback silently succeeds on detect_repo_root failure
description: In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4 step 5 (diff lines 8511-8537), when the fix-worker response omits the VCS field, the orchestrator falls back to detecting VCS in the main repo. The VCS detection snippet at line 8522 uses a subshell with echo; if the result is 'none', the skill adds a bead comment and continues. However, the bead comment template on lines 8528-8531 uses bash string interpolation ('test -d .jj && echo ...') inline in a bd comments add command, which may not execute correctly in all model invocations since this is instructional text for a skill (LLM prompt), not a real shell script. If the model misinterprets the nested quoting and the comment is malformed, the VCS failure is silently logged nowhere. Additionally, the text on line 8534 says 'Clean up the worktree directory: rm -rf <worktree-path>' but does not make clear this cleanup should occur even in the 'none' case. The real gap is: if VCS=none, the orphaned worktree directory is never cleaned up in the described procedure. The 'rm -rf' instruction appears in step 1's prose but the PARTIAL handling cleanup instructions (line 8561-8564) are separate and do not cover the none case. Recommendation: add an explicit 'rm -rf <worktree-path>' instruction in the VCS=none handler before the bead comment step.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — the recommended 'rm -rf <worktree-path>' instruction already exists at SKILL.md line 281, before the bead comment step.

title: No test for mktemp failure in worktree-remove.sh jj workspace list path
description:
In worktree-remove.sh line 3169, the secondary mktemp call for _ws_list_err uses `|| true`, so when mktemp fails _ws_list_err is empty and the code emits a WARNING via the elif branch. This branch is never exercised by any test.

The test_worktree_remove.bats has a test for the primary mktemp failure at startup ('exits with error when mktemp fails at startup'), but no test for the secondary mktemp failure that governs whether jj workspace list is run.

Missing scenario: jj repo present, jj installed, mktemp fails for _ws_list_err — expect WARNING about 'skipping jj workspace list check', exit 0, directory removed.

This branch is important because the WARNING contains a manual recovery command. Without a test, a refactor could silently break the soft-failure contract (exit 0 vs exit 1).

title: _ws_list_err trap references empty-string path, leaking temp file on non-jj remove path
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the EXIT trap (diff line 3111) guards cleanup with '[[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err"'. However, _ws_list_err is initialized to empty string (line 3110). When the script removes a git worktree (not jj), _ws_list_err is never assigned a temp file path — it stays ''. The trap guard '-n' on an empty string is false, so that branch is a no-op regardless. But more critically: when jj IS used and mktemp succeeds (line 3169), _ws_list_err is set to a temp file path. The rm calls inside the elif chain (lines 3176, 3179, 3182) delete it on success paths, but if the code falls through to the final 'rm -f "$_ws_list_err"' at line 3208 after already deleting it, that rm is harmless (rm -f is idempotent). So the trap condition '-n' is actually needed to avoid deleting the file twice on the non-jj path. The real risk is the opposite: if mktemp fails (line 3169 uses '|| true'), _ws_list_err stays ''. The code then checks '-z "$_ws_list_err"' (line 3172) and issues a warning, but the EXIT trap guard '[[ -n ]]' correctly skips the rm. This logic is correct but the interaction between '' initialization, the mktemp-failure '|| true' suppression, and the trap condition is extremely fragile and undocumented. The '|| true' on line 3169 silently swallows mktemp failure without setting an error code, causing the script to continue and then emit only a WARNING rather than failing. In a production cleanup scenario, this means jj workspace metadata leaks without clear user notification of the root cause (mktemp failure). Recommendation: replace '|| true' with a named error variable and log 'ERROR: mktemp failed — jj workspace will not be deregistered' with exit 1, or at minimum escalate from WARNING to ERROR for this case.

title: detect_repo_root leaks temp file on early-return success path
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root(), a temp file (_jj_err_file) is created via mktemp but only cleaned up via 'rm -f $_jj_err_file' on the failure path. When jj root succeeds (the early return at line ~3021-3024 in the diff), the function returns 0 before the rm -f call, leaking the temp file. The function has no enclosing trap, so there is nothing to catch this.
