Fix: add 'rm -f "$_jj_err_file"' immediately before the 'return 0' on the success path (around diff line 3023), or restructure with a local trap 'rm -f "$_jj_err_file"' EXIT within the function.

title: detect_repo_root emits misleading 'jj not in PATH' when mktemp fails
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root() has a misleading error message when mktemp fails inside the jj root detection path.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh lines 53-84

Flow when mktemp fails:
1. jj IS in PATH, so jj_attempted is set to true (line 56)
2. mktemp fails (line 59) — jj_attempted is reset to false (line 61), WARNING emitted
3. The jj root call is skipped
4. Code falls through to line 73: if [[ jj_attempted == true ]] — false
5. Else branch fires at line 82: prints 'ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse failed; jj not in PATH)'

The message 'jj not in PATH' is factually incorrect — jj IS in PATH; mktemp was the failure.

Existing test gap: test_worktree_helpers.bats line 1480 verifies the WARNING but does not assert the absence of the misleading ERROR message.

Fix: Track jj PATH availability separately from jj_attempted. For example, add a jj_in_path variable set when command -v jj succeeds. Update the else branch error message to reference 'mktemp failed, jj root skipped' when jj was found but mktemp blocked the attempt.
close_reason: Duplicate of oy7.26 — same misleading error message bug

title: Temp file _ws_list_err leaked when jj not installed in worktree-remove.sh
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, when .jj/ exists but jj is not installed, a temp file is created at line 120 (_ws_list_err=$(mktemp 2>/dev/null) || true) but never deleted.

The 'jj not installed' branch (lines 121-122) emits a WARNING and falls through to directory removal (line 151), then rm -f _root_err_file (line 158), then trap - EXIT (line 159). None of these steps delete _ws_list_err.

The EXIT trap at line 62 would have cleaned it up, but trap - EXIT cancels that trap before the script exits normally. On the success path, only _root_err_file is explicitly cleaned at line 158.

Affected file: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 120-122 and 158.

Fix: add 'rm -f "$_ws_list_err"' before 'trap - EXIT' at line 159 alongside the existing _root_err_file cleanup. The _ws_list_err file is already cleaned in the branches that use it (lines 127, 130, 133) but not in the 'jj not installed' branch.

title: detect_repo_root mktemp failure emits misleading 'jj not in PATH' error
description: In worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root(), when mktemp fails, jj_attempted is reset to false. The subsequent error path falls into the else branch that prints 'ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse failed; jj not in PATH)' — even though jj IS in PATH. The existing mktemp failure test (test_worktree_helpers.bats ~line 1480) correctly verifies the WARNING about mktemp failure, but does not assert that the subsequent error message does not contain the misleading 'jj not in PATH' claim. Suggested test addition: [[ "$output" \!= *"jj not in PATH"* ]] alongside the existing mktemp WARNING assertion. Alternatively, the implementation should use a more accurate message when jj_attempted was attempted but blocked by mktemp.

title: worktree-remove.sh: mktemp failure inside elif produces misleading 'workspace not found' warning
description: Closing as duplicate of fzymgc-house-skills-oy7.9 (same finding, different aspect reviewer)

title: detect_repo_root: mktemp failure causes misleading error and jj stderr loss
description:
In worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root() (line 59-63), when mktemp fails, _jj_err_file is set to empty string and jj_rc is set to 1 via || { jj_rc=1; }. The subsequent jj root 2>"$_jj_err_file" then redirects stderr to an empty-string path, which bash cannot open. This causes the entire jj command to fail due to the redirect failure, not due to jj itself. The reported error says 'jj root failed' with no jj error text (since jj_err is empty), when the real failure was mktemp. The actual jj output is completely lost.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh lines 59-63

Fix: Add a guard so that if mktemp fails, skip jj entirely with a clear message:
  _jj_err_file=$(mktemp) || { echo 'ERROR: mktemp failed — cannot check jj root' >&2; return 1; }
Or set jj_attempted=false when mktemp fails so the correct error branch is taken.

title: Missing test: worktree-remove.sh jj workspace forget uses bare name not worktree-prefix when workspace lacks prefix
description: In worktree-remove.sh, the jj workspace forget call constructs the workspace name as 'worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}' where WORKSPACE_NAME=basename of the worktree directory path. This is consistent with worktree-create.sh which creates workspaces with --name 'worktree-${NAME}'. However, if a jj workspace was created outside this hook system (e.g., via 'jj workspace add /path/foo' directly, where jj auto-generates a name like 'foo' without the 'worktree-' prefix), the remove hook will look for 'worktree-foo' in jj workspace list, fail to find it, warn, and skip workspace forget — leaving stale jj workspace metadata. There is no BATS test for this scenario. The test would create a mock jj whose workspace list shows a bare name (e.g. 'foo:' rather than 'worktree-foo:') for the worktree path being removed, and verify that: (1) the script warns about not finding the workspace, (2) still removes the directory, (3) exits 0. This gap matters for adoption scenarios where some workspaces were created manually before this PR. Criticality: 6/10 — stale jj workspace metadata is recoverable ('jj workspace forget' manually) but the behavior is silently incorrect.

title: AGENTS.md VCS detection uses 'test -d .jj' which fails in jj worktrees missing .jj/ in cwd
description: AGENTS.md uses 'test -d .jj && VCS=jj || VCS=git' for VCS detection. This is inconsistent with the vcs-detection-preamble.md reference, which explicitly notes that 'jj root' is the correct approach because '.jj/ may not be present in the working directory' in jj workspaces (the .jj/ directory lives at the repo root, not in each workspace subdirectory). In a jj workspace where the working directory does not contain .jj/ directly (e.g., when working in a subdirectory or in a workspace that shares the .jj/ from the root), 'test -d .jj' returns false, causing the script to fall back to git commands and potentially mutate git state in a jj repo. The vcs-detection-preamble.md is the authoritative reference and explicitly warns against relying on .jj/ presence for this reason. Fix: Change 'test -d .jj && VCS=jj || VCS=git' to 'if jj root >/dev/null 2>&1; then VCS=jj; elif git rev-parse --git-dir >/dev/null 2>&1; then VCS=git; else VCS=none; fi' consistent with the detection preamble.

title: worktree-remove.sh leaks temp file on success path due to trap - EXIT clearing rm -f trap
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, a temporary file is created at line 60 to capture detect_repo_root stderr: _root_err_file=$(mktemp). The EXIT trap is set at line 61 to clean it up: trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}"' EXIT. However, at line 150 the script executes trap - EXIT to disarm the trap before the script exits on the success path. This means on every successful removal the mktemp file is never deleted, leaking one temp file per hook invocation. On error paths (exit 1) the EXIT trap fires correctly before being cleared, so the leak only affects success paths. Fix: explicitly rm -f "$_root_err_file" before trap - EXIT at line 150, or restructure to not clear the trap.

title: git path: no test for cleanup_on_error rm-rf-ing pre-existing worktree directory
description:
worktree-create.sh's cleanup_on_error always calls rm -rf WORKTREE_PATH regardless of whether the directory pre-existed. If a worktree name collides with an existing directory, cleanup_on_error silently destroys it after git worktree add fails.

The jj path has a partial test ("colocated jj+git: fails gracefully when worktree path already exists", test_worktree_create.bats line 604) that pre-creates the path with a stale file, but only asserts status != 0. It does not verify whether the stale file was preserved or deleted.

There is no equivalent git-path test at all. In the git path: mkdir -p succeeds on the existing dir, git worktree add fails (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false), cleanup_on_error calls git worktree prune then rm -rf the directory — destroying pre-existing content without warning.

Suggested tests:
1. git path: pre-existing worktree directory with content causes git worktree add to fail; verify that cleanup_on_error removes the directory (document this is the defined behavior)
2. jj path: extend the preexist test to verify the stale file is gone after cleanup (or preserved — document the intended behavior)

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh line 74 (rm -rf WORKTREE_PATH in cleanup_on_error)
Test file: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats

title: Test asserts exit 0 for jj workspace forget failure but code exits 1
description: In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, the test 'jj path: handles workspace forget failure' (line 190) asserts [ "$status" -eq 0 ] when jj workspace forget fails. However, worktree-remove.sh line 145 explicitly exits 1 when jj_forget_failed=true: 'if $jj_forget_failed; then exit 1; fi'. The test and code are in direct contradiction. The test will fail at runtime (the script exits 1 but the test expects 0), meaning the test incorrectly documents successful exit for a workspace leak condition. Given the ERROR-level log message at line 126 and the jj_forget_failed variable, exit 1 is correct — workspace metadata may be leaked. Fix: change line 190 of test_worktree_remove.bats from [ "$status" -eq 0 ] to [ "$status" -eq 1 ].

title: worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error does not exit non-zero when jj workspace forget fails after successful create
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error(), when WORKSPACE_CREATED=true and jj workspace forget fails, the code sets CLEANUP_FAILED=true and exits with the original _exit_code only if it was 0. However, the CLEANUP_FAILED branch on line 2487 only sets CLEANUP_FAILED=true; it does NOT set _exit_code=1 unless the original exit code was already 0. The final block is: if [[ "$CLEANUP_FAILED" == "true" ]] && [[ $_exit_code -eq 0 ]]; then _exit_code=1; fi. So if the original error (that triggered cleanup) was itself non-zero (e.g., jj workspace add succeeded but a later step failed), CLEANUP_FAILED is true but _exit_code is already non-zero — so the exit code is preserved as-is, which is correct. But the error message emitted is 'ERROR: cleanup: .jj/ found but jj not installed — workspace was created and cannot be cleaned up' with no indication that the primary failure was something else. This is confusing for debugging. More critically, when jj IS installed but workspace forget fails (line 2481-2483), this is only a WARNING, not an ERROR, and CLEANUP_FAILED is never set — so the exit code from the primary failure is returned but the leaked workspace metadata is not signaled as a hard failure. Consider setting CLEANUP_FAILED=true when jj workspace forget fails after WORKSPACE_CREATED=true.

title: detect_repo_root merges jj root stdout+stderr, silently fails when jj emits warnings
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root(), jj root is invoked as: jj_out=$(jj root 2>&1). This merges stdout and stderr into jj_out. If jj emits any warning to stderr alongside the repo path on stdout, jj_out will contain e.g. '/repo/path\nWarning: deprecated config key', causing the -d "$jj_out" check to fail (directory lookup on a multi-line string always returns false). The function then falls through to the error-reporting block with jj_rc=0, producing the confusing message: 'jj root returned /repo/path... but directory does not exist'. The actual repository root is never returned. Fix: separate stdout and stderr capture — capture stdout only for the path (jj_out=$(jj root 2>/tmp/jj_err_$$)), and only include stderr in error messages on failure.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: jj checkout verification uses jj edit which mutates working copy unexpectedly
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 89, the jj checkout verification uses `jj edit <pr-bookmark>` instead of `jj new <pr-bookmark>`.

After `jj edit`, the working-copy commit (`@`) IS the PR bookmark — not a child of it. This causes two concrete problems:

1. **Phase 4 `@-` verification is wrong** (line 215): `jj log -r @-` checks the bookmark's *parent* commit, not the bookmark itself. The check says 'MUST show PR bookmark parent' which is semantically confused.

2. **Accidental mutation risk**: Any unintended file changes in the orchestrator workspace would directly amend the PR bookmark commit, since `@` is the bookmark.

Note: Fix-workers are NOT affected because they run in isolated worktrees — the originally stated risk that 'fix-worker commits would mutate the bookmark' is incorrect.

**Fix:** Replace `jj edit <pr-bookmark>` with `jj new <pr-bookmark>` at line 89. Then `@` is a safe empty child, `@-` correctly points to the PR bookmark, and the Phase 4 verification works as documented.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text:
**Refined analysis after deep-dive into jj source (via DeepWiki):**

The stated risk that 'fix-worker commits would mutate the bookmark directly' is overstated — fix-workers run in isolated worktrees and don't commit to the orchestrator's workspace.

**Confirmed risks from `jj edit` at line 89:**
1. **`@-` verification is semantically wrong** — After `jj edit <pr-bookmark>`, `@` IS the bookmark, so `@-` (Phase 4 line 215) checks the bookmark's *parent*, not the bookmark itself.
2. **Accidental mutation** — Any unintended file changes in the orchestrator workspace would directly amend the PR bookmark commit.
3. **jj best practice violation** — jj docs recommend `jj new` + `jj squash` over `jj edit` for most workflows.

**Recommended fix:** Replace `jj edit <pr-bookmark>` with `jj new <pr-bookmark>` at line 89. This makes `@` a safe empty child and `@-` correctly points to the PR bookmark for Phase 4 verification.

Severity remains P1/important but the fix-worker mutation concern should be removed from the description.

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md step 2 jj branch check uses path heuristic that may false-positive inside nested _worktrees dirs
description:
In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, the jj branch check for worktree-isolated agents uses:

  case "$_cwd" in
    *_worktrees/*)
      ;; # Good
    *)
      echo "STATUS: FAILED -- Operating in default workspace"
      exit 1
      ;;
  esac

The document acknowledges this limitation: 'It may false-positive if the repo is inside a directory whose name contains _worktrees'. However, the CLAUDE.md gotcha section explicitly warns: 'The repo directory name MUST NOT end in _worktrees' — this naming constraint exists precisely because of this check.

The issue is: the false-positive risk isn't just about the repo name ending in _worktrees. It also applies to any path component containing _worktrees/ — e.g., a developer who has their project at /home/user/my_worktrees/fzymgc-house-skills would have ALL agents incorrectly skip the worktree check and operate from main.

The false-NEGATIVE case (path check passes but agent is on main workspace) is more dangerous than false-positive (agent gets FAILED when it shouldn't). The current check is asymmetrically risky in the wrong direction for jj repos.

This is accepted as a trade-off per the document's own Known Limitation note, but the documentation should note that false-NEGATIVEs (pass when shouldn't) are possible for repos under paths containing _worktrees, not just the repo name.

title: VCS preamble bats tests missing for jj and git positive branches
description: test_worktree_helpers.bats line 1722 tests only the 'none' branch of the VCS detection preamble snippet. The 'jj' and 'git' branches have no bats coverage. All 12 pr-review agents and 3 skills use this exact snippet for VCS detection; if either positive branch is broken (e.g., jj root exits non-zero unexpectedly, or the elif is malformed), agents fall through to 'none' and hard-stop. Behavioral evals (B-JJ1, B-FW1, B-VCS2) cover agent behavior in jj repos but not the preamble snippet itself. Suggested tests: (1) preamble outputs 'jj' when jj root succeeds via mock; (2) preamble outputs 'git' when jj is absent and git rev-parse --git-dir succeeds; (3) preamble outputs 'jj' in a colocated repo where both jj and git would succeed. File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_helpers.bats (add after line 1729).

title: jujutsu SKILL.md missing jj edit in allowed-tools
description: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md allowed-tools section (lines 7-33) lists Bash(jj new) and Bash(jj new *) but does NOT include Bash(jj edit *). The jj-git-interop.md reference (line 74) explicitly documents 'jj edit <bookmark>' as the equivalent of 'git checkout <branch>' for switching to an existing bookmark. When the jujutsu skill activates and an agent needs to check out an existing PR bookmark (the primary use case for bookmark navigation), the jj edit command will be blocked by the allowed-tools restriction. By contrast, address-findings/SKILL.md correctly includes Bash(jj edit *) in its allowed-tools. Fix: add 'Bash(jj edit *)' to jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md allowed-tools alongside the existing jj new entries.

title: worktree-remove.sh logs ERROR for jj workspace forget failure but exits 0
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 124-126: when jj workspace forget fails, the script emits 'ERROR: jj workspace forget failed ...' to stderr but then falls through to rm -rf and exits 0. This is a misleading severity mismatch — the caller (Claude framework) sees a successful exit code despite a jj workspace metadata leak. Orphaned jj workspace registrations are not surfaced as a hook failure, so the user has no signal that manual cleanup is needed.

Two related issues:
1. The script should exit non-zero after the ERROR log so the framework surfaces the failure. The current code treats the leaked workspace as a non-blocking warning despite labeling it ERROR.
2. The directory removal proceeds even when workspace deregistration failed, which is correct behavior, but the exit code should still reflect the failure.

Suggested fix: After the error echo on line 125, add 'exit 1' (or set a flag and exit after rm -rf succeeds) so the hook exits non-zero, signaling to the framework that manual cleanup is required.

title: detect_repo_root discards jj stderr, losing failure detail in error message
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh line 58: jj_out=$(jj root 2>/dev/null) silently discards jj stderr. When jj root fails, it writes its error message to stderr (not stdout), so jj_out is always empty on failure. The conditional ${jj_out:+; jj: ...} in the error message at line 66 therefore never appends jj diagnostic text. Operators see 'git rev-parse and jj root both failed' with no detail about why jj failed (e.g. 'No jj repo found here; ...').

Fix: merge stderr into stdout so the error text is captured:
  jj_out=$(jj root 2>&1) || jj_rc=$?

title: No bats test exercises the VCS detection 'none' branch in fix-worker/verification-runner agents
description:
The vcs-detection-preamble.md documents a 'none' branch that fires when neither jj root nor git rev-parse succeed, instructing the agent to stop with STATUS: FAILED. This is safety-critical behavior: a silently-incorrect fix applied in an undetected VCS state could corrupt a repo.

The behavioral eval B-VCS-NONE1 (pr-review/evals/evals.json) addresses the orchestrator's response to this failure, but there is no bats-level shell test that exercises the preamble's 'none' branch directly. The eval is model-invoked and not automatable.

File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md (lines 27-28, the else/echo 'none' branch)

Suggested fix: add a bats test that sources the preamble's detection snippet with PATH stripped of both git and jj, and asserts the output is 'none'.

title: worktree-create.sh: jj workspace add captures combined stdout+stderr but success output may include non-error content
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, jj workspace add invocation (diff lines 2385-2393).

The jj workspace add call is:

  if \! jj_out=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace add "$WORKTREE_PATH" \
    --name "worktree-${NAME}" 2>&1); then
    if echo "$jj_out" | grep -qi 'unexpected argument.*--name|unrecognized.*--name|unknown.*--name'; then
      echo "ERROR: jj version too old — 'jj workspace add --name' not supported." >&2
    else
      echo "ERROR: jj workspace add failed: $(sanitize_for_output "${jj_out:0:500}")" >&2
    fi
    exit 1
  fi

The error detection pattern 'unexpected argument.*--name|unrecognized.*--name|unknown.*--name' uses shell pipe character '|' as alternation within the grep -qi pattern. However, 'grep -qi' uses extended regex by default on some systems (not others). On systems where grep is BRE (basic regex), the '|' character is a literal character, not alternation.

The fix: use 'grep -qiE' (extended regex) to ensure the '|' is treated as alternation across all POSIX systems. The current tests pass on Linux/macOS because bash scripts typically use GNU grep or macOS grep, both of which support -E. But the CI uses ubuntu-latest, and the test for this path ('jj path: rejects old jj without --name support') relies on this grep working correctly.

Looking at test_worktree_create.bats line 1070-1073:
  [[ "$output" == *"--name"* ]]
The test only checks that '--name' appears in output, not that the specific grep-based error message is triggered. The grep might fail silently (returning false due to BRE treating | as literal), causing the generic 'jj workspace add failed' error instead of the version-too-old message. The test would still pass because '--name' appears in both messages.

Suggested fix: Change 'grep -qi' to 'grep -qiE' for reliable extended regex alternation across platforms.

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md: git branch verification step missing path rules for worktree-isolated agents
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, Step 2 git path (diff line 7433-7434).

The preamble says for git verification in step 2:
  'git: Run pwd and git branch --show-current — verify you are on a worktree/* branch, NOT main'

This is prose instruction, not structured shell code. In contrast, the jj path has explicit shell code. The git path lacks:
1. Structured shell code showing the exact check
2. The 'exit 1' instruction when the check fails

More importantly, the old agent Environment sections (now replaced) had:
  '2. Verify you are NOT on main -- you should be on a worktree/* branch
   3. If anything looks wrong, STOP and report the error to the orchestrator'

The new preamble says 'If anything looks wrong, STOP and report STATUS: FAILED' only at Step 3 (after both VCS and location checks). For the git path, if git branch --show-current returns 'main', agents should fail immediately with STATUS: FAILED. The preamble's Step 3 catches this, but the prose-only git instruction in Step 2 is less clear than the jj path's explicit shell code.

Also notable: the preamble correctly handles the review-gate agent (no isolation) with the note 'worktree-isolated agents only — orchestrator skills running from the main repo root should skip this step'. The review-gate.md change in this PR correctly only adds an Environment section about receiving the VCS diff, not a worktree check, which is consistent.

Suggested fix: Add structured shell code for the git verification step parallel to the jj code, showing the exact commands and exit condition:
  _branch=$(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null) || { echo 'STATUS: FAILED -- git branch check failed'; exit 1; }
  case "$_branch" in
    worktree/*) ;; # Good
    *) echo "STATUS: FAILED -- On branch '$_branch', expected worktree/*"; exit 1 ;;
  esac

title: address-findings: jj bookmark set failure handling runs jj undo but does not verify undo result before re-queuing
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b for jj repos (lines 7924-7939 in diff), the conflict recovery sequence for bookmark set failure is:

  1. Run jj undo once
  2. If jj undo fails, STOP with escalation message
  3. Verify: jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1 -- parent should match pre-rebase state. If not, STOP.
  4. Clean up workspace (step 4). Mark FAILED, add bead comment, re-queue.

The verification step 3 checks that '@-' matches pre-rebase state. However, the verification uses '@-' (parent of working copy), not the specific change-id from before the rebase. If the rebase moved multiple commits, '@-' would only show the immediate parent, which may not be the pre-rebase PR bookmark tip. The comparison 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1' provides no expected value to compare against -- the instruction says 'parent should match pre-rebase state' but doesn't specify HOW to verify this match.

This means: after a failed bookmark set followed by jj undo, the orchestrator has no reliable way to confirm the undo succeeded. If undo partially succeeded (e.g. reverted the rebase but left the working copy in an unexpected state), the orchestrator would proceed to re-queue the finding -- potentially on a corrupted repo state.

Suggested improvement: Before Phase 4b begins, capture the pre-rebase state:
  _pre_rebase_tip=$(jj log -r <pr-bookmark> --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)')
Then after jj undo, verify:
  _post_undo_tip=$(jj log -r <pr-bookmark> --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)')
  [[ "$_pre_rebase_tip" == "$_post_undo_tip" ]] || STOP with error

This makes the verification concrete and deterministic.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md (Phase 4b Jj repos section)

title: worktree-remove.sh: _worktrees suffix check uses *_worktrees suffix but parent-name check is fragile with sibling naming
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, path inference fallback (diff lines 2571-2580).

When detect_repo_root fails, the script infers the repo root from the worktree path:

  _worktrees_dir=$(dirname "$WORKTREE_PATH")
  _inferred_root=$(dirname "$_worktrees_dir")
  _inferred_name=$(basename "$_worktrees_dir")
  if [[ "$_inferred_name" == *_worktrees ]]; then
    REPO_ROOT="${_inferred_root}/${_inferred_name%_worktrees}"
    _REPO_ROOT_INFERRED=true

The check '_inferred_name == *_worktrees' uses a glob pattern that matches any directory ending in _worktrees. This is correct for the sibling naming convention.

However, the inferred REPO_ROOT is constructed as:
  REPO_ROOT="${_inferred_root}/${_inferred_name%_worktrees}"

For example, if worktrees dir is '/code/myrepo_worktrees/', then:
  _inferred_name = 'myrepo_worktrees'
  _inferred_root = '/code'
  REPO_ROOT = '/code/myrepo'

This is correct.

The actual issue: The script then validates:
  validate_safe_name "$REPO_NAME" "repository directory name" || exit 1
  EXPECTED_PARENT="$(dirname "$REPO_ROOT")/${REPO_NAME}_worktrees"

But REPO_NAME could contain underscores. A repo named 'my_project_worktrees' (ending in _worktrees) would produce REPO_NAME='my_project_worktrees' after stripping the suffix, then EXPECTED_PARENT would be '.../my_project_worktrees_worktrees' — a non-existent path causing the realpath check to fail with 'inconsistent state'.

This edge case could only occur if someone names their repo directory 'something_worktrees', which would create ambiguity in the naming scheme. While unlikely, the pattern should be documented as a known limitation.

Suggested fix: Document in CLAUDE.md that repo directories MUST NOT end in '_worktrees' to avoid worktree path ambiguity.

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj workspace forget failure logged as WARNING but orphaned workspace is permanent data loss
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, when 'jj workspace forget' fails (line 2628-2629 in diff), the script emits a WARNING and continues to rm -rf the directory. The WARNING message helpfully includes a manual remediation command.

However, the script exits 0 (success) after emitting the WARNING. The framework calling this hook receives exit 0 and treats the removal as successful. Any higher-level orchestration that relies on 'worktree removed successfully' will proceed normally despite the workspace metadata being permanently orphaned in jj's op log.

The test at line 1770-1797 (test_worktree_remove.bats) confirms this behavior: '[ "$status" -eq 0 ]' and '[[ "$output" == *"jj workspace forget failed"* ]]' -- the test explicitly asserts exit 0 on workspace forget failure.

This means:
1. The user receives no indication that their jj workspace list is now polluted with an orphaned entry until they manually inspect it.
2. Future 'jj workspace list' calls will show a phantom workspace pointing to a deleted directory.
3. The jj workspace can degrade to a 'stale workspace' state requiring 'jj workspace update-stale' or manual forget.

The remediation command in the warning ('run: jj workspace forget worktree-... manually to clean up') is correct but agents won't see it in the framework response since only exit code is used by the orchestrator.

Suggested improvement: Either (1) exit 1 when workspace forget fails (framework can handle this as a partial success), or (2) add this to a structured error output that the orchestrator parses, or (3) at minimum escalate severity from WARNING to ERROR in the log message.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (line 2629 in diff)

title: address-findings SKILL.md: VCS inference fallback when fix-worker omits VCS field has unchecked failure path
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4 step 5 describes the fallback when fix-worker omits the VCS field (lines 7824-7843 in diff):

  0. Verify worktree path exists: test -d <worktree-path>
  1. Detect VCS in main repo: if jj root ...; echo jj; elif git rev-parse ...; echo git; else echo none; fi
  2. git: git -C <worktree-path> branch --show-current
  3. jj: cd <worktree-path> && jj log -r @- --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)'
  4. Log warning

The CRITICAL gap: Step 3 says 'If it fails, mark FAILED.' But step 2 for git ('git -C <worktree-path> branch --show-current') has NO such failure handling instruction. If git branch --show-current returns empty string (which it does on detached HEAD) or fails (worktree no longer registered), the VCS field inference silently produces an empty WORKTREE_BRANCH. The orchestrator would then pass an empty branch to git cherry-pick, which would fail with an opaque git error.

Additionally, when VCS is 'none' (both jj and git detection fail), the instruction says 'mark FAILED and skip integration. Clean up the worktree directory: rm -rf <worktree-path>'. This correctly cleans up the directory but says 'VCS deregistration is not possible'. This is a silent failure: if the worktree is a jj workspace that lost its jj binary, the jj workspace metadata is permanently orphaned. No guidance is given to alert the user about this permanent metadata corruption.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md (Phase 4, step 5 VCS inference block)

title: worktree-remove.sh: mktemp failure exits 1 but EXIT trap tries to rm nonexistent temp file
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the script registers an EXIT trap immediately after creating a temp file:

  _root_err_file=$(mktemp) || { echo '...' >&2; exit 1; }
  trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}"' EXIT

If mktemp succeeds, the trap correctly cleans up the temp file. However, there is a race condition: if an earlier error (before mktemp is called) caused by set -e fires the EXIT trap registered later in the script, the ${_root_err_file:-} expansion evaluates to empty string and 'rm -f' is called with no argument -- on some systems this is a no-op, on others it may print an error.

More critically: if mktemp fails, the 'exit 1' fires before the trap is registered, so no cleanup is needed -- this path is handled correctly. But the trap is registered AFTER the mktemp call, and between the start of the script and the mktemp line, set -e is active. If any earlier command (e.g. the jq call at line 2511 or the realpath/cd block at line 2532) raises an error via set -e, the EXIT trap (if it was already registered from a different trap statement) would fire. In this script there is only ONE trap registration (for the temp file), so this is less of an issue -- but the pattern is fragile.

More concretely: the 'trap - EXIT' pattern used in worktree-create.sh (to disarm the trap on success) is ABSENT from worktree-remove.sh. The temp file cleanup trap remains active for the entire script lifetime. This is fine for temp file cleanup, but means any future addition of another EXIT trap in worktree-remove.sh could interfere.

The primary issue: at line 2565, after the realpath/canonicalize block, various 'exit 1' paths can fire. Each of these paths correctly exits 1, but none of them produces a structured error to stdout (only stderr). The orchestrator framework may not distinguish between worktree-remove.sh exit 0 (success) and exit 1 (error). There is no documented contract about what exit code the WorktreeRemove hook should return -- if the framework ignores non-zero exit codes from worktree hooks, errors in removal are silently swallowed.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (lines 2564-2566 in diff)

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md: jj worktree location check uses pwd not pwd -P, susceptible to symlinks
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, Step 2 jj worktree check (diff line ~7425).

The vcs-detection-preamble.md instructs agents to verify they are in a worktree workspace using:

  _cwd=$(pwd -P 2>/dev/null) || _cwd=$(pwd)
  case "$_cwd" in
    *_worktrees/*)
      ;; # Good — operating in a worktree workspace
    *)
      echo "STATUS: FAILED -- Operating in default workspace"
      exit 1
      ;;
  esac

However, the worktree-remove.sh script (line 2552 in the diff) has this note about macOS:
'# macOS resolves symlinks in tmpdir paths (e.g. /var/... → /private/var/...)
# so we can't match WORKTREE_PATH exactly'

The same macOS symlink issue applies here. On macOS, the actual worktrees directory (/Volumes/Code/..._worktrees/) is not symlinked, so this is likely fine. But the pattern '_worktrees/*' requires the _worktrees component to appear anywhere in the path, which would also match paths like '/some/path/with_worktrees/fake'. This is a fuzzy match that could fail in edge cases.

More importantly: the preamble says 'Do NOT rely on jj workspace list output to identify the current workspace; jj 0.39+ does not emit a (current) marker.' This is correct guidance, but the pwd-based check is also fragile for jj workspaces that are accessed via symlinks or alternative mounts.

The git verification step ('Run pwd and git branch --show-current — verify you are on a worktree/* branch') remains in place but is not shown as structured shell code, which is inconsistent with the jj path that has explicit shell code. Agents following the preamble may interpret the git verification differently across implementations.

Suggested fix: Use a more precise path pattern check. Instead of '*_worktrees/*', use a check that verifies the immediate parent directory ends in '_worktrees': case "$(basename "$(dirname "$_cwd")")" in *_worktrees) ... esac. This is more precise but slightly more complex. Alternatively, document the known limitation with the fuzzy match pattern.

title: no BATS test for worktree-create git path: prune stale metadata on partial add (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false)
description:
worktree-create.sh lines 57-59 contain an else branch in cleanup_on_error for git repos: when WORKSPACE_CREATED is false (git worktree add partially started but did not complete), the script runs git worktree prune to clean up stale .git/worktrees/ metadata.

test_worktree_create.bats has a test 'git path: cleanup_on_error skips git worktree remove when WORKSPACE_CREATED is false' (line 589) that verifies 'git worktree remove' is NOT called, but it does not verify that 'git worktree prune' IS called in the failure path, nor does it test the warning path when prune itself fails.

This matters because a future refactor could inadvertently remove the prune call, leaving stale .git/worktrees/ entries, and no test would catch it.

Missing tests:
1. Git path: partial add (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false) calls git worktree prune
2. Git path: partial add where git worktree prune also fails emits the correct warning

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats
Relevant production code: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 56-59

title: worktree-create.sh: cleanup_on_error does not preserve exit code — may exit 0 after failure
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, cleanup_on_error is registered via 'trap cleanup_on_error EXIT'. The function does not capture or restore the original exit code. When the EXIT trap fires (because set -e terminated the script on error), bash executes cleanup_on_error, and if cleanup_on_error's last command succeeds (e.g. cleanup_empty_parent returns 0), the script exits 0 -- masking the original failure code.

The existing tests exercise 'exit 1' paths that are explicit (the main body calls 'exit 1' before the trap fires), so tests pass. But any path where set -e triggers the EXIT trap without an explicit 'exit 1' call (e.g. a future failure in a newly added command) would exit 0 silently.

Suggested fix: Capture the failure code at the top of cleanup_on_error:
  _exit_code=$?
  # ... cleanup ...
  exit $_exit_code

This is the standard pattern for EXIT trap handlers that must preserve failure signals.

title: jj-init: no test for read-only .gitignore append failure path
description:
test_jj_init.bats covers 4 scenarios for the gitignore fragment (no file, idempotent, existing without entry, partial match) but omits the error path where .gitignore exists but is not writable. jj-init.md step 4 explicitly calls for surfacing this error: 'Could not update .gitignore: <append_err>'. Without a test, a regression that silently swallows the error or exits non-zero instead of continuing would go undetected.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats
Fragment tested: GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT (lines 24-28)
Missing test: chmod a-w .gitignore before running the fragment, assert exit 0 and output contains 'Could not update .gitignore'.

Suggested fix: add a @test that makes .gitignore read-only, runs the fragment, and asserts the error message is surfaced while the script exits 0 (warning, not fatal).

title: CI: actions/checkout pinned to SHA with comment 'v6' — no such version exists
description:
File: .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, lines 14 and 56.

The workflow pins actions/checkout to SHA de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd with the comment '# v6'. However, actions/checkout does not have a v6 release (latest LTS is v4 as of 2026). If this SHA does not correspond to any published release tag, the comment is misleading and it is impossible to verify the integrity of the pinned action without looking up the SHA against the actions/checkout repository.

This is a supply chain security concern: a SHA that does not correspond to a known release tag could resolve to an unreviewed commit (a commit that was later force-pushed off a branch tip, for example). Best practice is to pin to a SHA AND verify it matches a tagged release.

Recommended fix: Update to actions/checkout v4 with its corresponding SHA, for example:
  uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683 # v4

Or at minimum, verify this SHA corresponds to a known release and update the version comment accordingly.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is invalid — actions/checkout v6 exists and SHA de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd matches the v6 tag exactly. Verified via gh api repos/actions/checkout/git/refs/tags/v6.

title: address-findings: VCS inference fallback references undefined worktree-path
description: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4 step 5 (lines 268-279): when VCS is missing from fix-worker response, step 0 says 'Verify worktree path exists: test -d <worktree-path>'. But <worktree-path> is not part of the fix-worker output contract (vcs-equivalence.md defines only STATUS, VCS, FILES_CHANGED, DESCRIPTION, WORKTREE_BRANCH, CHANGE_ID — no WORKTREE_PATH field). The orchestrator cannot reliably know the worktree path from the fix-worker response alone; it would need to reconstruct it from the worktree name (which isolation: worktree may or may not expose). If the orchestrator cannot determine the path, step 0's guard check is skipped and steps 2-3 proceed against an unknown/wrong path, potentially reading a branch or change-id from the wrong location. Fix: either (1) add WORKTREE_PATH to the fix-worker output contract, or (2) document that the path is always '<repo_worktrees_dir>/<worktree-name>' from the Task framework's worktree isolation metadata.

title: address-findings: jj conflict detection string comparison may silently pass on whitespace
description: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4b jj repos step 2 (lines 329-337): the conflict check is 'has_conflict=$(jj log -r <change-id> --no-graph -T "if(conflict, \"true\", \"false\")")'. The LLM agent then checks if this equals 'true'. However, 'jj log' output may include trailing newlines or whitespace, causing a naive string comparison ([[ "$has_conflict" == "true" ]]) to silently evaluate as false even when the commit has conflicts. If this check passes a conflicted commit, the skill proceeds to 'jj bookmark set <pr-bookmark> -r <change-id>', pointing the PR bookmark at a conflicted commit — silently corrupting the PR branch state. The template should use a numeric check (e.g., count conflicts) or strip whitespace explicitly. At minimum, the skill should document that the LLM must strip the output before comparing: 'has_conflict=$(... | tr -d "\n")'

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj workspace list failure gives no manual recovery instruction
description: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 118-119: when 'jj workspace list' fails, the warning says 'skipping workspace forget' but gives no manual recovery instruction. By contrast, the jj workspace forget failure at line 123 explicitly includes the manual recovery command. An operator reading the log cannot know what to run to deregister the stale workspace. Suggested fix: append to the warning: "(run: cd $(sanitize_for_output "$REPO_ROOT") && jj workspace forget worktree-$(sanitize_for_output "$WORKSPACE_NAME") to clean up)"

title: worktree-create.sh: jj workspace add --help probe may fail on old jj (exits non-zero for --help)
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, lines 84-91.

The probe 'jj workspace add --help' is used to verify jj supports the --name flag. In jj, 'jj <subcommand> --help' historically outputs help to stdout and exits 0 (standard clap behavior). However, some versions of jj and some CLI frameworks output --help to stderr, or exit with status 2 (usage error). If jj exits non-zero on 'jj workspace add --help', the code on line 84-86 reports ERROR: jj failed to run, masking the real issue (version too old) with a misleading message.

Additionally, this probe adds an extra jj invocation on every worktree creation, even on jj versions that do support --name. A more robust approach would be to attempt the workspace add directly and check for a specific error message if it fails, or to probe once and cache the result. The probe as written also documents a TOCTOU window (line 81-83) but the comment underestimates the real concern: if jj is upgraded between the --help probe and the actual workspace add, and the new version changes its argument parsing, the probe may succeed while the add uses different semantics.

The mock in test_worktree_create.bats line 95-98 (create_old_jj_mock) verifies the --help probe works when jj exits 0. There is no test for jj exiting non-zero on --help while still being a valid jj install.

title: worktree-create.sh: jj cleanup_on_error missing-REPO_ROOT branch untested for jj path
description: worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error lines 32-33 check '\! -d "$REPO_ROOT"' and skip all VCS cleanup with a WARNING. The existing test 'cleanup_on_error warns and skips VCS cleanup when REPO_ROOT is missing' (line 575 of test_worktree_create.bats) exercises this path via a git repo (no .jj/ in setup). However, the jj variant — where REPO_ROOT has .jj/ set up, workspace add begins, then REPO_ROOT is deleted before the post-add failure triggers cleanup — is not tested. The jj path cleanup_on_error has separate branching (.jj/ check at line 34 happens AFTER the REPO_ROOT existence check at line 32), so the missing-REPO_ROOT warning should fire regardless of whether .jj/ was present. A test should: setup_jj, use a working mock, patch 'trap - EXIT' to delete REPO_ROOT then exit false, verify the WARNING about REPO_ROOT missing appears (not any jj-specific warning), verify exit non-zero.

title: worktree-remove.sh: EXIT trap deletes temp file before error message is read
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 58-61, a temp file is created and an EXIT trap is set to clean it up. However, the script has 'set -euo pipefail' at the top, which means any error after line 59 will trigger the EXIT trap. The temp file stores detect_repo_root stderr, which is read at line 61 via 'cat $_root_err_file'. If the cat itself or anything between the trap registration and line 61 fails, the EXIT trap fires and deletes the temp file before the error path reads it. More importantly: when detect_repo_root fails and the script exits at line 71-73 (the _worktrees pattern check failure branch), the EXIT trap fires immediately after the exit call and deletes _root_err_file. Since _root_stderr was already captured at line 61 before those exit branches, this is not a data-loss bug in the current code. However, the trap-then-read pattern is subtle. A more defensive pattern would be to delete the temp file explicitly after reading it (line 61) rather than relying on an EXIT trap, making the lifecycle obvious. The real risk is that if a future change adds code that reads _root_err_file after any of the early exit paths, it will get a missing-file error rather than the error content.

title: jj cleanup_on_error: jj-not-in-PATH branch untested after successful workspace add
description: In worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error (lines 34-45), when .jj/ exists but jj is not installed at cleanup time, a WARNING is emitted and no workspace forget is attempted. The existing test 'jj path: rejects when jj not installed' covers the pre-workspace-add failure (jj never runs so WORKSPACE_CREATED=false). However, there is no test for the scenario where jj IS in PATH when create starts, jj workspace add succeeds (WORKSPACE_CREATED=true), and jj disappears from PATH before the post-add step that triggers cleanup via EXIT trap. This exercises the branch at line 36 of cleanup_on_error where 'command -v jj &>/dev/null' returns false inside cleanup for a .jj/ repo, after a successful workspace add. Test should: setup_jj, use a mock that reports --help support and succeeds on workspace add, patch 'trap - EXIT' with a PATH-clearing false, run the script; verify WARNING about jj not installed appears and the workspace directory is removed.

title: CI drift-check: error annotations lost due to stdout redirect to temp file
description:
In .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, the drift-check step uses a while loop with stdout redirected to a temp file: 'done <<< "$all_keys" >> "$configured_tmp"'. Any 'echo "::error::..."' calls inside the loop body are also redirected to the temp file rather than appearing in GitHub Actions output. The step still exits 1 (failing the check), but the diagnostic message (e.g. 'Unsafe character in release-please key: X') is silently written to the temp file and never shown in the action log. CI failures become opaque and hard to debug.

File: .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, inside the while loop in the drift-check job.

Fix: Change the error echo calls to write to stderr: echo "::error::Unsafe character in release-please key: $key" >&2 (and similarly for the '..' check). Stderr is not affected by the stdout redirect on the while loop.

title: address-findings: VCS inference fallback always resolves to jj in colocated repos, may use wrong integration method
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4 step 5, the VCS inference fallback for a missing VCS field uses:
  if jj root > /dev/null 2>&1; then echo jj; elif git rev-parse --git-dir > /dev/null 2>&1; then echo git; else echo none; fi

In a colocated repo (both .jj/ and .git/), jj root succeeds first, so the inferred VCS is always 'jj'. But the fix-worker agent runs its own VCS detection (vcs-detection-preamble.md step 1) which also puts jj first — so in a colocated repo, the fix-worker will always use jj and report CHANGE_ID.

However, if a fix-worker omits the VCS field but includes WORKTREE_BRANCH (a git identifier), the inference returns 'jj' and the orchestrator will attempt jj rebase integration with a branch name that is not a jj change ID. This produces a hard-to-diagnose failure.

The fallback at step 5 step 0 notes: 'If already removed, use WORKTREE_BRANCH or CHANGE_ID directly.' This partially mitigates the issue (use whichever identifier is present), but the VCS inference still overrides the identifier-based decision.

Suggested fix: in the fallback, prefer the present identifier to determine VCS rather than running a separate detection:
- If WORKTREE_BRANCH is present (and VCS missing), infer VCS=git
- If CHANGE_ID is present (and VCS missing), infer VCS=jj
- Only run the jj root / git rev-parse detection when neither identifier is present
close_reason: Invalid: in colocated repos, jj workspaces MUST be used (not git worktrees). The VCS inference checking jj first is correct behavior — jj workspace add creates the worktree, and jj rebase is the right integration method.

title: address-findings: orphaned jj workspace after conflicted rebase — no cleanup before re-queue
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4b jj section, step 2 handles a conflicted rebase: after jj undo reverts the rebase, the instruction is 'Mark FAILED, re-queue.' — but there is no workspace cleanup step. The worktree-forget + rm -rf cleanup is only in step 4, which is reached only for non-conflicted results. A conflicted rebase leaves the workspace registered in jj workspace list and the directory on disk, then re-queuing spawns a new fix-worker into a fresh worktree. The orphaned workspace accumulates across retries.

The git equivalent (step 3, conflict handling) also does not include explicit cleanup, but git cherry-pick --abort restores the working tree and git worktree remove is still needed. Both paths are missing post-failure cleanup.

Suggested fix: add a cleanup step to both the conflict branch and the bookmark-set-fail branch in jj, mirroring the structure in step 4:
  jj workspace forget worktree-<name>
  rm -rf ../<repo>_worktrees/<worktree-name>
before re-queuing. Similarly, add git worktree remove cleanup after cherry-pick --abort in the git conflict branch.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Review-gate FAIL (attempt 1): fix added 19 lines, pushing SKILL.md to 505 lines (limit: 500). Need to add cleanup instructions more concisely or trim elsewhere.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Review-gate FAIL (attempt 2): missed step 1 (rebase returns non-zero) — that path also leaves an orphaned workspace. Need cleanup in ALL 4 failure paths: git conflict, jj step 1 non-zero, jj step 2 conflict, jj step 3 bookmark fail.

title: worktree-create.sh cleanup: git worktree prune errors silently swallowed in partial-create path
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error() (line 57), the else branch for a partial git worktree add (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false) runs: git worktree prune 2>/dev/null || true — silently swallowing all errors and output. The equivalent path when WORKSPACE_CREATED=true (lines 51-53) captures stderr and logs a warning. This inconsistency means git worktree prune errors in the partial-create path are invisible.

Suggested fix (consistent with lines 51-53):
  if ! prune_err=$(git worktree prune 2>&1); then
    echo "WARNING: cleanup: git worktree prune failed: $(sanitize_for_output "${prune_err:0:500}")" >&2
  fi

title: cleanup_on_error skips git worktree prune when workspace add fails before WORKSPACE_CREATED=true
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, cleanup_on_error function (lines 31-60)

When git worktree add fails partway through execution, git may have written partial registration metadata to .git/worktrees/<name>/ before exiting non-zero. The cleanup_on_error function only calls git worktree remove/prune when WORKSPACE_CREATED=true (line 46). Since WORKSPACE_CREATED is only set after successful completion (line 99), a partial git worktree add failure leaves that stale metadata uncleaned.

The cleanup does unconditionally rm -rf WORKTREE_PATH (line 56), which removes the directory, but git worktree metadata in .git/worktrees/ is separate and survives. This stale entry causes subsequent git operations (git status, git worktree list) to emit warnings about missing worktree directories.

Suggested fix: In the git branch of cleanup_on_error, replace the elif condition with an else that also covers the WORKSPACE_CREATED=false case by always calling git worktree prune:

  elif [[ WORKSPACE_CREATED == true ]]; then
    git worktree remove --force (existing logic)
  else
    # Partial registration may exist — prune to clear stale metadata
    git worktree prune 2>/dev/null || true

The existing WORKSPACE_CREATED=true path is correct; the new else handles partial-registration cleanup.

title: cleanup_on_error REPO_ROOT-missing branch has no test in worktree-create.sh
description:
The cleanup_on_error function in .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh has a guard at lines 32-33 that skips VCS workspace cleanup when REPO_ROOT no longer exists as a directory. This path fires when the repo directory disappears between detect_repo_root succeeding and cleanup_on_error being triggered by the EXIT trap.

There is no test exercising this branch. The test suite covers: jj workspace forget on failure, git worktree remove on failure, rm-rf failures, and WORKSPACE_CREATED guard — but not the REPO_ROOT-missing guard. A future refactor of cleanup_on_error that accidentally removes or reorders this check would not be caught.

Suggested test: Create a temp dir, git init, run worktree-create.sh with a patched script that (1) removes REPO_ROOT after worktree add succeeds, (2) simulates failure (false) to trigger cleanup, and verify the WARNING 'REPO_ROOT ... missing — VCS workspace cleanup skipped' is emitted.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats
Related code: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 31-33

Criticality: 7/10 — the branch is a real defensive path; if removed silently, cleanup could attempt VCS ops against a non-existent REPO_ROOT.

title: jj workspace list grep uses substring match — wrong workspace may satisfy existence check
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, jj workspace cleanup block (new code in PR)

The check at:
  echo "$_jj_ws_list" | grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}"

uses a plain substring match. 'jj workspace list' output is one workspace per line, e.g.:
  default: abc123 (no description set)
  worktree-foo: xyz789 (no description set)

If WORKSPACE_NAME is 'foo', the grep searches for 'worktree-foo'. This also matches a line like 'worktree-foobar: ...' because 'worktree-foo' is a substring of 'worktree-foobar'.

Consequence: When workspace 'worktree-foo' has already been forgotten but 'worktree-foobar' still exists, the existence check incorrectly finds a match and proceeds to 'jj workspace forget worktree-foo'. The forget call will fail (workspace not registered), log a WARNING, and the rm -rf still runs — so the end result is correct but the WARNING is misleading. More critically, the reverse case: WORKSPACE_NAME 'foobar' would NOT match if only 'worktree-foo' is listed, so the forget is correctly skipped. The false-positive case is the problematic one.

Suggested fix: anchor the grep pattern to match the full workspace name field:
  echo "$_jj_ws_list" | grep -qE "^worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:"

This matches the workspace name at the start of the line followed by a colon (the delimiter in jj workspace list output format).

title: jj --no-pager flag does not exist — SKILL.md agent rule will cause command errors
description: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md, Agent Environment Rules section states: 'Always use --no-pager or pass --config ui.paginate=never if pager interferes'. The --no-pager flag does not exist in jj. It is a git flag. Running any jj command with --no-pager will produce: 'error: unexpected argument --no-pager found'. The correct approach for jj is only --config ui.paginate=never or setting the JJ_CONFIG environment variable. An agent following the SKILL.md instruction and appending --no-pager to jj commands will get errors on every jj invocation. Fix: remove '--no-pager' from the instruction and keep only '--config ui.paginate=never' as the pager-suppression mechanism.

title: Missing test: jj workspace list failure in worktree-remove.sh
description:
worktree-remove.sh has a three-branch guard at the jj workspace list/forget block: (1) jj workspace list fails entirely → skip forget with WARNING, (2) workspace name not found in list → skip with WARNING, (3) forget fails. Branch (1) — 'jj workspace list failed' — has no dedicated test in test_worktree_remove.bats. Branch (2) — 'workspace not found in jj workspace list — skipping forget' — is also untested.

Both branches produce distinct WARNING messages that protect against silent metadata corruption if jj workspace state is out of sync. Without tests, a future refactor that accidentally inverts the early-exit guards would go undetected and could leave stale workspace metadata.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (jj workspace list/forget block)
Tests file: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats

Suggested fixes:
- Add test 'jj path: warns and skips forget when jj workspace list fails' using a mock jj where workspace list exits 1; assert WARNING contains 'jj workspace list failed' and directory is still removed.
- Add test 'jj path: warns and skips forget when workspace not in list' using a mock jj where workspace list succeeds but omits the target workspace name; assert WARNING contains 'not found in jj workspace list'.

title: jj workspace forget in worktree-remove.sh called from wrong working directory
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 115, jj workspace forget is invoked as:
  jj_err=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace forget "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}" 2>&1)

When REPO_ROOT was inferred (not detected by detect_repo_root), the script sets _REPO_ROOT_INFERRED=true and verifies .jj/ exists there (lines 102-107). However when REPO_ROOT was properly detected, the cd "$REPO_ROOT" succeeds, but jj workspace forget may fail with a misleading message if the hook's CWD is a child directory of the repo.

More importantly: when the worktree hook runs, the CWD provided by the Claude Code framework may not be the repo root. The script does NOT verify REPO_ROOT equals the jj repo root for the workspace being removed — it infers workspace name solely from WORKSPACE_NAME (basename of the canonicalized path). If two different jj repos share the same _worktrees sibling directory naming pattern, the wrong repo's workspace could be targeted.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 111-117 and 62-75.

Suggested fix: After inferring or detecting REPO_ROOT, run 'jj workspace list' (or 'jj root') from REPO_ROOT to verify the workspace named worktree-WORKSPACE_NAME actually exists in that repo before attempting workspace forget. If it does not exist, skip with a warning rather than failing.

title: cleanup_on_error skips jj workspace forget on partial jj workspace add failure
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, WORKSPACE_CREATED is only set to true (line 86/93) AFTER the full jj workspace add or git worktree add succeeds. If jj workspace add partially completes (creates the workspace directory and registers partial jj metadata, then returns non-zero), the exit 1 on line 83 fires the EXIT trap with WORKSPACE_CREATED=false. The cleanup_on_error guard (line 35) skips jj workspace forget, so only rm -rf is run. This can leave orphaned jj operation log entries or workspace metadata at REPO_ROOT that jj workspace forget would normally clean up.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, lines 81-86 and 31-54.

The fundamental tension: WORKSPACE_CREATED=true is correct for full failures (prevents spurious jj errors when workspace was never created), but incorrect for partial jj workspace add failures (workspace partially registered in jj op log).

Suggested fix: Attempt jj workspace forget unconditionally on failure when the workspace directory exists at WORKTREE_PATH (regardless of WORKSPACE_CREATED flag), letting jj return a non-zero exit code silently if no workspace was registered. Alternatively, set WORKSPACE_CREATED=true earlier (before jj workspace add), and let jj workspace forget be a best-effort cleanup call that emits a warning on failure.

title: Interactive command safety evals (B4, B5) use only manual assertions — no automated verification
description: JJ-B4 (jj split interactive warning) and JJ-B5 (mutating git commands warning) are agent-safety-critical behaviors documented in SKILL.md, but both assertions are type 'manual'. This means no regression is ever automatically caught if someone edits SKILL.md to remove or soften these warnings. JJ-B4 tests that an agent warns about 'jj split' being interactive (which would hang a non-interactive agent). JJ-B5 tests that an agent warns against 'git rebase' in a jj repo (which would corrupt history). Both behaviors are explicitly described with specific warning language in SKILL.md ('not safe for agents', 'Do NOT use'). Suggested fix: Add 'contains' assertions for B4 (check: 'interactive' or 'not agent-safe') and B5 (check: 'should not' or 'Do NOT'). These are automatable since the SKILL.md specifies the exact guidance language to use.

title: Missing behavioral eval for jj-init failure propagation (step 3 error path)
description: jj/commands/jj-init.md step 3 explicitly states: 'If the command fails, report the full error output to the user and stop. Do NOT proceed to modify .gitignore or run verification steps.' This is a critical behavioral contract — silent continuation after a failed jj git init --colocate would corrupt the workflow. However, there is no JJ-B-INIT4 eval that verifies this failure path. The existing INIT evals (B-INIT1/2/3) only cover: already-initialized detection, success path, and no-git-repo pre-check. The jj git init --colocate failure scenario (e.g., filesystem error, unsupported jj version flag) is completely uneval'd. An automated 'contains' assertion checking for the error text being surfaced would catch regressions if jj-init.md is edited to swallow errors. Suggested fix: add JJ-B-INIT4 with context 'jj git init --colocate exits non-zero with error message' and a contains assertion verifying the error text appears in the output.

title: jujutsu SKILL.md Detection section uses 'test -d .jj' which fails in jj workspaces
description:
The jujutsu SKILL.md Detection section (lines 57-62) instructs agents to use:
  test -d .jj && echo 'jj repo detected'

This check FAILS in jj workspaces, where the workspace working directory does NOT contain .jj/ — only the main repo root does. When an agent (fix-worker, verification-runner, or any of the 9 review agents) runs inside a jj workspace, 'test -d .jj' returns false even though the repo IS a jj repo.

By contrast, pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md (the authoritative detection reference for agents) correctly uses 'jj root' which succeeds in any jj workspace. The preamble explicitly notes: 'jj root succeeds in any jj workspace (including workspaces where .jj/ is absent from the working directory).'

The result of this inconsistency: an agent in a jj workspace that consults the jujutsu skill's Detection section will be told to check 'test -d .jj', which fails, and may incorrectly conclude it is in a git repo (falling back to git commands in a jj workspace).

Suggested fix in jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md Detection section:
Change the detection command from:
  test -d .jj && echo 'jj repo detected'
To the command-based check that works in all contexts:
  if jj root >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo 'jj repo detected'; fi
And update the note to clarify this works in workspaces where .jj/ is not present.

File: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md, lines 57-62

title: validate_safe_name error message omits trailing-dot and dotdot restrictions
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh (line 22), the validate_safe_name function rejects four conditions:
1. Names with non-alphanumeric chars (except ._-)
2. Names with a leading dot
3. Names containing '..' anywhere
4. Names with a trailing dot

The error message only documents condition 2: 'alphanumeric, dots, hyphens, underscores only; no leading dot'

When a name like 'agent.' (trailing dot) or 'a..b' (dotdot) is rejected, the message says 'no leading dot' which is actively misleading — the name does not have a leading dot. A developer or operator debugging an unexpected rejection would be confused.

Suggested fix: Change error message to accurately document all restrictions:
'(alphanumeric, dots, hyphens, underscores only; no leading dot, trailing dot, or double-dot component)'

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, line 22

title: cleanup_on_error silently loses cd-failure context when REPO_ROOT is unavailable
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, line 38

In cleanup_on_error, the jj workspace forget call is:
  if ! jj_err=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace forget "worktree-${NAME}" 2>&1); then
    echo "WARNING: ... failed — run manually if needed: $(sanitize_for_output "${jj_err:0:200}")")" >&2

If 'cd "$REPO_ROOT"' fails (REPO_ROOT deleted, permissions changed, etc.), the subshell exits non-zero, jj_err is empty, and the WARNING fires with a blank diagnostic: 'WARNING: cleanup: jj workspace forget worktree-X failed — run manually if needed: '. The user cannot distinguish a cd failure from a jj failure, and the real cause is silently hidden.

Suggested fix: Pre-check the cd step separately before running jj, so a cd failure produces an explicit message:

  if [[ ! -d "$REPO_ROOT" ]]; then
    echo "WARNING: cleanup: REPO_ROOT '$REPO_ROOT' missing — jj workspace forget skipped" >&2
  elif ! jj_err=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace forget "worktree-${NAME}" 2>&1); then
    echo "WARNING: cleanup: jj workspace forget worktree-${NAME} failed — run manually if needed: $(sanitize_for_output "${jj_err:0:200}")" >&2
  fi

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Review gate FAIL (attempt 1): The REPO_ROOT missing-directory check is shadowed by the outer guard — if REPO_ROOT is absent, [[ -d REPO_ROOT/.jj ]] is already false and the new warning is never reached. Fix must move REPO_ROOT check above the .jj guard.

title: Missing test for sanitize_for_output in detect_repo_root error message (the jj injection fix)
description: The fix in fzymgc-house-skills-8ho.117 added sanitize_for_output to the jj_out interpolation in detect_repo_root's error message (worktree-helpers.sh line 62). However, test_worktree_helpers.bats has no test verifying that a jj binary returning control characters or ANSI escape sequences has those stripped from the error output. The three existing detect_repo_root tests (file path, nonexistent path, non-git/no-jj) all return clean strings. A test with a mock jj whose 'root' returns embedded control chars (e.g., printf 'path\x01with\x1Bcontrols') and asserting the ERROR output is free of those bytes would directly validate the security fix and catch regressions.

title: jujutsu SKILL.md: missing no-argument allowed-tool patterns for jj st, jj log, jj undo
description:
jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md has allowed-tools entries using glob patterns like 'Bash(jj st *)', but many jj commands are documented and instructed to be run without arguments in the skill body. In Claude Code, 'Bash(jj st *)' matches 'jj st --porcelain' but NOT bare 'jj st'. The missing no-argument forms prevent agents from running these commands when the skill is loaded.

Missing allowed-tool patterns (bare invocations documented in SKILL.md body):
- 'Bash(jj log)' — used as bare 'jj log' at lines 125, 286
- 'Bash(jj st)' — used at multiple lines
- 'Bash(jj undo)' — used as bare 'jj undo' at line 169
- 'Bash(jj new)' — used as bare 'jj new' at lines 115, 310
- 'Bash(jj squash)' — used as bare 'jj squash' at lines 146, 317
- 'Bash(jj absorb)' — used as bare 'jj absorb' at lines 156, 318
- 'Bash(jj restore)' — used as bare 'jj restore' at line 172
- 'Bash(jj root)' — needed for VCS detection

Compare: address-findings/SKILL.md correctly lists both 'Bash(jj st)' and 'Bash(jj st *)' separately. Fix: add the bare forms alongside the existing *-suffixed variants in the allowed-tools frontmatter of jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md.

title: mktemp failure causes silent exit in worktree-remove.sh
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the line '_root_err_file=$(mktemp)' is unchecked. If mktemp fails (e.g., /tmp is full, TMPDIR unwritable, or restrictive tmpfs permissions), the script exits immediately due to 'set -euo pipefail' with no error message. The user sees an unexplained non-zero exit and no indication that temp file creation failed rather than worktree removal.

Additionally, the _root_err_file is only cleaned up in the two explicit branches of the subsequent if/else block. Any set -e exit between mktemp and those branches leaves a stale temp file.

Suggested fix:
  _root_err_file=$(mktemp) || { echo 'ERROR: mktemp failed — cannot create temp file for error capture' >&2; exit 1; }
  trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}"' EXIT
  ...and remove the trap after the normal cleanup branches.

title: worktree-create.sh: cleanup_on_error does not exit non-zero
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, cleanup_on_error is registered as an EXIT trap. When it fires due to an error (set -e triggered), the function runs but does not explicitly exit with a non-zero status. The EXIT trap's exit status does not propagate the original error — the script exits with whatever cleanup_on_error exits with. Since cleanup_on_error has no 'exit 1' at the end, if the cleanup succeeds (rm -rf and cleanup_empty_parent both return 0), the overall script exit code will be 0 even though a VCS operation failed. The WorktreeCreate hook framework reads the exit code to determine success. Fix: add 'exit 1' at the end of cleanup_on_error, or capture and re-emit the original exit code. Note: tests pass because they check '[ "$status" -ne 0 ]', which would fail if cleanup_on_error exits 0 — but test coverage for this path should be verified carefully.

title: release-please-config: jj plugin-level key will not match drift-check skill expansion
description: In release-please-config.json, the 'jj' package key is a plugin-level entry (no '/skills/' in the path). The check-skills.yml drift-check job expands such plugin-level keys using 'find $key/skills -name SKILL.md' — so it tries 'find jj/skills -name SKILL.md' and produces 'jj/skills/jujutsu'. Meanwhile the 'actual' list is produced by 'find homelab/skills pr-review/skills jj/skills -name SKILL.md' which also produces 'jj/skills/jujutsu'. These match, so drift-check passes. However, the 'jj' release-please package key is intended to version the plugin root (jj/plugin.json), while 'jj/skills/jujutsu' versions the skill. The concern is that the drift-check expansion of 'jj' and 'jj/skills/jujutsu' produces two entries for jj/skills/jujutsu, causing a diff failure against the unique actual set. Verified: the sort -u on configured_tmp dedups, so it passes. This is not a crash bug but the design intent is ambiguous and the 'jj' key is YAGNI — it duplicates what 'jj/skills/jujutsu' already tracks. Consider removing the bare 'jj' package from release-please-config.json unless plugin-root versioning is intentionally decoupled from skill versioning.

title: vcs-detection-preamble: redirect order silently discards jj root output
description: In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, the VCS detection snippet on line 11 reads: 'if jj root 2>/dev/null >&2'. The redirect order is wrong: 2>/dev/null redirects stderr to /dev/null, then >&2 redirects stdout to (the now-null) stderr, which also silently discards stdout. The intent is to suppress all output and only check the exit code. The fix is to swap the order to >&2 2>/dev/null — or more idiomatically: 'if jj root >/dev/null 2>&1'. As written, the snippet correctly suppresses output (both fd1 and fd2 go to /dev/null), but only by accident; the intent expressed in comments does not match and any agent or developer trying to understand or adapt this snippet will be confused. The same issue exists in the git fallback line on line 12.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Comprehensive review of PR #31: New jj plugin with jujutsu skill, jj-init command, VCS detection preamble across all pr-review agents/skills, VCS-aware worktree hooks, vcs-equivalence reference, and behavioral evals. Aspects: code, tests, errors.

title: Bare jj undo and jj st not permitted by allowed-tools in address-findings
description:
address-findings/SKILL.md calls 'jj undo' (bare, no arguments) and 'jj st' (bare) in its skill body (lines 231, 367, 387), but the allowed-tools list only includes 'Bash(jj undo *)' and 'Bash(jj st *)'. In the Claude Code tool permission system, the '*' wildcard in 'Bash(cmd *)' requires at least one argument — bare invocations like 'jj undo' (no args) and 'jj st' (no args) will be blocked.

Evidence from the codebase: review-pr/SKILL.md lists 'Bash(git branch *)' AND separately 'Bash(git branch)' because the bare form requires its own entry. The same pattern is used for 'Bash(git status)' and 'Bash(git status *)'.

Affected file: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md (allowed-tools, lines 17 and 24)

Fix: Add separate 'Bash(jj undo)' and 'Bash(jj st)' entries alongside the existing 'Bash(jj undo *)' and 'Bash(jj st *)' entries. Same fix needed in pr-review/skills/respond-to-comments/SKILL.md for jj st.

title: Missing Bash(jj root) allowed-tool in address-findings and respond-to-comments breaks VCS detection
description:
Both address-findings/SKILL.md and respond-to-comments/SKILL.md reference vcs-detection-preamble.md, which instructs the agent to run bare 'jj root' to detect VCS. Neither skill has 'Bash(jj root)' or 'Bash(jj root *)' in their allowed-tools lists. Claude will be blocked when attempting to execute 'jj root' for VCS detection.

review-pr/SKILL.md correctly handles this by listing both 'Bash(jj root *)' and 'Bash(jj root)' (lines 32-33 of the PR diff), but neither was added to address-findings or respond-to-comments.

Affected files:
- pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md (allowed-tools section, lines ~12-35)
- pr-review/skills/respond-to-comments/SKILL.md (allowed-tools section, lines ~9-22)

Fix: Add 'Bash(jj root)' to the allowed-tools list in both files.

title: POSIX fallback on cd+pwd-P silently drops pwd error context
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 33 and 90.

The POSIX fallback construct is:
  _cd_err=$(cd "$_ORIG_PATH" 2>&1 && pwd -P) || { echo "...: ${_cd_err:0:200}" ... }

The '2>&1' redirect applies only to 'cd', not to 'pwd -P'. If 'cd' succeeds but 'pwd -P' fails (e.g. kernel returns ENOENT mid-execution), pwd's stderr goes to the outer shell's stderr (not captured) and '_cd_err' is set to empty string. The error handler then fires with '_cd_err' empty, producing a message like: 'ERROR: could not canonicalize path '/foo': ' — no diagnostic context.

The same pattern repeats on line 90 for the EXPECTED_PARENT canonicalization.

Suggested fix: apply the redirect to the entire group:
  _cd_err=$(cd "$_ORIG_PATH" && pwd -P) 2>/dev/null
  ... capture cd-specific errors separately if needed, or use:
  { cd "$_ORIG_PATH" && pwd -P; } 2>&1
  wrapped in a variable capture so both command's stderr is included.

title: jj path: no negative assertion that cleanup_on_error skips workspace forget when WORKSPACE_CREATED is false
description:
The git path has a dedicated guard test ('git path: cleanup_on_error skips git worktree remove when WORKSPACE_CREATED is false', test_worktree_create.bats:386) that verifies git worktree remove is NOT called when workspace add fails while WORKSPACE_CREATED is still false.

The jj path has no equivalent test. In 'jj path: cleans up worktree and parent on workspace add failure' (test_worktree_create.bats:123), create_failing_jj_mock causes jj workspace add to exit 1 before WORKSPACE_CREATED=true (worktree-create.sh:84) is reached. The guard at worktree-create.sh:35-36 should prevent jj workspace forget from being called. The test verifies the directory was removed but never asserts that jj workspace forget was NOT called.

A regression that removes the WORKSPACE_CREATED guard (or the .jj check inside it) would silently call 'jj workspace forget' for workspaces that were never created — corrupting jj workspace metadata on every early-exit failure (name validation, mkdir failure, jj install missing, old jj version, --help probe failure).

Suggested fix: Add a test using create_failing_logging_jj_mock (which logs forget calls to forget-called.log) with a scenario that keeps WORKSPACE_CREATED false (e.g., jj workspace add failure), then assert [ ! -f "${REPO_ROOT}/forget-called.log" ].

title: detect_repo_root stderr suppressed in worktree-remove.sh, discarding diagnostic context
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 56, detect_repo_root is called with 2>/dev/null:

  if ! REPO_ROOT=$(detect_repo_root 2>/dev/null); then

This discards the full ERROR message from detect_repo_root, which includes the jj error output (e.g. 'ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse and jj root both failed; jj: <details>)'). When the path inference fallback succeeds, the user only sees the WARNING about the inferred root but loses why both VCS tools failed. When inference fails too, there is zero context about why git/jj couldn't identify the repo root.

The suppression was likely added to prevent double-printing since detect_repo_root already writes to stderr -- but because this code path continues with a fallback, preserving the context in the warning would aid debugging.

Fix: Capture and re-emit stderr rather than discarding it:
  _root_err=$(detect_repo_root 2>&1 >/dev/null) ...

Or pass the detect_repo_root error through as additional WARNING context when the inference fallback fires.

title: jj/evals/evals.json: negative-trigger evals JJ-T5/JJ-T7/JJ-T8 use wrong type ('triggering' instead of 'negative')
description:
jj/evals/evals.json — Three evals that test non-triggering or command-invocation scenarios are incorrectly typed as 'triggering' rather than 'negative':
