title: Harden mktemp mock atomicity in test_worktree_remove.bats
description: Counter-based mktemp mock uses non-atomic read-modify-write. Low risk since BATS runs sequential. From gl4.38.

title: counter-based mktemp mock in test_worktree_remove.bats uses unprotected shared state
description: The 'WARNING when secondary mktemp fails for jj workspace list' test (test_worktree_remove.bats line 938) uses a counter-based mktemp mock that reads and writes a shared counter file without file locking. The mock script does: count=$(cat $counter_file); echo $((count+1)) > $counter_file. This is a non-atomic read-modify-write that is vulnerable to a TOCTOU race if the test were ever run in parallel. While BATS runs tests sequentially within a file by default, the pattern is fragile: if --jobs > 1 is added to bats invocation or if BATS parallel mode is enabled in CI, this test could produce flaky results. Additionally, the counter file is created outside the clean_bin temp dir and uses system mktemp, so if the test is interrupted before cleanup, it leaks a temp file in /tmp. Suggested fix: use a simpler approach — create two sequential executable scripts (mktemp1, mktemp2) that the mock dispatches by checking a marker file with an atomic mv operation, or use a directory-based counter (mkdir is atomic).

title: Dead variable git_prune_failed declared but never set in worktree-remove.sh
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, git_prune_failed is declared at line 125 (git_prune_failed=false) alongside git_remove_failed and jj_forget_failed. However, git_prune_failed is never set to true anywhere in the script and never read in any conditional. The actual post-removal prune is gated on git_remove_failed (line 183), which is correct. The dead git_prune_failed variable creates a false impression that prune failures are tracked and affect exit code behavior — they are only logged as WARNINGs. Fix: remove the git_prune_failed=false declaration, or if prune failure tracking was intended, add git_prune_failed=true in the prune failure branch at line 185 and use it in the exit code decision.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: duplicate of gl4.26 (same git_prune_failed dead variable finding)

title: VCS detection preamble lint test proximity check could silently pass on non-conforming agents
description: The lint test in .claude/hooks/tests/test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats uses an awk proximity check: both 'Follow ... procedure' and the preamble path must appear within 10 lines of each other. This check has two weaknesses: (1) If an agent file contains 'Follow ... procedure' elsewhere in the document more than 10 lines before the preamble reference, the awk script may latch to the wrong occurrence and fail; (2) An agent could technically pass by having both phrases near each other in a comment or non-instructional section, while still omitting the actual execution instruction. The test comment acknowledges this: 'this test is a textual presence check only'. The risk is acceptable for a lint test, but the check should be documented with an example of what a conforming and non-conforming agent looks like, to prevent future agents from satisfying the check superficially. The test also uses find with maxdepth 1 without -type f, which would process subdirectories if any were added to agents/.

title: POSIX fallback test for jj workspace removal missing 'git' symlink creates PATH without git but jj mock delegates to git
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, the test 'POSIX fallback: jj workspace removal when realpath absent from PATH' (lines ~3334-3384) builds a curated PATH without 'git'. The test comment says 'Also excludes git (not needed for pure-jj repos) so the jj path is taken.' However, the detect_repo_root function in worktree-helpers.sh calls 'git rev-parse --show-toplevel' first, then falls back to 'jj root'. Without git in PATH, the git call fails and the code falls through to the jj mock.

The jj mock itself uses '#!/bin/bash' and does NOT call git internally. However: worktree-remove.sh at line 118 calls 'git -C "$REPO_ROOT" rev-parse --git-dir' for the inferred-root corruption check when _REPO_ROOT_INFERRED is true. If git is not in PATH and the script reaches this check, it would fail silently (the &>/dev/null suppresses the error). In this test scenario, _REPO_ROOT_INFERRED is false (jj root succeeds), so the check at line 118 is never reached.

But the concern is: for the pure-jj scenario in production (JJ_ROOT has only .jj/ not .git/), the script could still encounter the git -C check on line 118 in edge cases. The test does not cover this path.

More critically: the clean_bin PATH does not include 'git', but worktree-remove.sh's git worktree prune call (line 184) would fail if reached in a scenario where _skip_vcs_cleanup=false and the git else-branch is taken. The jj mock covers the jj path so git prune is not reached here — the test is actually correct for what it tests. The concern is that removing 'git' from the PATH could mask test failures in other scenarios that accidentally reach the git path.

Suggestion: add 'git' back to the jj POSIX fallback test or add an explicit assertion that git is not called (similar to the worktree-create.sh test that logs git worktree remove calls).

Files: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats lines 3334-3384.

title: git_prune_failed variable declared but never set to true in worktree-remove.sh
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 125, git_prune_failed=false is declared and initialized, but it is never set to true anywhere in the script. The variable appears to be dead code from a prior design where git worktree prune failure was tracked separately from git_remove_failed. No test verifies this variable's behavior, nor is its absence from the conditional at lines 183-187 (which gates on git_remove_failed) tested. This creates a misleading dead code path. Recommended action: remove git_prune_failed from line 125 (and the comment at the top of test_worktree_remove.bats line 3 that references it being removed). Low risk since git prune failure is already handled via the WARNING message at line 185 without needing a separate exit code flag.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: duplicate of gl4.26 (same git_prune_failed dead variable finding)

title: mock jj workspace list uses filesystem scan rather than real jj state model
description: The standard mock jj binary in .claude/hooks/tests/helpers.bash derives its 'workspace list' output by scanning the ${REPO_NAME}_worktrees/ filesystem directory at runtime (helpers.bash lines 76-87). Real jj stores workspace state independently of filesystem contents — a workspace entry can exist in jj's repo state without its directory, or a directory can exist without a workspace registration. This means tests using create_mock_jj or create_logging_jj_mock never exercise the divergence scenario (workspace registered but dir absent, or dir present but workspace not registered). This is exactly the gap the production code's workspace list check is designed to handle (worktree-remove.sh lines 150-157). Tests that test non-listed workspaces (e.g. 'attempts forget when workspace not in list') use fully custom inline mocks with hardcoded list output, which correctly avoids this limitation. Suggested improvement: document in helpers.bash that all standard mocks implicitly model the 'filesystem state == workspace state' case, and add a comment noting that divergence scenarios must use custom inline mocks.

title: helpers.bash _NO_JJ_FILTERED_DIR temp dir leaked between tests in helpers load
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/helpers.bash lines 329-346, the _NO_JJ_FILTERED_DIR temp directory is created with mktemp -d but is never cleaned up. The file-level setup code runs at helpers.bash load time (not per-test), so this temp dir persists for the entire test suite run but is not registered for cleanup.

Since helpers.bash is sourced once per BATS test file (via 'load helpers'), the _NO_JJ_FILTERED_DIR dir leaks into /tmp after each test run. While the OS eventually cleans tmp, CI environments may accumulate these and the symlinked binaries inside them.

This is a test infrastructure issue, not a production code issue. Suggestion: register cleanup via 'trap "rm -rf $_NO_JJ_FILTERED_DIR" EXIT' after creation, or document that the leak is intentional for the process lifetime.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/helpers.bash, lines 329-346.

title: worktree-remove.sh: EXIT trap leaks temp files when detect_repo_root stderr capture mktemp succeeds but later error exits before trap reset
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the EXIT trap at line 71 handles cleanup of _root_err_file and _ws_list_err. The trap is set before detect_repo_root is called. If detect_repo_root succeeds but a later error causes early exit (e.g., validate_safe_name fails at line 91), the trap fires and correctly cleans up _root_err_file. However, at line 191 the explicit cleanup 'rm -f "$_root_err_file"' and then 'trap - EXIT' are both reached only on the happy path. On the happy path, the file is removed twice (once explicitly, once the trap would have fired but it's been disarmed). The double rm is harmless.

The actual issue: when the script errors at lines 97-109 (EXPECTED_PARENT canonicalization failure), the trap fires and tries to clean up _ws_list_err. At this point, if the error is in the jj branch (line 129+), _ws_list_err might be a file path or empty string. The guard '[[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err"' correctly handles both cases (empty string skips the rm, non-empty removes the file).

This is working correctly as designed — no actual bug. The suggestion is just that the 'rm -f "$_root_err_file"' at line 191 before 'trap - EXIT' is redundant since disarming the trap with 'trap - EXIT' means the trap handler won't run again. The explicit rm is still good practice for clarity. No code change needed.

title: POSIX fallback test symlinks tools into clean_bin but uses bash from PATH, risking bash finding wrong tools
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, the 'POSIX fallback: removes worktree when realpath absent from PATH' test (line ~3302) builds a curated PATH using symlinks for a specific set of tools including 'bash'. However, the test invokes 'bash' to run the script under the curated PATH via 'PATH="$clean_bin" run bash -c ...'. This means the outer bash (from BATS) is used to interpret the shell, but the inner script's PATH is curated. Since worktree-remove.sh uses POSIX commands resolved via PATH, this is correct in principle.

However, the tool list at line 3308 includes 'bash' as one of the symlinked tools, which means it symlinks the system bash. But the PATH="$clean_bin" assignment affects the running script's PATH, not the outer bash invocation. This is fine, but the 'bash' symlink in clean_bin is then unused overhead in the curated PATH (it's only needed if the script itself calls 'bash').

The real concern: for the jj POSIX fallback test (line ~3334), the tool list does NOT include 'git' but the jj mock uses 'bash' shebang. If the curated PATH's bash is not found via PATH when the mock jj script's shebang resolves, the test could fail on systems where /bin/bash doesn't exist. This is a test robustness issue, not a production code issue. The mock script uses hardcoded '#!/bin/bash' which bypasses PATH lookup, so it is actually fine.

Severity: suggestion — the overhead is harmless and tests pass. But review should note the bash symlink in clean_bin is unused.

title: VCS detection preamble: jj worktree check relies on parent-dir naming convention that can fail in edge cases
description:
In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, the jj worktree verification step checks whether the current working directory's parent ends with '_worktrees'. This is documented as a known limitation with a false-negative risk — if the hook naming convention changes, agents incorrectly think they are in the default workspace and fail with STATUS: FAILED even when actually in a valid worktree.

The preamble acknowledges this trade-off, so this is informational. However, the 'git' branch has a stronger guarantee (checks actual branch name starts with 'worktree/'). The asymmetry between jj and git verification rigor means jj-repo agents have a weaker startup guard that could fire false-negatives after hook renames.

Suggested improvement: Document that the jj worktree check is heuristic-only and that the consequence of a false-negative is an unnecessary STATUS: FAILED, not data corruption.

title: worktree-remove.sh: git_prune_failed variable declared but never read
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 125, git_prune_failed=false is declared but never written to or read from in any subsequent logic. The variable git_prune_failed has no effect — prune is controlled only via git_remove_failed. This is dead code that adds confusion.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 125:
  git_prune_failed=false

The only prune call at line 184 does not set git_prune_failed, and git_prune_failed is never tested in any conditional. This variable should be removed, or the code should be audited to confirm prune-failure is intentionally non-fatal (which is documented in the exit comment, so removal is the right fix).

title: PATH restriction in BATS tests breaks on macOS where jq is not at /usr/bin/jq
description: Several tests in .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats and test_worktree_remove.bats set PATH="/usr/bin:/bin" to exclude jj from the path (testing the jj-not-installed code path). On ubuntu-latest CI this works because jq lives at /usr/bin/jq. On macOS (dev machines, or future macOS CI), jq is at /opt/homebrew/bin/jq or /usr/local/bin/jq, so the hooks would fail for the wrong reason (jq not found, not jj not found), causing false test failures or masking the real assertion. Fix: either capture the jq path before restricting PATH and prepend it (e.g., JQ_PATH=$(which jq); PATH="$JQ_PATH_DIR:/usr/bin:/bin"), or mock jj absence differently (e.g., by creating a jj mock that returns non-zero for all commands rather than removing it from PATH entirely).

title: .beads/ init and AGENTS.md are out of scope for the jj VCS support PR
description: The PR includes 11 .beads/ files (config.yaml, metadata.json, interactions.jsonl, README.md, .gitignore, and 5 hook scripts) plus AGENTS.md (91 lines of beads workflow boilerplate). None of these are required by the jj VCS feature: they are beads issue-tracking infrastructure initialization unrelated to Jujutsu VCS support. This violates YAGNI and obscures the review surface for the actual feature. These changes should be split into a separate PR or committed independently. AGENTS.md in particular adds agent lifecycle rules that apply repo-wide, not specifically to jj support.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: won't-fix — .beads/ init and AGENTS.md are already committed. Splitting would require history rewriting.

title: worktree-remove.sh: git_prune_failed set but never checked for exit-code influence
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the variable git_prune_failed is set to true (line 185) when git worktree prune fails after a failed git worktree remove, but it is never used anywhere — neither as an exit-code influencer nor in any subsequent conditional. The comment at lines 197–198 explicitly says git prune failure exits 0 (best-effort), which is a reasonable policy, but the variable is dead code. The WARNING stderr message is the intended signal. Suggested fix: remove git_prune_failed=true since the policy is documented in the comment and the WARNING is the actual surfacing mechanism. This is cosmetic but could mislead future readers into thinking the variable does something.

title: pr-review evals missing behavioral test for colocated jj+git agent VCS path selection
description:
The pr-review evals include B-VCS-GIT1 (git-only repo path — agent reports FAILED when not on worktree/* branch) and B-JJ1/B-JJ2 (jj repo paths). However there is no behavioral eval testing the colocated jj+git scenario for pr-review agents — i.e., when both .jj/ and .git/ are present, the agent must choose jj commands over git commands.

B-VCS-GIT1 tests the git-only path, and B-JJ1 et al. test pure-jj. The critical third case — colocated repo where jj takes precedence — has no eval. The CLAUDE.md states 'Never use mutating git commands when jj is present' and the VCS detection preamble codifies this, but there is no automated eval verifying pr-review agents respect it in the colocated scenario.

This is lower priority since the jj skill evals (JJ-B5) do test this for the jujutsu skill, and BATS verifies the text reference exists. The gap is specifically in pr-review agent behavioral coverage for the colocated case.

Suggested eval: pr-review B-VCS-COLO1 — agent in colocated jj+git repo uses jj commands (not git commit/worktree) for VCS operations.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: won't-fix — existing VCS detection tests sufficient. Evals can be added independently.

title: Behavioral evals for VCS detection preamble in pr-review agents not present in evals.json
description:
The pr-review evals.json is updated in this PR. The BATS test test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats verifies that all pr-review agents contain the text reference to vcs-detection-preamble.md (a structural/lint check). However, there is no behavioral eval verifying that a pr-review agent actually executes the VCS detection preamble logic correctly at runtime — i.e., that when both .jj/ and .git/ exist it chooses jj commands, and when only .git/ it uses git.

The jj evals.json contains JJ-B1 through JJ-B9 for the jujutsu skill. The comment in test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats mentions 'Runtime invocation and correct branching behavior are covered by behavioral evals (B-FW1, B-VCS-GIT1)' — but inspecting pr-review/evals/evals.json, B-FW1 and B-VCS-GIT1 should be present. Worth verifying these evals actually exist and adequately test the colocated-repo jj-over-git branching behavior in agents.

File: pr-review/evals/evals.json — confirm B-FW1 and B-VCS-GIT1 are present and cover the colocated jj+git agent branching.

title: No BATS test for worktree-remove.sh exit code 0 when git_prune_failed=true (behavior documented in comment only)
description:
worktree-remove.sh has an intentional design: git worktree prune failure leaves git_prune_failed=true but the script still exits 0 (comment: 'git_prune_failed is metadata-only'). The test at test_worktree_remove.bats line 51 verifies the exit 0 case when both git worktree remove AND prune fail, and the inline comment 'Exit 0: directory removed; git_prune_failed=true is metadata-only (mxm.1)' documents the intent.

However there is no test asserting that git_prune_failed alone does NOT cause a non-zero exit. The existing test verifies exit 0 indirectly (both failures happen together), but does not isolate the specific code path where git_remove_failed=false and git_prune_failed=true. This path is unreachable in practice (prune is only called when git_remove_failed=true) — which means the git_prune_failed variable is dead code. The test coverage gap is that the test title implies 'both fail' but the production code only calls prune after remove fails, so a prune-only-fails scenario cannot be triggered by the current implementation. A test or comment making this constraint explicit would prevent future maintainers from introducing the dead branch.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, test 'git path: continues to rm-rf when both git worktree remove and prune fail'.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Fixed: updated test comment to reflect git_prune_failed removal (gl4.8) and document unreachable code path.

title: jj-not-installed test uses exact string match for INFO message, brittle
description:
In test_worktree_create.bats line 134, the test asserts on the exact INFO message string:
  [[ "$output" == *"INFO: cleanup: .jj/ found but jj not installed — no workspace was registered, no cleanup needed"* ]]
This is testing implementation detail (exact message wording) rather than behavior. If the message is rephrased (e.g., for clarity), the test fails without any functional regression. The assertion should instead check for the key observable facts: exit code 1 + 'INFO' level (not ERROR/WARNING) + 'no workspace' and 'no cleanup' semantics.
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, test 'jj path: rejects when jj not installed'.

title: worktree-create.sh: WARNING on jj-not-installed before WORKSPACE_CREATED should be WARNING not suppressed
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, when jj is detected but not installed (lines 88-91), the script exits with ERROR. The EXIT trap then fires cleanup_on_error with WORKSPACE_CREATED=false. In cleanup_on_error lines 54-57, the WORKSPACE_CREATED=false + jj-not-installed branch emits: 'WARNING: cleanup: .jj/ found but jj not installed — workspace metadata not cleaned; run jj workspace forget worktree-NAME manually'. The phrase 'workspace metadata not cleaned; run jj workspace forget ... manually' is factually incorrect when WORKSPACE_CREATED=false — no workspace was registered, so there is nothing to forget. A user reading this output after the main 'ERROR: .jj/ directory found but jj is not installed' could incorrectly believe they need to manually run workspace forget. The test (test_worktree_create.bats line 972) asserts this confusing double-message. Fix: in cleanup_on_error, when WORKSPACE_CREATED=false and jj is not installed, do not emit the workspace forget advice (the outer error at line 89 already explains the issue).

title: JJ-T7 eval has contradictory type=triggering with expected_skill=null
description: In jj/evals/evals.json, eval JJ-T7 has type='triggering' but expected_skill=null. The 'triggering' type semantically means the skill should activate, but this eval tests slash command invocation (/jj-init), not skill triggering. This is a semantic inconsistency — evals with type='triggering' and expected_skill=null are contradictory per the schema semantics (triggering type implies skill activation is expected). The eval should use type='negative' (which tests that a skill does NOT trigger) since expected_skill is null, or the eval intent (testing command_invoked assertion) may warrant a new type. As-is, this could confuse eval harnesses that branch on type='triggering' to expect skill activation. File: jj/evals/evals.json, eval id JJ-T7.

title: jj-init.md verification step treats init failure as advisory rather than error
description: In jj/commands/jj-init.md, Step 5 (Verify) runs 'jj st' and 'jj log --no-graph -n 3' after init. The instruction says: 'If jj st or jj log fails, report the error output and advise the user to check jj log manually — the init may have partially succeeded.' This is an inappropriately soft response to a verification failure on a command that just ran. If 'jj st' fails immediately after 'jj git init --colocate', this indicates a broken init, not a minor advisory. The instruction uses 'may have partially succeeded' language that could lead agents to report success or near-success when the init actually failed. Suggested fix: if jj st fails in Step 5, report: 'WARNING: jj git init --colocate may have failed — jj status check returned an error: <error>. Run jj status manually to verify.' and surface this as a potential failure, not merely an advisory.

title: jj workspace add old-version error loses original error output in user message
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, when jj workspace add fails with a '--name not supported' error pattern (old jj version detection), the error handler emits: 'ERROR: jj version too old — jj workspace add --name not supported. Update jj.' This message discards the full jj_out content (which contains the original jj error text). If the version detection pattern has a false positive (e.g., a future jj error message that mentions --name for a different reason), the user receives a misleading 'update jj' message instead of the actual error. Suggested fix: include the original jj_out in the error message as context, e.g. 'ERROR: jj version too old (jj workspace add --name not supported; jj output: <jj_out:0:200>). Update jj.'

title: detect_repo_root silently degrades to failed root detection when mktemp fails
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root() has a mktemp failure path that sets jj_attempted=false and mktemp_failed=true, then skips the jj root call entirely. The function then returns 1 with message 'git rev-parse failed; mktemp failed so jj root check was skipped'. In low-resource environments where mktemp fails, a valid pure-jj repo would be reported as 'not inside a git/jj repository' — worktree-create.sh exits 1 with a misleading error. The mktemp failure is a resource/environment issue but the user sees a VCS detection failure with no indication that mktemp is the root cause. The WARNING about mktemp failure is emitted but the ERROR about no VCS follows immediately and conflates the two issues. Suggested fix: emit a more specific error that clearly separates 'mktemp failed' from 'no VCS' to help diagnose resource exhaustion vs. actual VCS absence.

title: worktree-remove.sh: no test for rm -rf failure when jj workspace forget also failed (both-fail path, exit code behavior)
description: worktree-remove.sh has two failure modes that can compound: jj workspace forget fails AND rm -rf fails. The existing tests cover each failure in isolation: 'jj path: reports error when rm -rf fails' (line 542) tests rm failure, 'jj path: handles workspace forget failure' (line 176) tests forget failure. But there is no test combining both failures. In the combined case, the script emits 'ERROR: jj workspace forget failed' then attempts rm -rf and emits 'ERROR: failed to remove worktree directory', then exits 1 (from jj_forget_failed). The exit code logic is: if rm -rf fails, it exits 1 immediately (line ~235 'exit 1'). So the jj_forget_failed exit at the bottom is never reached when rm fails. This means both failure paths exit 1 but for different reasons — a test covering the combined case would verify the correct exit code and that both error messages appear. File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats

title: No test for worktree-create.sh when jj workspace add succeeds but lefthook install runs in newly created jj workspace with wrong CWD
description: test_worktree_create.bats line 640 ('jj path: runs lefthook install after jj workspace add') tests that lefthook runs after jj workspace add and the marker file lands in the worktree. However, the lefthook mock writes to './lefthook-marker' (relative CWD). The production script runs 'cd $WORKTREE_PATH && lefthook install'. The test verifies the marker is at ${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees/jj-hook-test/lefthook-marker — this correctly validates the CWD is the worktree. This test is adequate. However, there is no test for the case where the jj workspace add creates the directory but lefthook install fails specifically because the jj workspace directory is missing expected git/jj state (e.g., the directory exists as a plain dir from mock but jj workspace metadata is absent). This is a minor gap: lefthook install failure is tested (line 620) but only for the git path scenario, not specifically for jj workspaces. The jj+lefthook failure case (line 640 variant) should have a corresponding failure test. File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats

title: No end-to-end test for worktree-create.sh + worktree-remove.sh with real git repo lifecycle (only lifecycle test uses jj mocks)
description: test_worktree_create.bats line 974 has a 'jj path: create-then-remove lifecycle' test that exercises the full create+remove cycle — but only for the jj mock path. There is no equivalent git lifecycle test that exercises both scripts in sequence using a real git repo: worktree-create.sh creates a git worktree, then worktree-remove.sh removes it. The individual git tests verify each script in isolation, but the integration between them (e.g., that the branch name format produced by worktree-create.sh is correctly consumed by worktree-remove.sh's git worktree remove) is only covered by the individual tests. A regression where create uses 'worktree/NAME' branch format but remove expects 'worktrees/NAME' would not be caught by the existing test suite structure. Recommend adding a git-path lifecycle test parallel to the jj one at test_worktree_create.bats:974. Files: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats

title: jj-init.md verification fragment tests: jj st stderr capture not verified in combined-failure case
description: test_jj_init.bats tests the VERIFICATION_FRAGMENT (jj-init.md step 5) in three cases: both succeed, jj st fails, jj log fails. Missing: test for when BOTH jj st AND jj log fail. In the fragment, jj_err is set from jj st output and then extended with log_err when jj log also fails. The concatenation logic (${jj_err:+$jj_err; }${log_err}) is only exercised by a test where both commands fail simultaneously. Without this test, a regression in the error concatenation path (e.g., losing one error message) would not be caught. File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats — should add a test after line 140 with a mock jj where both 'st' and 'log' exit non-zero, then assert both error strings appear in output.

title: No behavioral eval for VCS detection preamble runtime branching in pr-review agents
description: RETRACTED: B-FW1 and B-VCS-GIT1 evals in pr-review/evals/evals.json adequately cover the runtime VCS detection preamble branching for the critical agents (fix-worker, address-findings). The lint test comment accurately references these evals. Coverage is sufficient.

title: No test for worktree-remove.sh exit code when directory rm -rf succeeds but jj workspace forget never ran (jj not installed, inferred root path)
description: worktree-remove.sh test 'inferred root with .jj/ but jj not installed' (test_worktree_remove.bats:321) only checks for generic 'WARNING' and 'jj not installed' strings. It does not verify the exact exit code path through the code. The test mixes two behaviors: (1) inferred root, and (2) jj not installed. Without checking specific WARNING message text, the test could pass trivially even if the wrong warning branch fires. Suggestion: add assertion on the specific message text to distinguish which warning branch was taken.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Comprehensive review of PR #31: new jj plugin, VCS detection preamble, worktree hooks, evals. Aspects: code, tests, errors.

title: JJ-B9 'mentions-no-vcs' automated assertion matches any response — check: 'no' is too weak
description:
jj/evals/evals.json, eval JJ-B9, assertion 'mentions-no-vcs'.

The turn-1 fix for h6b.6 added an automated assertion to JJ-B9:
  name: mentions-no-vcs
  type: contains
  check: 'no'

The string 'no' is a substring of countless English words (not, know, no VCS, notion, etc.) and will match virtually any model response. This assertion cannot distinguish a correct response from a hallucinated one — it provides essentially zero regression protection.

Context: JJ-B9 tests that the skill, when the directory has neither .jj/ nor .git/, reports no VCS detected and avoids running jj commands. A more specific check like 'no VCS' or 'not a' or 'VCS detected' would actually catch a regression where the model incorrectly claims it is in a jj repo.

Fix: Change check from 'no' to a more specific string such as 'no VCS' or 'not detected'. The not_contains check for 'jj st' is good and should stay.

title: Unquoted ${real_git} in worktree-remove.bats mock git heredoc
test_worktree_remove.bats: stale comment claims exit 0 for jj-not-installed path but test asserts exit 1
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, line 2552 (diff line 2552)

The comment immediately before the 'jj path: warns and exits 1 when jj not installed' test reads:

  # jj_forget_failed=true from jj-not-installed → exit 0 (metadata-only failure)

But the test assertion on line 2556 checks:

  [ "$status" -eq 1 ]

The comment is a leftover from before h6b.1 was fixed (when the script incorrectly exited 0 on jj-not-installed). The fix correctly changed to exit 1, but the comment was not updated and now contradicts the test it annotates.

This is misleading to future readers: someone debugging why the script exits 1 here will see the comment claiming exit 0 is expected and be confused.

Fix: Update the comment to:
  # jj_forget_failed=true from jj-not-installed → exit 1 (metadata cannot be cleaned up)

title: address-findings Phase 1 Step 3 uses 'test -d .jj' inconsistent with vcs-detection-preamble 'jj root' approach
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 Step 3, the PR-checkout code uses 'if test -d .jj; then' to detect jj. However, the vcs-detection-preamble.md defines the canonical detection as 'jj root >/dev/null 2>&1', which works in ANY jj workspace—including workspaces where .jj/ is absent from the current working directory (only the main workspace has .jj/ at the repo root).

If the orchestrator skill is running from a non-default jj workspace (a _worktrees/ sibling directory), 'test -d .jj' returns false even though jj is active, causing the skill to incorrectly take the 'else' git path and run 'gh pr checkout' in a jj repo—violating the MUST NOT use mutating git commands rule from CLAUDE.md.

Fix: replace 'if test -d .jj; then' with the canonical preamble detection:
  USE_VCS=$(if jj root >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo jj; elif git rev-parse --git-dir >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo git; else echo none; fi)
  if [[ "$USE_VCS" == "jj" ]]; then ... else ... fi

The VCS Detection section at the top of address-findings already says to follow vcs-detection-preamble.md; the Step 3 code block should be consistent with it.

title: Misleading test comment claims exit 0 but implementation exits 1 for jj-not-installed case
description: In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, the comment above the 'jj path: warns and exits 1 when jj not installed' test reads: 'jj_forget_failed=true from jj-not-installed → exit 0 (metadata-only failure)'. The test name says 'exits 1', the assertion is [ "$status" -eq 1 ], and worktree-remove.sh correctly exits 1 when jj_forget_failed=true. The comment is stale from when jj-not-installed was treated as non-fatal. It now contradicts both the code and the test. Fix: update the comment to 'jj_forget_failed=true from jj-not-installed → exit 1 (leaked workspace metadata)'.

description: Change exit behavior: exit 1 when jj_forget_failed=true instead of exit 0. Remove the comment justifying exit 0.

title: [code] fix-worker.md missing 'jj st' allowed-tool needed for VCS detection preamble step 1
description: The fix-worker agent (pr-review/agents/fix-worker.md) references vcs-detection-preamble.md which uses 'jj root' for VCS detection. The fix-worker's tools list includes 'Bash' broadly but is restricted by agent tool permissions. The VCS detection preamble also runs 'jj st' after changes. However, the fix-worker's allowed tools are inherited from its tools field which lists only 'Read, Edit, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash' — meaning ALL Bash commands are allowed for agents (unlike skills which have restricted Bash patterns). This is not a bug per se, but it is worth noting that the VCS detection preamble step 1 also verifies 'jj st' in verification-runner.md, and those agents have broader Bash access. For agents (unlike skills), the Bash tool is unrestricted, so no fix is needed. LOW PRIORITY — informational only. File: pr-review/agents/fix-worker.md

title: [code] address-findings SKILL.md jj checkout step uses @- for verification but worktree agents inherit from HEAD not @-
description: In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3, the jj checkout verification runs 'jj log -r @- --no-graph -n 1'. Using @- (parent of working copy) for verification assumes the working copy (@) is empty after jj edit. In jj, jj edit moves the working copy to an existing commit — the @ revision IS the PR commit, not a new empty child. @- would show the commit before the PR, not the PR itself. The correct verification after 'jj edit <pr-bookmark>' would be 'jj log -r @ --no-graph -n 1' to confirm @ now points to the PR commit. File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 90

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: duplicate of h6b.2 — same @- vs @ issue in address-findings SKILL.md line 90.

title: [code] vcs-detection-preamble step 2 for jj uses pwd pattern that misidentifies location in non-worktree _worktrees/ paths
description: In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md step 2, the jj worktree location check uses 'case "$_cwd" in *_worktrees/*)'. The Known Limitation note correctly identifies that intermediate path components containing '_worktrees' cause a false-positive (agent thinks it's in a worktree when it isn't). However, the note says this is 'relatively safe — the agent just reports FAILED unnecessarily.' In fact, the consequence is NOT just unnecessary FAILED — the agent stops completely and cannot perform any work. If a developer has a repo at /home/user/code_worktrees/myrepo, all worktree-isolated agents dispatched to any sub-path of that repo will always fail step 2, making the entire agent system unusable from that path. This is a more severe impact than the note suggests. The limitation should be more strongly documented, or the check should use a more precise heuristic (e.g., verifying the worktree path segment ends with _worktrees and is immediately followed by the workspace name). File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md lines 6152-6176

title: [code] Unquoted $(which git) in three git mock blocks in test_worktree_create.bats
description: Three git mock blocks in .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats (lines 785, 814, 839) use unquoted exec $(which git) within a heredoc where git path is not pre-captured. Elsewhere in the same file (lines 436, 484) the correct pattern is used: capture real_git before the heredoc, then use ${real_git}. This inconsistency creates a word-splitting risk if the git binary path ever contains spaces. Suggested fix: add 'local real_git; real_git="$(command -v git)"' before each heredoc and replace $(which git) with ${real_git}. File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats lines 785, 814, 839

title: [tests] worktree-remove.sh: no test for git path with inferred root and jj also present but root fails
description:
test_worktree_remove.bats covers the 'inferred root with .jj/ and jj installed' paths thoroughly (workspace forget called, workspace list fails, etc.). However, it does not cover the combined scenario where detect_repo_root fails AND the inferred root has BOTH .jj/ and .git/ present (colocated) but git rev-parse fails from the inferred root.

Looking at .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 3381-3388:
  if [[ ! -d REPO_ROOT/.jj ]] && [[ ! -d REPO_ROOT/.git ]]; then
    ...skip VCS cleanup
  elif [[ ! -d REPO_ROOT/.jj ]] && ! git -C REPO_ROOT rev-parse --git-dir; then
    ...warn corrupt git, skip VCS cleanup

The colocated case (both .jj/ and .git/ in inferred root, detect_repo_root failed because CWD is outside repo) falls through to the jj cleanup branch without going through the corrupt-git check. This is actually correct behavior but there is no test covering this scenario to prevent future regression.

Relatedly, the 'inferred root with .jj/ and jj installed: workspace forget called when detect_repo_root fails' test only covers the pure-jj (root fails) scenario, not the colocated (root fails because run from outside repo) scenario.

title: [tests] VCS detection preamble lint test uses fragile two-grep OR pattern instead of proximity check
description:
The lint test in .claude/hooks/tests/test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats checks for VCS detection preamble inclusion using two independent grep calls:
  if \! grep -qE 'Follow.*(procedure|startup procedure)' file || \
     \! grep -qE '`pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble\.md`' file; then

This passes if both patterns appear ANYWHERE in the file, even non-adjacent. An agent file could satisfy this test by having an unrelated 'Follow this procedure' sentence and a separate mention of the preamble path in a comment, without actually following the preamble in the startup section.

The real behavioral contract (agent MUST run the VCS detection preamble on startup) is better enforced by eval B-FW1 and B-VCS-GIT1 (per the test comment). The lint check is a useful belt-and-suspenders check but a proximity/context-window grep (e.g., checking that both patterns appear within N lines of each other) would be more robust and reduce false-pass risk.

Low priority since the behavioral evals are the primary guard, but the lint check's false-pass surface is non-trivial given the agent files are 100-300 lines long.

title: [tests] No test for jj-init.md step 3 error-propagation (jj git init failure path)
description:
test_jj_init.bats covers the gitignore fragment (step 4) and the verification fragment (step 5) of jj/commands/jj-init.md, but these are pure shell fragments extractable for unit testing.

The comment at line 8-11 of test_jj_init.bats acknowledges that steps 1-3 are covered by behavioral evals JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4. However, the eval for JJ-B-INIT4 (jj git init --colocate fails) only asserts 'contains error' and 'not_contains .gitignore' but does NOT assert the exact stop behavior: the assertion 'no-gitignore-shell-command' checks 'not_contains: grep -qxF', which tests that the bash command is not echoed in response text but does not assert that agent execution actually stops.

A complementary behavioral eval or a more specific assertion verifying that the verification step commands (jj st, jj log) are also absent from the response when step 3 fails would improve confidence. Current JJ-B-INIT4 assertion set: surfaces-error-text, reports-failure (not_contains .gitignore), no-gitignore-shell-command (not_contains grep -qxF).

Suggested addition to JJ-B-INIT4 assertions:
  {"name": "no-verification-run", "type": "not_contains", "check": "jj st"}

File: jj/evals/evals.json, eval JJ-B-INIT4

title: [tests] JJ-B9 behavioral eval has no automated assertion (manual-only)
jj-init.md gitignore error written to stderr only; Claude tool output may not surface it as user-visible warning
description:
jj/commands/jj-init.md step 4: the gitignore append fragment writes the error to stderr (>&2) in the shell block, but the prose instruction says 'capture and surface the error as: Could not update .gitignore: ...' to the user. 

The shell fragment:
  if ! append_err=$({ echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore; } 2>&1); then
    echo "Could not update .gitignore: ..." >&2
  fi

Routes the error to stderr. When Claude runs this via Bash tool, stderr appears in tool output but may be presented differently than a user-visible message depending on the Claude UI. The prose instruction implies a user-facing message (not just stderr), but the fragment only uses >&2.

Additionally, step 5 says 'If step 4's .gitignore append failed, append this note to the success message' — but there is no mechanism to track whether step 4 failed across the step boundary. The agent must remember this from step 4's output. Since the fragment uses >&2, the agent sees it as tool stderr rather than a named variable, which could be missed.

Suggested fix: Either (a) echo the warning to stdout (no >&2) so it clearly appears in Bash tool output that Claude processes as the main response, or (b) add an explicit note to the instructions that the agent must check Bash tool stderr from step 4 and propagate the warning to step 5.

File: jj/commands/jj-init.md lines 49-53

title: address-findings SKILL.md does not specify how to derive pr-bookmark name for jj edit after gh pr checkout
description:
pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3: the jj checkout verification path says 'run jj edit <pr-bookmark>' but does not explain how to determine the bookmark name after 'gh pr checkout <number>' in a jj/colocated repo.

In a colocated jj repo, 'gh pr checkout' creates/updates a local git branch (e.g., 'feat/my-feature'). The agent must know this branch name to run 'jj edit feat/my-feature'. Without explicit guidance, the agent may:
- Guess based on the PR title (unreliable)
- Run 'jj bookmark list' and pick one (fragile if multiple bookmarks exist)
- Silently use the wrong bookmark (worst case — proceeds on wrong code)

The git path does not have this problem because 'git branch --show-current' tells you what branch you are on.

Suggested fix: Add a step to list bookmarks after gh pr checkout and identify the PR branch. For example: 'run jj bookmark list to find the bookmark corresponding to the PR branch, typically matching the PR head branch name returned by gh pr view <number> --json headRefName --jq .headRefName'.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md lines 88-90

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Resolved by h6b.12 fix — bookmark derived via gh pr view --json headRefName

The existing test 'jj path: cleanup_on_error always attempts jj workspace forget even when workspace add fails' (test_worktree_create.bats) verifies that forget IS called but does not assert the WARNING message content. A test asserting '$output == *WARNING: cleanup: jj workspace forget*' for the WORKSPACE_CREATED=false forget-failure path would catch any regression where the WARNING is accidentally escalated to ERROR (or silenced). title: Missing test: jj-init verification fragment does not test jj log failure path
description: test_jj_init.bats covers VERIFICATION_FRAGMENT for jj st failure but has no test for the path where jj st succeeds and jj log --no-graph -n 3 fails. The fragment accumulates errors with '${jj_err:+$jj_err; }${log_err}' before exiting, so a jj-log-only failure takes a different code path than jj-st-only. Without this test, a regression in the error-accumulation logic would not be caught. File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats. Suggested: @test 'verification: reports error when jj log fails but jj st succeeds'.

title: jj-init.md step 4 suppresses grep errors that may mask .gitignore permission issues
description:
In jj/commands/jj-init.md step 4, the .gitignore check uses:

  grep -qxF '.jj/' .gitignore 2>/dev/null || { ... append ... }

The 2>/dev/null suppresses all grep errors, including both 'no such file' (expected — means .gitignore does not exist, so we should append) and 'permission denied' (unexpected — means .gitignore exists but is unreadable). In the permission-denied case, grep exits non-zero with suppressed stderr, triggering the append attempt, which also fails. The append failure IS surfaced via the append_err capture. So no true silent failure, but the distinction between 'file missing' and 'file unreadable' is lost.

This is a skill (model-invoked prompt) not a shell script, so the impact is low — the model reads both branches. However, the code snippet embedded in the skill instructs the model to run this exact shell fragment.

Suggested fix: Use 'grep -qxF .jj/ .gitignore 2>/dev/null; grep_rc=$?' pattern, then check for ENOENT vs EACCES, or add a comment explaining that 2>/dev/null intentionally treats unreadable-file the same as missing-file (since append will fail and be reported).

title: detect_repo_root sets jj_rc=0 before mktemp then never uses it, creating misleading state
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root():

  local jj_out jj_err="(not run)" jj_rc=1  # line 3467
  ...
  jj_rc=0  # line 3471 — set to 0 before mktemp check
  local _jj_err_file
  _jj_err_file=$(mktemp) || {
    ...
    jj_attempted=false   # mktemp failed, skip jj
    mktemp_failed=true
  }
  if [[ "$jj_attempted" == "true" ]]; then
    jj_out=$(jj root 2>"$_jj_err_file") || jj_rc=$?   # only assigned if jj runs

When mktemp fails, jj_rc is left as 0 even though jj never ran. The error dispatch at lines 3488-3500 correctly checks jj_attempted first, so the mktemp_failed branch is reached correctly — no observable bug. However, jj_rc=0 in this state is misleading (jj did not run, did not succeed). If the error-dispatch logic is ever refactored, this stale 0 value could cause incorrect branch selection.

Suggested fix: Only set jj_rc=0 inside the 'if jj_attempted == true' guard, or initialize jj_rc to a sentinel value (e.g., -1 or leave as 1) that accurately represents 'not run'.

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md: step 2 worktree check is skipped for skills but preamble title says 'agents and skills'
description:
In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, the file header says 'Standard startup procedure for agents and skills' but step 2 (Verify location) is explicitly annotated as 'worktree-isolated agents only — orchestrator skills running from the main repo root should skip this step'.

The pr-review lint test (test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats) checks that skills also reference the VCS detection preamble and checks for the 'Follow ... procedure' pattern. This means skills are expected to invoke the full preamble, but they should skip step 2.

The preamble document does note this caveat, but the skip instruction is embedded in the body of step 2 rather than being clearly flagged at the top. A skill author reading 'Follow the startup procedure in vcs-detection-preamble.md' could implement all steps including the worktree path check, which would cause skills to fail when run from the main repo root.

Location: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, step 2 heading.

Suggested fix: Add a clear callout box or note at the top of step 2 that is prominently visible, e.g.: '> **Skills only:** Skip this step. Step 2 is for worktree-isolated agents only.' — the current parenthetical is easy to miss when following instructions quickly.

title: worktree-helpers.sh: detect_repo_root does not check .jj/ before calling jj root for non-git directories
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root falls back to 'jj root' whenever git rev-parse fails AND jj is in PATH. It does not check whether .jj/ exists in the current directory tree before spawning jj root. This means:

1. In a pure non-VCS directory with jj installed (e.g., /tmp/test-dir), detect_repo_root will spawn 'jj root', which will fail with an error, requiring the mktemp+stderr-capture path.

2. The worktree-create.sh already checks 'if [[ -d "${REPO_ROOT}/.jj" ]]' before doing jj operations, but detect_repo_root is called BEFORE REPO_ROOT is known (it IS the function that determines REPO_ROOT).

This is a minor efficiency/clarity issue rather than a correctness bug — the code handles all cases correctly via the mktemp+stderr-capture mechanism. However, callers of detect_repo_root that have .git/ will always succeed via git rev-parse, so the jj fallback only triggers in non-colocated scenarios where jj is actually needed.

The doc comment on detect_repo_root says 'Supports colocated jj repos (have .git/) and falls back to jj root for non-colocated repos when .jj/ exists.' — but the implementation does not actually check '.jj/ exists' before calling jj root; it calls jj root whenever git fails and jj is in PATH.

This discrepancy between the comment and implementation could mislead contributors about the guard condition.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root function, the comment vs implementation.

Suggested fix: Either update the comment to match the implementation ('falls back to jj root when jj is in PATH'), or add a .jj/ existence check before spawning jj root to match the comment's stated behavior.

title: worktree-remove.sh: redundant git_remove_failed=false redeclaration inside else branch
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, git_remove_failed is initialized to false at the top-level scope (line ~3637 of diff), and then redundantly re-initialized to false again at the start of the else branch (line ~3674). The variable is already false at this point because it was set at the top level and the jj branch above does not touch it. The redundant assignment adds noise and could confuse future readers into thinking the variable might be in a different state by the time we reach this branch.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, inside the 'else' block after the jj/skip_vcs_cleanup branches.

Suggested fix: Remove the duplicate git_remove_failed=false inside the else branch.

title: Fix(mxm.6): strengthen preamble reference check to verify Execute/Read context

title: Fix(mxm.5): add machine-enforceable shell guard for VCS none case
description: In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, replace prose-only instruction with a concrete shell block that exits immediately when VCS detection returns 'none'. Agents can copy-paste the guard.

title: Fix(mxm.2): arm trap before mkdir, add parent-dir cleanup guard
description: In worktree-create.sh, move trap cleanup_on_error EXIT before mkdir -p. Add a guard inside cleanup_on_error to also clean up WORKTREE_PARENT if it was created but is empty. This eliminates the theoretical signal race window.

title: Fix(mxm.1): change metadata-only cleanup failures to exit 0 with warning
description: In worktree-remove.sh, when jj is absent but directory removal succeeded, exit 0 instead of 1. Keep the WARNING on stderr. Update tests that assert exit 1 for jj-not-installed path.

title: worktree-remove: no test for non-zero exit when jj_forget_failed in regular (non-inferred) path
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 194: 'if jj_forget_failed || git_prune_failed; then exit 1; fi'. The test 'jj path: handles workspace forget failure' asserts status -eq 1 and checks error output. However, it exercises the 'forget failed with a non-idempotent error' branch which sets jj_forget_failed=true AND the test explicitly verifies exit 1. What is not tested: the path where rm -rf succeeds, workspace forget fails, AND the exit 1 at line 194 is reached through jj_forget_failed=true from the jj-not-installed branch (line 139). That path IS exercised by 'jj path: warns but still removes directory when jj not installed' which asserts status -eq 1. So the exit code path is covered. This is a documentation gap: the connection between jj_forget_failed=true and exit 1 at line 194 is not explicitly called out in any test name, making it harder to see coverage at a glance. Adding a comment or dedicated test name referencing line 194 would improve maintainability. Criticality: 2/10 — behavior is already tested, just not clearly labeled.

title: worktree-remove.sh: _ws_list_err temp file leaked if jj not installed branch taken without prior cleanup
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines ~134-140

When the jj branch is taken, the script runs:
  _ws_list_err=$(mktemp 2>/dev/null) && _ws_list_mktemp_ok=true || true

If mktemp succeeds (_ws_list_err is now a path), and then the 'command -v jj' check fails, the code sets _attempt_forget=false and jj_forget_failed=true without calling 'rm -f "$_ws_list_err"'. The temp file is eventually cleaned by the EXIT trap (which checks '[[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]]'), so this is not a permanent leak. However, if the script hits 'exit 1' at the rm_err block (line ~176) before the manual cleanup at line ~192, the EXIT trap fires and cleans up correctly.

The issue is subtle: the EXIT trap's condition relies on _ws_list_err being non-empty to do cleanup, but if _ws_list_err was set to a path and later set back to '' (lines 145, 148 do '_ws_list_err='''), the EXIT trap won't clean it up. Lines 145 and 148 both call 'rm -f "$_ws_list_err"' before resetting to '', so the manual cleanup path is correct. However the 'jj not installed' branch does NOT reset _ws_list_err to '' — leaving the EXIT trap to clean it (correct behavior since _ws_list_err still holds the path). Minor but worth auditing during review.

title: test_pr_review_agents_lint: preamble reference check is text-only, not path-load verification
description: In .claude/hooks/tests/test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats, the check verifies that each agent file contains the text pattern '(references/|pr-review/).*vcs-detection-preamble'. This is a textual grep, not a validation that the preamble is actually referenced in a loadable way (e.g., as a path in an 'Execute' instruction vs. an inline comment). An agent could satisfy the grep by mentioning the path in a comment without actually instructing the model to read the file. The test is documented as intentionally structural, but a stronger check would verify the preamble path appears in an 'Execute' or 'Read' instruction context. Criticality: 3/10 — the existing check catches the most obvious omissions; a false positive (comment mention) would require deliberate circumvention.

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md: 'none' VCS case has no fallback — agent hangs or crashes if neither jj nor git is available
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, VCS detection step

The detection snippet:
  if jj root >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "jj"
  elif git rev-parse --git-dir >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "git"
  else echo "none"; fi

Instructions say 'If the result is none, STOP and report STATUS: FAILED'. However, this is a prose instruction to the model, not a shell script guard. If the model fails to check the output before proceeding (e.g. due to context pressure), subsequent VCS commands (jj st, git status) will fail with opaque errors rather than the clear STATUS: FAILED message.

A stronger pattern would be a single shell block that exits immediately:
  USE_VCS=$(...)
  [[ "$USE_VCS" == "none" ]] && { echo "STATUS: FAILED -- No VCS detected"; exit 1; }

This makes the guard machine-enforceable rather than instruction-enforceable.

title: jj-init step 5 verification failure has no unit test
description: jj/commands/jj-init.md step 5 says: if jj st or jj log fails, report the error and advise the user to check manually. This failure path has no unit test in .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats — only the gitignore fragment (step 4) is unit-tested. The step 5 failure path is covered only by behavioral eval JJ-B-INIT2, which verifies jj st is called but does not assert failure handling. A unit test for the verification fragment testing that a failed jj st produces the correct advisory message would add regression coverage. The path is simple but a broken message would be invisible to CI without a test.

title: worktree-create.sh: trap disarmed before mkdir, leaving window with no cleanup on exit-trap interactions
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, lines 87-92

The script sequence is:
  trap - EXIT          # line 87: disarm any pre-existing trap
  mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT" || { echo ERROR; exit 1; }
  trap cleanup_on_error EXIT   # line 92: arm our trap

This is intentional per comments, but the pattern is fragile. If `mkdir -p` succeeds but the shell receives an asynchronous signal between lines 87 and 92, no trap would fire and partially-created state could remain. The inline '|| { exit 1 }' handles synchronous mkdir failure but cannot handle async signals during this window.

This is a low-probability race but worth documenting. A more robust approach would arm the trap before mkdir and include the parent directory cleanup inside cleanup_on_error (with a guard checking whether mkdir had succeeded). This is a suggestion rather than a critical defect since the window is extremely narrow and async signals are unlikely in hook context.

title: worktree-create.sh: jj version-too-old error message suppressed by sanitize_for_output truncation
description: RETRACTED: Not a real issue -- the grep detection on line 103 checks full jj_out without truncation. Display truncation on line 106 doesn't affect detection. Below confidence threshold.

title: worktree-remove.sh: git_remove_failed referenced after block scope it's defined in
description: In worktree-remove.sh, git_remove_failed is declared and set inside the 'else' branch of the jj/git if-elif-else block (lines 164-168), but referenced on line 180 outside that block via ${git_remove_failed:-false}. While the :-false default makes this safe at runtime (bash local variables don't go out of scope at block boundaries), it is a readability issue: a reader must notice the :-false guard to understand why this works when the jj path is taken. The variable would be clearer if initialized to 'false' alongside jj_forget_failed and git_prune_failed on lines 124-125. Suggested fix: add 'git_remove_failed=false' on line 125 alongside the other boolean initializations.

title: no test for worktree-create.sh jj path when jj workspace add produces --name-incompatible error alongside partial directory creation
description: worktree-create.sh lines 3254-3259: when jj workspace add fails with an error matching 'unexpected argument|unrecognized|unknown' for --name, the script emits an 'jj version too old' message. The existing tests (test_worktree_create.bats 'rejects old jj with ...' tests) cover the error message for old jj, but none of those tests pre-create a partial directory. If an old jj binary both rejects --name AND creates the target directory before exiting, the cleanup_on_error path for the 'version too old' case has no coverage. The cleanup would still run (EXIT trap fires), but the test does not verify the directory is cleaned up when the old-jj error pattern matches. Criticality: 4/10 — the cleanup path is the same regardless of error message content, and the existing partial-add cleanup test ('cleans up mkdir-created directory') covers the general mechanism, but the specific combination of old-jj error + partial directory is untested.

title: detect_repo_root: mktemp failure sets jj_attempted=false but mktemp_failed=true — logic is correct but fragile to future refactoring
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root function (diff lines 3347-3376)

When mktemp fails, the code sets jj_attempted=false and mktemp_failed=true inside a || block:
  _jj_err_file=$(mktemp) || {
    echo "WARNING: mktemp failed..." >&2
    jj_attempted=false
    mktemp_failed=true
  }

Then the subsequent 'if [[ "$jj_attempted" == true ]]; then' block is skipped, and the error message branch correctly reaches 'elif [[ "$mktemp_failed" == true ]]; then'. 

The issue: jj_rc is declared but never initialized (local jj_rc=0 is inside the if block). If jj_attempted is false (mktemp failure), the error path relies on the mktemp_failed check to avoid referencing uninitialized jj_rc. This is safe today, but a future addition inside the jj_attempted==false block that references $jj_rc would silently use the default value of 0 (treating it as success).

Also, the jj_err variable is not initialized before the if block that sets it. If future code references $jj_err when jj_attempted is false, it will be empty with no indication it was never populated.

Suggested fix: Initialize jj_rc=1 and jj_err='(not run)' before the conditional block so any accidental access during future refactoring produces a visible rather than silent default.

title: worktree-remove.sh: no test for jj workspace forget when mktemp-for-ws-list fails but workspace list output is needed for idempotent-forget check
description: test_worktree_remove.bats covers the mktemp failure for the secondary _ws_list_err file (the 'WARNING when secondary mktemp fails' test at line 3042). However, the production code path at worktree-remove.sh line 3523-3525 emits a WARNING and sets _attempt_forget=true when _ws_list_mktemp_ok is false. The test verifies the output WARNING and that forget is called, but it does not check the exit code is 0 in the success-forget sub-case or that the correct workspace name appears in the forget-arg log. This means a regression where mktemp failure causes the wrong workspace name to be passed to forget would not be caught. Criticality: 3/10 — the test exists but the forget-arg assertion is missing, so the behavioral contract on the workspace name passed to forget is unverified in the mktemp-failure path.

title: test_pr_review_agents_lint: preamble check is text-only with no behavioral contract test for jj-specific branching
description: test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats line 703: the lint test verifies that agent .md files textually reference 'vcs-detection-preamble', but does not verify that the preamble invocation is syntactically correct (e.g., correct relative path, correct shell block). A file could reference the string in a comment and still pass. The comment in the test acknowledges runtime behavior is covered by behavioral evals (B-FW1, B-VCS-GIT1), but those evals are LLM-evaluated and not part of the CI BATS run. A lightweight structural grep for the actual read/source pattern would catch copy-paste errors where the reference string is present but the include is malformed. Criticality: 3/10 — low risk because functional breakage would be noticed, and evals provide indirect coverage.

title: jj-init.md: .gitignore append error is only surfaced via echo, not propagated as failure
description:
File: jj/commands/jj-init.md (diff lines 6316-6320)

The .gitignore append step uses:
  grep -qxF '.jj/' .gitignore 2>/dev/null || {
    if \! append_err=$({ echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore; } 2>&1); then
      echo "Could not update .gitignore: ${append_err:-permission denied}"
    fi
  }

When the append fails, the code outputs the error via echo to stdout (not stderr), and continues to step 5 (verification). The jj-init.md prose says to surface the error, which it does — but only via an inline echo inside a subshell that gets swallowed into append_err. The outer echo is in the script body, not directed to stderr, so it will mix with normal output in Claude's response.

More importantly: the script proceeds to step 5 (jj st + jj log) and reports SUCCESS even when .gitignore was not updated. While step 5's success message correctly appends the 'NOTE: .jj/ was not added' caveat, the error from the append is printed to stdout at step 4 but the success message at step 5 may visually override it in a long output stream, creating a soft silent failure for the user.

Suggested fix: Direct the 'Could not update .gitignore' message to stderr so it's visually distinct, or set a flag variable and check it in step 5 before emitting the success message. The current flow is technically correct but the warning is easy to miss.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Comprehensive review of PR #31 adding jj VCS support across all plugins. 65 files, 7470 additions, 304 deletions. Aspects: code, tests, errors.

title: Fix(.62): disarm EXIT trap before mkdir failure exit

title: Fix(.61): replace jj new with jj edit in address-findings

title: jj plugin.json missing 'commands' and 'skills' registry entries

title: address-findings jj integration: jj log conflict check uses non-portable template syntax
description: After re-reading the diff more carefully: the template syntax 'if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "OK")' in the skill doc is inside a bash code block with single quotes around the -T argument, so the double quotes are literal and valid. The jj template if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "OK") is correct jj template syntax. The _jj_log_failed pattern with assignment || true is also correct. This finding was a false positive.

title: worktree-create.sh EXIT trap fires on mkdir failure, causing spurious jj workspace forget warning
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, when mkdir -p WORKTREE_PARENT fails, the script does:

  mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT" || {
    echo "ERROR: failed to create worktree parent directory ..." >&2
    exit 1
  }

Under set -e with a trap cleanup_on_error EXIT, the explicit exit 1 inside the || block fires the EXIT trap. cleanup_on_error runs with WORKSPACE_CREATED=false. In a jj repo (.jj/ exists), it attempts jj workspace forget worktree-NAME, which fails because no workspace was ever created. This produces:

  WARNING: cleanup: jj workspace forget worktree-<name> failed (may not have been registered)

This warning is noisy and misleading — the mkdir failure was the real error and no jj workspace was ever involved.

The test 'jj path: fails gracefully when mkdir -p fails' (test_worktree_create.bats line ~1091) does not assert the absence of a spurious jj workspace forget warning, so this goes undetected.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh

Suggested fix: Before calling exit 1 in the mkdir failure block, run trap - EXIT to disarm the cleanup trap since there is nothing to clean up at that point.

title: jj checkout uses 'jj new' instead of 'jj edit' in address-findings
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3 (jj VCS path), the checkout verification instructs: 'Then run jj new <pr-bookmark>'. This is semantically wrong.

jj new <pr-bookmark> creates a NEW empty commit whose parent is the PR bookmark's tip. After this operation, @ (the working copy) is an empty child commit, not the PR bookmark itself.

For the orchestrator to be positioned at the PR bookmark's tip, the correct command is jj edit <pr-bookmark>. This makes @ equal to the existing PR commit, not a new empty child.

Downstream impact: over multiple fix integration rounds, empty orphan commits accumulate above the bookmark (since each round starts with a new empty @). The jj log -r @- -n 1 verify step masks the problem because @- correctly shows the PR bookmark tip.

File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, in Phase 1 step 3 jj checkout block.

Fix: Replace 'jj new <pr-bookmark>' with 'jj edit <pr-bookmark>'.

title: Fix(.52): add forget-called assertion to secondary mktemp test

title: Fix(.50): add test for detect_repo_root jj stderr error format

title: Fix(.49): add setup_jj_only helper for jj-specific tests

title: Fix(.53): move git worktree prune after rm -rf

title: Secondary mktemp failure test does not verify jj workspace forget is attempted
description: The test 'WARNING when secondary mktemp fails for jj workspace list' (test_worktree_remove.bats ~line 843) verifies the warning message and exit status (1) when the second mktemp call fails (preventing jj workspace list). However, the jj mock in this test exits 1 for all commands except 'root', so 'jj workspace forget' will also fail. The test only asserts the warning message and that the directory was removed — it does not assert that jj workspace forget was actually attempted. If a regression removed the '_attempt_forget=true' branch when _ws_list_mktemp_ok=false (worktree-remove.sh line ~129), the test would still pass because the exit code 1 is expected due to jj_forget_failed=true from forget failing. An assertion like '[ -f ${JJ_ROOT}/forget-called.log ]' using a logging jj mock would confirm forget is actually called, not just that its failure is correctly reported.

title: No test for detect_repo_root path when jj root returns non-zero and jj stderr is captured via temp file
description: test_worktree_helpers.bats covers the case where jj root returns a file path, a non-existent directory, or empty output. It also covers the mktemp failure path and the sanitize_for_output on jj stderr. However, there is no test for the case where jj root exits non-zero AND the jj_err variable contains a non-empty string (i.e., the standard error path in detect_repo_root at line 1321 of worktree-helpers.sh: 'ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse and jj root both failed; jj: ...)'). The test 'sanitize_for_output strips control chars from jj error output' (line 1989) tests sanitization of jj stderr but the mock outputs to stderr and exits 1—the assertion only checks that the sanitized content appears in error output, not that the error message format matches the actual ERROR string produced by detect_repo_root. A dedicated test confirming the exact error message format when jj root fails with stderr would catch regressions in the error formatting at lines 1320-1327 of worktree-helpers.sh.

title: test_worktree_remove.bats setup() leaves stale real git worktree when jj tests run
description: The setup() in test_worktree_remove.bats creates a real git worktree at ${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees/test-wt, then many jj-specific tests call setup_jj_worktree() which adds a jj directory but does NOT clean up the git worktree first. Tests that assert '[ \! -d ${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees ]' after removing only the jj worktree could fail on timing issues if the test-wt directory still exists. Several tests explicitly work around this by calling 'git -C $REPO_ROOT worktree remove --force ${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees/test-wt' inline before proceeding (e.g., jj path: cleans up empty parent directory after removal). However tests that do NOT perform this cleanup step first leave the test-wt directory present, making any assertion of '[ \! -d ${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees ]' in those tests unreliable. Suggested fix: add a helper setup_jj_only_worktree() that tears down the git worktree before setting up the jj state, or reorganize jj tests into their own describe block with a jj-specific setup().

title: Fix(.41): clarify _ws_list_err sentinel with explicit boolean
description: Add _ws_list_mktemp_ok boolean to make mktemp failure state explicit instead of relying on empty-string dual semantics

title: Fix(.37): add automated assertion for forget-before-rmrf ordering
description: Add automated contains assertions to B-JJ3 eval for jj workspace forget and rm -rf so CI catches ordering regressions

title: Fix(.40): add cd REPO_ROOT to git worktree prune in cleanup

title: Fix(.38): add BATS test for jj workspace add with whitespace path

title: Fix(.36): make JJ-B5 assertion less brittle

title: Fix(.35): rephrase JJ-B-CLEANUP1 eval to test hook stderr surfacing
