title: Fix(.39): add git_prune_failed flag for symmetric exit codes
description: Add git_prune_failed flag in worktree-remove.sh git path, exit 1 when both git worktree remove and prune fail

title: worktree-remove.sh: _ws_list_err empty-string sentinel conflates two distinct states
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 128, the pattern _ws_list_err=$(mktemp 2>/dev/null) || true uses an empty string as a sentinel to detect mktemp failure. The elif [[ -z "$_ws_list_err" ]] check at line 133 conflates two states: (1) mktemp failed returning nothing, and (2) mktemp succeeded but returned empty filename. In practice case 2 is impossible, but the code does not document this invariant. The EXIT trap guard at line 67 also relies on this invariant: [[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err". The variable encodes three states (never-used/empty-init, mktemp-failed/empty, has-path) into a single string. Suggested fix: use a separate boolean _ws_list_mktemp_failed=false alongside _ws_list_err to make failure state explicit, rather than relying on empty-string dual-semantics.

title: git worktree prune in cleanup_on_error runs without cd to REPO_ROOT
description:
File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills_worktrees/agent-ad5b4209/.claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh

Line 70: if ! prune_err=$(git worktree prune 2>&1)

What is wrong:
In the cleanup_on_error function, the git partial-add branch (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false) calls git worktree prune without first cd-ing to REPO_ROOT. git worktree prune operates on the repo detected from the current working directory (CWD). If the hook CWD differs from REPO_ROOT (e.g., the hook was invoked from a subdirectory or from an unrelated directory), the prune command would either fail or target the wrong repository.

By contrast, the jj branch at line 43 correctly does: jj_err=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace forget ...) -- the cd is explicit.

This is a latent risk: in typical Claude Code hook invocation the CWD is likely inside the repo, but the contract is not enforced and a future change could break this silently.

Suggested fix:
Change line 70 to:
  if ! prune_err=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && git worktree prune 2>&1)

The WORKSPACE_CREATED=true git branch at line 62 similarly calls git worktree remove without cd, but git worktree remove takes the worktree path explicitly and finds the repo from --worktree-path, so it is less at risk.

title: BATS tests have no coverage for jj workspace add producing a path with embedded spaces or special chars in workspace name
description: The worktree-create.sh validation (validate_safe_name) rejects names with shell metacharacters before reaching jj. However, there is no BATS test verifying that the workspace name passed to 'jj workspace add --name' is correctly quoted/sanitized when used in the jj invocation itself. The logging mock in create_logging_jj_mock captures args via echo $*, which word-splits. If validate_safe_name were ever weakened to allow more chars, the quoting in the jj call (jj workspace add $WORKTREE_PATH --name worktree-${NAME}) could become an injection vector. A test with a name containing only valid chars that are shell-significant in other contexts (e.g., a name with a lone dot like 'a.b') would strengthen confidence that the workspace name survives the subshell call intact. This is a low-risk gap given validate_safe_name's strict allowlist, but worth noting for defense-in-depth. File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats.

title: No behavioral eval for fix-worker 'jj workspace forget before rm -rf' ordering in cleanup path
description: pr-review/evals/evals.json has eval B-JJ3 which tests that fix-worker cleanup does 'jj workspace forget' before 'rm -rf'. The ordering assertion is type: manual with check: 'Verify jj workspace forget appears BEFORE rm -rf in the output sequence'. This is the critical invariant — removing the directory before forgetting the workspace would leave jj in an inconsistent state. The manual-only assertion means automated CI cannot catch a regression where fix-worker (or the orchestrating skill) reverses the order. Consider adding an automated assertion that checks for the sequence, e.g., by asserting 'jj workspace forget' appears in the response AND adding a second contains check for 'rm -rf' with a note that the manual assertion verifies ordering. The manual assertion alone means this regression path has no automated signal. File: pr-review/evals/evals.json, eval id B-JJ3.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding already resolved — B-JJ3 already has automated contains assertions for 'jj workspace forget' and 'rm -rf' (added in commit a91b40b).

title: JJ-B5 automated assertion 'Do NOT' is too tightly coupled to exact skill phrasing
description: jj/evals/evals.json eval JJ-B5 has an automated assertion with check: 'Do NOT' (type: contains). This asserts the skill output contains the literal phrase 'Do NOT', which is brittle — it will fail if the skill is reworded to use 'Never use' or 'Avoid using' or other equivalent phrasing. The manual assertion for the same eval uses a more behavioral description ('Output warns that mutating git commands should not be used in jj repos'). The automated check should be changed to something like 'git rebase' (verify the response addresses the specific command) or 'jj rebase' (verify it offers the jj equivalent), rather than asserting on exact warning phrasing. File: jj/evals/evals.json, eval id JJ-B5, assertion 'warns-no-git-mutate-auto'.

title: Fix(7ju.19): add behavioral eval JJ-B-CLEANUP1 for worktree-create cleanup error escalation
description: Add eval testing agent behavior when worktree-create returns non-zero with ERROR about leaked workspace metadata

title: Fix(7ju.20): add CI-coverage comment to self-skipping POSIX fallback test

title: Fix(7ju.21): add unit test for detect_repo_root when jj root returns empty string

title: Fix(7ju.24): document cleanup exit code promotion behavior in worktree-create
description: Exit code promotion at line 79 only applies when _exit_code was 0. Add comment documenting this is intentional: compound failure preserves original error code, cleanup failure is surfaced via WARNING/ERROR on stderr.

title: Fix(7ju.18): update test for always-attempt-forget behavior in worktree-remove

title: Fix(7ju.23): guard jj_rc/jj_out evaluation with jj_attempted check

title: Fix(7ju.22): remove grep -qF format check, always attempt jj workspace forget

title: Fix(7ju.25): align BATS GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT with jj-init.md production fragment
description: Align GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT in test_jj_init.bats with the verbatim fragment from jj-init.md step 4 (braced group + fallback)

title: worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error does not capture its own exit code on rm failure
description:
In worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error(), the function captures $? into _exit_code at the start (line 32), but if rm -rf at line 74 fails AND the original _exit_code was already non-zero, CLEANUP_FAILED is set to true but _exit_code is NOT promoted (the promotion at line 79 only applies when _exit_code was 0). This means a compound failure (original error + cleanup rm failure) exits with the original error code and the rm failure is only surfaced via a WARNING message on stderr.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, lines 74-81.

In practice this is acceptable because the rm failure message is still emitted to stderr. However, callers that only inspect exit codes will not see that cleanup also failed. This is a minor UX issue rather than a silent failure, since the WARNING is logged.

Suggested fix: document explicitly that cleanup failures are always logged via WARNING/ERROR on stderr regardless of exit code, or change the exit-code promotion to apply when _exit_code is non-zero as well (e.g. set to a distinct error code or use bitwise OR).

title: detect_repo_root uses unguarded jj_rc/jj_out variables after mktemp failure sets jj_attempted=false
description:
In worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root(), when mktemp fails (lines 60-64), jj_attempted is set to false and mktemp_failed=true. However, the locals jj_out, jj_err, jj_rc are declared with initial values (jj_rc=0, jj_out/jj_err empty) at line 58 inside the same if block. Line 70 then evaluates [[ $jj_rc -eq 0 && -n "$jj_out" && -d "$jj_out" ]] — this accidentally evaluates to false (jj_out is empty) so the function does not return 0 incorrectly.

This is not a runtime bug because jj_rc=0 + jj_out='' makes the guard false. However, it is fragile: the code appears to assume jj_rc/jj_out are only meaningful after jj_attempted==true, but line 70 is inside the outer 'if command -v jj' block regardless of jj_attempted. A future refactor that changes the initialization could introduce a false positive return.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, lines 58-73.

Suggested fix: wrap line 70's check inside 'if [[ "$jj_attempted" == "true" ]]; then' to make the guard semantically clear and resilient to refactoring.

title: detect_repo_root: jj root returning empty string path not covered by test
description:
The detect_repo_root function in worktree-helpers.sh has three distinct jj failure branches:
1. jj_rc != 0 (jj root exited non-zero)
2. jj_out is empty (jj root succeeded but returned empty string)
3. jj_out is non-empty but not a directory (jj root returned a file path)

Branch 1 is covered by 'detect_repo_root: sanitize_for_output strips control chars from jj error output'.
Branch 3 is covered by 'detect_repo_root: falls through to error when jj root returns a file path'.
Branch 2 (empty output, exit 0) is NOT explicitly covered in test_worktree_helpers.bats.

The worktree-remove.sh test 'infers repo root when jj root returns empty in pure-jj repo' (line ~2773) covers the empty-return case for worktree-remove but this goes through the inference fallback rather than directly testing detect_repo_root.

Suggested test: a unit test in test_worktree_helpers.bats that creates a mock jj which exits 0 with empty stdout, and verifies detect_repo_root emits the 'jj root returned empty output' error message.

Location: worktree-helpers.sh lines ~3273-3274 (the empty output error branch), test_worktree_helpers.bats (missing test).

title: POSIX fallback test self-skips on Ubuntu CI — coverage gap for cd+pwd-P path in worktree-remove.sh
description:
test_worktree_remove.bats contains 'POSIX fallback: removes worktree when realpath unavailable' (line ~2839) which correctly uses 'skip' when realpath is available. On ubuntu-latest (the CI environment per .github/workflows/check-skills.yml), realpath is always present in /usr/bin, so this test never runs.

The companion test 'POSIX fallback: removes worktree when realpath absent from PATH' (line ~2861) correctly addresses this by building a curated PATH that excludes realpath — and this test DOES run in CI. So the primary fallback path for worktree removal is covered.

However, the first self-skipping test at line ~2839 provides no value in CI and could create a false sense of POSIX coverage to future maintainers who only look at test counts. Suggest either: (a) removing the self-skipping test entirely since the curated-PATH variant covers the same code path, or (b) adding a comment explicitly noting it is a manual-only test for POSIX-minimal systems, and that CI coverage comes from the curated-PATH variant.

Location: test_worktree_remove.bats line ~2839.

title: No behavioral eval for jj workspace add --name fallback when WORKSPACE_CREATED flag goes missing post-refactor
description:
The worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error function distinguishes between WORKSPACE_CREATED=true (leak risk → ERROR) and WORKSPACE_CREATED=false (partial → WARNING). This distinction is critical: the WORKSPACE_CREATED=false path relies on the assumption that jj workspace add either fully registers or fully fails.

The BATS tests cover the WORKSPACE_CREATED flag transitions thoroughly at the unit level. However, there is no behavioral eval in jj/evals/evals.json testing the agent-level behavior when cleanup emits an ERROR-level message (WORKSPACE_CREATED=true + jj workspace forget fails in cleanup). The agent should surface this to the user as a metadata leak requiring manual jj workspace forget, not just log and continue.

Suggested eval: JJ-B-CLEANUP1 testing that when worktree-create returns non-zero with an ERROR about leaked workspace metadata, the jj skill correctly instructs the user to run 'jj workspace forget worktree-NAME' manually before retrying.

This gap means the cleanup error escalation path is tested at the shell level but not at the agent behavior level.

title: Fix(.3): add test for jj workspace list format variation
description: Add BATS test covering colon-anchor grep format variation in worktree-remove.sh

title: Fix(.5): add BATS tests for jj-init steps 1-3
description: Add BATS tests for prerequisite check, already-init guard, and jj git init steps

title: Fix(.10): fix error capture for .gitignore append in jj-init.md
description: When >> redirection fails, error goes to stderr not stdout. Fix capture pattern.

title: Fix(.2): reset _ws_list_err after inline rm in worktree-remove.sh
description: After rm -f _ws_list_err on lines 139 and 142, set _ws_list_err='' so EXIT trap skips cleanup

title: Fix(.4): defer trap clear until after lefthook install in worktree-create.sh
description: Move trap - EXIT after lefthook install block so cleanup fires if lefthook causes abnormal exit

title: Fix(.1): handle mktemp failure in worktree-remove.sh jj branch
description: When mktemp fails, attempt jj workspace forget directly instead of marking jj_forget_failed

title: Fix(.6): add detached HEAD check in git branch preamble
description: Add empty-branch guard after git branch --show-current in vcs-detection-preamble.md

title: jj-init.md step 4: error capture for .gitignore append will be empty when redirection fails, producing unhelpful error message
description: In jj/commands/jj-init.md step 4, the error capture pattern is: append_err=$(echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore 2>&1). When the >> redirection fails (e.g., .gitignore is read-only), the error message from the shell about the failed redirection goes to the shell's own stderr, not to the subshell's stderr that 2>&1 redirects. As a result append_err will be empty and the error message surfaced to the user will be 'Could not update .gitignore: ' with no detail. The 2>&1 only redirects stdout→stderr within the subshell; the redirection failure itself is reported by the outer shell before the subshell runs echo. Suggested fix: use a temp file or explicit write command: printf '.jj/\n' >> .gitignore to make the failure detectable, or restructure as: { echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore; } 2>&1 and capture the group output. Alternatively: if [[ \! -w .gitignore ]]; then report permissions error explicitly before attempting the append.

title: worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error: no test for REPO_ROOT missing + jj not installed (WORKSPACE_CREATED=false path)
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 50-57
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats

worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error has this branch (lines 50-57) for the case where REPO_ROOT exists, .jj/ exists, jj is NOT installed, and WORKSPACE_CREATED is false:

    else
      echo "WARNING: cleanup: .jj/ found but jj not installed — workspace metadata not cleaned; run 'jj workspace forget worktree-${NAME}' manually" >&2
    fi

The existing tests cover:
- WORKSPACE_CREATED=true + jj not installed → ERROR (test line 268: 'cleanup_on_error errors jj-not-in-PATH after successful workspace add')
- REPO_ROOT missing → WARNING (test line 771: 'cleanup_on_error warns and skips VCS cleanup when REPO_ROOT is missing')

But there is NO test for: WORKSPACE_CREATED=false + .jj/ present + jj not installed. This branch emits WARNING (not ERROR) and is the path taken when mkdir -p for WORKTREE_PARENT fails in a jj repo before workspace add is attempted.

The 'jj path: mkdir -p failure exits 1 with descriptive error message' test (line 596) exercises the mkdir failure but uses a mock jj (so the 'jj not installed' branch is never reached).

Suggested test: patch the script to fail after mkdir (simulating mkdir success but pre-workspace-add failure), run with .jj/ present and PATH excluding jj, assert WARNING message about 'workspace metadata not cleaned'.

title: jj-init.md: steps 1-3 (prereq check, already-init guard, jj git init) have no BATS tests — only behavioral evals
description:
File: jj/commands/jj-init.md
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats

The test file test_jj_init.bats (line 7-9) explicitly documents that steps 1-3 of jj-init.md (prerequisite check, already-initialized guard, jj git init --colocate) are not tested as BATS shell fragments because they are agent instructions rather than shell scripts.

The behavioral evals JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT5 in jj/evals/evals.json cover these steps at the model-behavior level. This is a reasonable approach for agent-defined workflows, but it means:

1. The `command -v jj` prerequisite check (step 1) is tested only via JJ-B-INIT5 (behavioral eval)
2. The `test -d .jj` already-init guard (step 2) is tested only via JJ-B-INIT1 (behavioral eval)
3. The error-halt behavior when jj git init fails (step 3, JJ-B-INIT4) has a not_contains assertion checking that .gitignore is not modified

The coverage gap: JJ-B-INIT4 asserts `not_contains: .gitignore` but that is checking the model's output text, not whether the model actually skips the gitignore file-system operation. A model could say 'updating .gitignore...' in its response while simultaneously calling the Edit tool. The assertion would pass but the behavior would be wrong.

Suggested: Add a not_contains assertion for 'grep -qxF' (the gitignore fragment command from step 4) to more tightly verify that the step 4 shell code is not executed on step 3 failure. Alternatively, document this limitation in the test file comment.

title: worktree-create.sh: trap cleared before lefthook install, leaving no cleanup if lefthook install causes abnormal exit
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, the EXIT trap (cleanup_on_error) is cleared at line 116 (trap - EXIT) after successful VCS workspace creation. Lines 119-123 then run lefthook install. If lefthook install itself triggers an abnormal exit (e.g., a signal, or some other unhandled set -e condition), no cleanup runs. The worktree directory and jj workspace registration would be left behind. This is low severity because lefthook failures are caught with 'if \! lh_err=$(...); then ... fi' pattern (line 120) which prevents set -e from triggering, and the WARNING is emitted on failure. However the trap - at line 116 is placed before the echo "$WORKTREE_PATH" at line 125 -- if an unexpected error occurs between trap - and echo, the caller gets no path and the worktree is leaked. Suggestion: move trap - EXIT to after echo "$WORKTREE_PATH", or keep the trap active through lefthook install but have it not remove the (successfully created) worktree on lefthook failure.

title: worktree-remove.sh: no test for jj workspace list output format variation — colon-anchor grep may fail on different jj versions
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 143
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats

The colon anchor in `grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:"` (line 143 of worktree-remove.sh) relies on jj workspace list output using 'name: ...' format. The existing 'colon anchor prevents prefix name false positive match' test (line 575 of test_worktree_remove.bats) correctly validates the prefix-ambiguity case.

However, all mock jj workspace list responses in tests use the same output format ('worktree-name: commit-id ...'). There is no test covering what happens if jj workspace list output omits the colon or uses a different format (e.g., a future jj version changes the format). If jj ever changes this output, the colon-anchor check silently skips workspace forget — leaving orphaned workspace metadata.

Suggested test: add a test where jj workspace list returns the workspace name without a trailing colon (e.g., just 'worktree-test-jj-wt') and verify the current behavior (skip forget, emit WARNING). This documents the format dependency and would catch a regression if the production code is changed to handle format variants.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Review of PR #31: Jujutsu VCS support — new jj plugin, VCS detection preamble in all pr-review agents/skills, VCS-aware worktree hooks, fix-worker jj integration, evals, release config updates

title: JJ-B-INIT2 eval missing verification step assertion — jj st check not tested
description:
jj/evals/evals.json eval JJ-B-INIT2 tests the happy-path /jj-init flow with two assertions:
1. 'contains': 'jj git init --colocate' — verifies the init command is issued
2. 'contains': '.gitignore' — verifies .gitignore is updated

However, the jj-init.md step 4 workflow includes a verification step ('jj st') after initialization to confirm the colocated repo is healthy. There is no assertion that this verification step is performed. A regression that skips verification would pass this eval silently.

Location: jj/evals/evals.json, eval id JJ-B-INIT2

Suggested fix: Add a third assertion:
  { "name": "runs-verification", "description": "Runs jj st to verify initialization succeeded", "type": "contains", "check": "jj st" }

This is a low-criticality gap since the init command itself would fail visibly if jj wasn't installed, but it provides defense-in-depth for the step sequence.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: B-VCS-GIT1 eval 'no-file-edits' assertion is fragile — 'not_contains: Edit' false-fails on prose output
description:
pr-review/evals/evals.json eval B-VCS-GIT1 has an assertion:
  { "name": "no-file-edits", "type": "not_contains", "check": "Edit" }
This asserts that the agent's fix-worker response does not contain the word 'Edit'. The intent is to verify the Edit tool was not invoked. However, 'Edit' is a common English word that can appear in prose (e.g. 'Cannot Edit files...', 'This would Edit the wrong branch'). A correct implementation that explains why it stopped could trigger a false failure.

Location: pr-review/evals/evals.json, eval id B-VCS-GIT1, assertion 'no-file-edits'.

Suggested fix: Use a more specific check string that is less likely to appear in prose, such as 'Edit(' (the tool invocation syntax) or switch to a 'manual' type assertion with a description like 'Verify no Edit tool calls appear in agent trace'.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: address-findings SKILL.md references jj undo for post-bookmark-set failure but jj bookmark set --allow-backwards not mentioned
description:
In address-findings/SKILL.md (Phase 4b, Jj repos section, step 4), when jj bookmark set fails, the recovery procedure is: run jj undo once (reverts the rebase), then clean up workspace and re-queue.

A common reason jj bookmark set fails is that the bookmark already points to a descendent of the target (a 'backwards move'). In this case, jj bookmark set requires the --allow-backwards flag. The skill should mention this specific failure mode and the fix (retry with --allow-backwards) as a first-line recovery before escalating to jj undo.

Without this guidance, the orchestrator always treats bookmark set failure as unrecoverable (undo + re-queue), even when a simple retry with --allow-backwards would succeed. This results in unnecessary re-queuing of findings that could be fixed immediately.

Location: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b Jj repos section, step 4 'Update the bookmark'

Suggested fix: Before the jj undo recovery path, add:
  If the error contains 'would move backwards', retry with:
    jj bookmark set <pr-bookmark> -r <change-id> --allow-backwards
  If this succeeds, continue to step 5 (workspace cleanup). Only escalate to jj undo if the retry also fails.

title: worktree-create.sh jj workspace add output lost on success — no confirmation logged
description:
In worktree-create.sh, the jj workspace add output is captured into jj_out (via 2>&1 redirect combining stdout+stderr) but is silently discarded on success. For the git path, git worktree add also captures into git_err which is discarded on success. This is intentional (no spurious output to framework).

However, on the jj path specifically, jj workspace add typically prints a confirmation message like 'Created workspace in ...' to stdout. That confirmation is mixed with stderr into jj_out and then swallowed. If something goes wrong after the workspace is created (during lefthook install), there is no log of which workspace was actually created.

This is a minor observability gap. In contrast to the git path where the WORKTREE_PATH is known precisely from the input, the jj workspace add may resolve the path differently (e.g., symlink resolution). The script assumes jj created the workspace at exactly $WORKTREE_PATH, which is correct per jj's contract, but there's no validation that the directory was actually created at that path.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, lines 97-106

This is a low-impact observation; the test 'jj path: forwards --name flag to jj workspace add' validates the --name flag is passed, but no test validates that the created workspace directory matches WORKTREE_PATH exactly. Not a blocking issue.

title: jujutsu SKILL.md trigger description uses .jj/ directory check not command-based detection
description:
The jujutsu skill description (jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md) says 'MUST activate on ANY VCS operation when .jj/ exists in the repo root'. However, the vcs-detection-preamble.md instructs agents to use 'jj root >/dev/null 2>&1' (command-based detection) rather than checking for .jj/ directory presence.

The skill description uses a file-existence trigger (.jj/) while the runtime preamble uses a command-based trigger (jj root). These are inconsistent:
- jj root succeeds in jj workspaces where .jj/ is NOT present locally (only in the main repo root)
- A workspace that has no .jj/ locally would not trigger the skill by the description's criterion, but would succeed with the command-based preamble check

For agents running in jj workspaces (which lack a local .jj/ directory), the skill description's '.jj/ exists' criterion would NOT trigger the skill. However, the actual runtime behavior (using jj root) would detect the jj repo correctly.

While this inconsistency doesn't break functionality (Claude agents use the description to decide when to load the skill, and skills load lazily), it creates a misleading gap between the trigger description and actual runtime detection approach.

Location: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md, description field in YAML frontmatter

Suggested fix: Update the description to reference 'jj root succeeds' or 'a jj repository is detected' rather than '.jj/ exists in the repo root', aligning with the command-based detection approach used in vcs-detection-preamble.md.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: worktree-remove.sh double-removes _ws_list_err temp file on success path
description:
In worktree-remove.sh, the temp file tracked by _ws_list_err is explicitly rm -f'd on lines 135 (workspace list failure path) and 138 (workspace list success path). Then on line 170, the near-final explicit cleanup does rm -f again if the variable is non-empty. Additionally, the EXIT trap (line 67) also attempts to rm -f the same file if the variable is non-empty.

This means the file is removed up to three times (inline rm, near-final cleanup, EXIT trap) on the normal success path. rm -f on a non-existent file is safe and exits 0, so this is functionally harmless, but the redundancy adds cognitive overhead when reading the cleanup logic.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 135, 138, 170 and the EXIT trap at line 67.

Suggested fix: After the inline rm -f calls on lines 135 and 138, clear the variable (_ws_list_err="") to prevent the near-final cleanup and EXIT trap from attempting a redundant removal. This makes the cleanup ownership unambiguous.

title: _jj_ws_list assigned but never read in worktree-remove.sh
description:
_jj_ws_list is assigned via command substitution on line 133 of worktree-remove.sh but is never read afterward. The variable capture is only used as a side effect (exit code check) for jj workspace list. This dead variable makes intent unclear.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 133

Current code:
  elif [[ -n "$_ws_list_err" ]] && ! _jj_ws_list=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list 2>"$_ws_list_err"); then

Suggested fix:
  elif [[ -n "$_ws_list_err" ]] && ! (cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list >/dev/null 2>"$_ws_list_err"); then

Using a subshell avoids the dead variable assignment and makes the health-check-only intent explicit.

title: validate_safe_name logs unsanitized label in empty-name error path
description:
In worktree-helpers.sh line 15, the empty-name error path echoes the label argument directly to stderr without sanitization: echo "ERROR: ${label} must not be empty". The non-empty invalid-name path at lines 20-22 correctly sanitizes both label and name via sanitize_for_output before logging.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, line 15.

In practice, all call sites pass hardcoded string literals for the label parameter ('worktree name', 'repository directory name'), so this is not currently exploitable. However, the inconsistency creates a trap for future callers who might pass dynamic values. The fix is to sanitize label before using it in the error message on line 15:
  safe_label=$(sanitize_for_output "$label")
  echo "ERROR: ${safe_label} must not be empty" >&2
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: jj workspace list output captured but never inspected in worktree-remove.sh
description:
In worktree-remove.sh line 133, the command substitution captures jj workspace list output into _jj_ws_list, but this variable is never read anywhere in the script. The list is used purely as a jj-availability check (failure = skip), but the captured output serves no purpose.

This is also accompanied by a redundant guard: the elif condition at line 133 includes [[ -n "$_ws_list_err" ]] which is always true at that point (the previous elif at line 131 already handled the empty case).

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 133.

Suggestion: Either remove the output capture (use jj workspace list > /dev/null 2>"$_ws_list_err" to discard stdout) or inspect the output to verify the workspace is actually registered before running forget. The latter would avoid a jj workspace forget call when the workspace is already gone, which is currently handled reactively via idempotency at lines 141-142.
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: VCS detection preamble lint test checks string presence only, not runtime invocation
description:
test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats verifies that all pr-review agent .md files contain the string 'vcs-detection-preamble'. This is a grep-for-filename check — it confirms the reference is present in the file but does not verify that the preamble is actually included/executed correctly by the agent.

An agent could satisfy the lint test by mentioning 'vcs-detection-preamble' in a comment or dead code path while never loading the preamble. The behavioral eval B-FW1 partially covers this for fix-worker, but:
- No behavioral eval directly tests that each of the 11 non-gate agents actually executes the preamble on startup
- The lint test gives a false sense of completeness: it asserts textual presence, not functional inclusion

This is a suggestion-level gap: the evals provide good behavioral coverage of the VCS detection outcome, but the lint test's semantics could be documented more clearly (or supplemented with a note that textual presence is necessary but not sufficient for runtime correctness).

Alternatively: expand evals.json with a behavioral eval for each agent class (code-reviewer, error-handler, etc.) verifying that VCS: jj vs VCS: git is correctly reported based on repo context.

Test file: .claude/hooks/tests/test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats
close_reason: Fixed: grep pre-check added, list-failure attempts forget, mktemp propagates failure, SKILL.md error handling added, tests/evals updated

title: worktree-remove.sh: rm -f of empty _ws_list_err string lacks guard present in EXIT trap
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, near the end of the script there is:

rm -f "$_ws_list_err"

This runs without a guard, but the EXIT trap established earlier uses the guarded form:
[[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err"

When mktemp fails (line 137: _ws_list_err=$(mktemp 2>/dev/null) || true), _ws_list_err remains the empty string (set at line 75 to ""). Calling rm -f "" with an empty argument is a no-op on macOS (returns 0), but this is inconsistent with the trap pattern established 4 lines earlier and could fail silently on other platforms/configurations.

The fix is to add the same guard: [[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err"

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, near-final cleanup block.

title: No behavioral eval for VCS preamble step 2 git-path branch check (non-worktree/* branch rejection)
description:
pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md Step 2 contains a git-path worktree location check: if the current branch is not `worktree/*`, agent must report STATUS: FAILED and stop.

B-VCS1 covers this check for jj (path-based: `*_worktrees/*`), but there is no eval covering the analogous git-path check (branch-based: `worktree/*`). An agent running on, say, `main` branch should report STATUS: FAILED rather than modifying production code.

Suggested eval:
```json
{
  "id": "B-VCS-GIT1",
  "type": "behavioral",
  "skill": "address-findings",
  "prompt": "Fix finding fzymgc-house-skills-abc.1 in this file",
  "context": "Agent is in a git repo (no .jj/). Current branch is 'main', not 'worktree/*'.",
  "expected_output": "Agent reports STATUS: FAILED because it is not on a worktree/* branch",
  "assertions": [
    {"name": "reports-failed-status", "type": "contains", "check": "STATUS: FAILED"},
    {"name": "no-file-edits", "type": "not_contains", "check": "Edit"}
  ]
}
```

Criticality: 3/10. Infrastructure safety check — the branch naming convention is enforced by worktree-create.sh (which tests cover), so this scenario should not arise in normal operation. The coverage gap is in the defense-in-depth layer.

title: No behavioral eval for jj-not-installed prerequisite check in jj-init.md step 1
description:
jj/commands/jj-init.md step 1 now includes a prerequisite check: `command -v jj` before proceeding. If jj is not installed, the command should tell the user to install it and stop.

This new behavior path (oy7.19 fix) has no corresponding behavioral eval in jj/evals/evals.json. The existing INIT1-INIT4 evals cover: already initialized, happy path, no git repo, and jj-init-failure — but none cover the jj-binary-absent case.

Suggested eval:
```json
{
  "id": "JJ-B-INIT5",
  "type": "behavioral",
  "skill": "jj-init",
  "prompt": "/jj-init",
  "context": "jj binary is not installed (command -v jj returns non-zero)",
  "expected_output": "Should detect missing jj, tell user to install it with URL, and stop without running any further steps",
  "assertions": [
    {"name": "surfaces-install-url", "description": "References jj install docs or suggests installation", "type": "contains", "check": "install"},
    {"name": "stops-early", "description": "Does not proceed to git rev-parse or jj git init", "type": "not_contains", "check": "jj git init"}
  ]
}
```

Criticality: 3/10. User-facing UX gap only — the agent would still produce an error when `jj git init` fails, just with a less helpful message.

title: check-skills.yml bats job: bats downloaded without pinned GitHub Actions step
description: In .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, the bats job installs bats via wget from GitHub Releases with a sha256sum check (good). However, the bats install.sh is then executed with 'sudo bats-core-1.11.0/install.sh /usr/local' — this script is downloaded from GitHub without verification of the install.sh content itself (only the tarball SHA is verified, and the install.sh comes from inside the tarball). The tarball is verified, so the install.sh is implicitly verified — this is correct. This is a suggestion, not a bug: consider using 'npm install -g bats' or a dedicated action for simpler maintenance. The wget+sha256sum pattern is correct but verbose compared to alternatives. Not a blocking issue.

title: worktree-remove.sh: rm -f called with empty string on git-only path
description: In /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills_worktrees/agent-a011e2c6/.claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 168, 'rm -f "$_ws_list_err"' is called unconditionally. On git-only repos (no .jj/), the jj branch at line 123 is never entered, so _ws_list_err retains its initial empty-string value (line 66). Calling 'rm -f ""' passes an empty argument to rm. Most GNU coreutils rm implementations silently return 0, but POSIX behavior for 'rm -f ""' is implementation-defined and some systems emit a warning. The EXIT trap at line 67 guards this correctly via '[[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]]', but the unconditional rm at line 168 does not. Fix: guard line 168 with 'if [[ -n "$_ws_list_err" ]]; then rm -f "$_ws_list_err"; fi', matching the EXIT trap pattern.

title: jj evals lack behavioral coverage for op-log at-minus semantics (@- vs @)
description:
The jj skill CLAUDE.md documents the @ vs @- semantic (@ is the working-copy empty commit; @- is the parent with meaningful committed state). The evals.json has 21 entries covering triggering, behavioral, and negative cases, but none test that agents correctly use @- (not @) when verifying committed state after jj commit or jj undo.

The existing JJ-B-UNDO1 eval covers the 'do not undo twice' gotcha but not the @/@ - distinction, which is the other documented jj gotcha in CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md.

Suggested eval (add to jj/evals/evals.json):
{
  "id": "JJ-B-AT1",
  "type": "behavioral",
  "skill": "jujutsu",
  "prompt": "verify that my last commit has the right content",
  "context": "In a jj repo; user just ran jj commit",
  "expected_output": "Should use @- to inspect the committed parent, not @ which is the empty working-copy commit",
  "assertions": [
    {"name": "uses-at-minus", "type": "contains", "check": "@-"},
    {"name": "no-bare-at-for-commit", "type": "manual", "check": "Verify @ is not used to reference the committed state"}
  ]
}

title: address-findings: no FAILED path when VCS absent and worktree gone and no WORKTREE_BRANCH/CHANGE_ID
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4 step 5 (VCS inference when VCS field is missing from fix-worker response), the fallback path says:

  'If worktree path already removed, use WORKTREE_BRANCH or CHANGE_ID directly.'

But if a fix-worker hard-failed (crashed, was killed, or hit an error before reporting), it may omit VCS, WORKTREE_BRANCH, AND CHANGE_ID from its output. The skill has no explicit guard for this combined failure case. The instruction to 'use WORKTREE_BRANCH or CHANGE_ID directly' assumes at least one of those fields is present.

Location: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4, step 5, sub-step 0 (verify worktree path).

Suggested fix: Add explicit instruction: 'If WORKTREE_BRANCH and CHANGE_ID are both absent, mark FAILED immediately — the fix-worker result is unrecoverable. Clean up the worktree directory if it still exists. Add a bead comment noting the missing fields.'

title: No bats test covers validate_safe_name control-character injection in the label argument
description:
validate_safe_name passes the label argument directly into an error message via sanitize_for_output. The existing tests exercise valid and invalid names but always pass a clean label string like 'worktree name'. There is no test verifying that a label containing control characters (e.g., label with embedded ESC/BEL) is sanitized before appearing in stderr output.

This is a suggestion-level gap — the sanitize_for_output function itself is well tested, but the compose path (label embedded in error string) is not exercised.

File: /Volumes/Code/github.com/fzymgc-house/fzymgc-house-skills/.claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh:22

Suggested test: call validate_safe_name 'evil' $(printf 'lab\x01el') and verify the error message does not contain the raw control character.

title: Behavioral evals lack assertions for jj workspace add --name flag format regression
description:
jj/evals/evals.json has behavioral eval JJ-B6 for workspace name collision handling, but no behavioral eval tests the worktree creation convention — specifically that the jj workspace name uses the 'worktree-{NAME}' prefix format (matching the format used and verified in shell tests).

The behavioral evals focus on skill-level behavior (jj st vs git status, jj commit vs git commit) but do not include an eval that verifies the worktree naming convention is documented or followed in the jujutsu skill itself. If the SKILL.md guidance drifts from the hook implementation (which enforces 'worktree-{NAME}' via --name flag), no eval would catch it.

This is a suggestion since the naming convention is enforced at the shell layer (well-tested), but a behavioral eval documenting 'worktree workspace names use worktree-{NAME} prefix' would link the skill-level guidance to the hook implementation.

title: No test for worktree-remove.sh when detect_repo_root fails but path lacks _worktrees suffix in jj repo
description:
worktree-remove.sh has a specific branch (lines 3122-3126) that fires when detect_repo_root fails AND the inferred parent directory does NOT end in _worktrees — the script refuses removal and prints 'refusing removal for safety'. This path is covered by a git-mode test ('errors when parent dir has no _worktrees suffix') but there is no test for the pure-jj-repo variant where jj root also fails AND the parent lacks the suffix.

The existing test 'errors when parent dir has no _worktrees suffix' (line 2450-2461) runs from a non-git directory so detect_repo_root fails via git, but it exercises the inference path. There is no equivalent test where a jj mock is present, jj root fails, and the parent suffix check also fails. The combined failure path (jj installed, jj root fails, bad parent name) is untested.

This is a suggestion-level gap; the existing test covers the logic structurally, but the jj variant adds confidence against VCS-specific regressions.

title: VCS detection preamble tests in test_worktree_helpers.bats test wrong abstraction level
description:
test_worktree_helpers.bats contains four tests for 'VCS detection preamble' (lines 1974-2036) that test an inline bash snippet, not the actual vcs-detection-preamble.md reference file content. These tests verify a hardcoded one-liner inside the test itself rather than sourcing or extracting the real preamble from pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md.

If the preamble reference file is updated (e.g., the jj detection command changes), these tests will continue to pass against the stale inline copy. This is fragile — the tests do not catch drift between the reference file and the tested snippet.

Suggested fix: either extract the shell fragment from vcs-detection-preamble.md in the test setup (similar to how test_jj_init.bats extracts GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT), or scope the tests to the actual file. The current tests still provide value for the preamble's logic, but their isolation from the real reference file is a maintenance hazard.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Deferred per user decision. Created follow-up bead fzymgc-house-skills-5ax.

title: jj workspace forget failure in worktree-remove.sh does not prevent directory removal
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (diff lines 3183-3186), when jj workspace forget fails, jj_forget_failed is set to true and an ERROR is logged, but execution continues unconditionally to the 'rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH"' block (line 3200). This means the directory is always removed even when jj workspace metadata could not be cleaned up. The intent (per the comment at lines 3198-3199) is that rm -rf is always safe, but removing the directory without deregistering the jj workspace leaves orphaned metadata in the main repo's op log. The script exits with code 1 at line 3210 ('if $jj_forget_failed; then exit 1; fi'), which is correct, but the error message at line 3184 includes a manual recovery command. This behavior is documented by intent but a user who sees exit code 1 without reading stderr may not realize the workspace metadata is leaked. Recommendation: add a comment above the rm -rf block explicitly noting that directory removal proceeds even after forget failure to avoid compounding the problem, and ensure the ERROR message text matches (it does, but add context: 'workspace directory will still be removed').

title: cleanup_on_error in worktree-create.sh always exits with original code even when cleanup itself fails
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, the cleanup_on_error EXIT handler (diff lines 2850-2901) captures the original exit code in _exit_code, sets CLEANUP_FAILED=true on VCS cleanup failures, and conditionally overrides _exit_code to 1 when CLEANUP_FAILED is true AND _exit_code was 0. However, the handler exits with _exit_code at line 2900 regardless of whether directory removal (rm -rf, line 2893) or cleanup_empty_parent (line 2896) failed. If rm_err from rm -rf is non-empty (line 2893-2895), the WARNING is printed but CLEANUP_FAILED is never set — so the handler may exit 0 even though the worktree directory could not be removed, leaving orphaned files on disk. This is a suggestion-level issue since the WARNING is emitted and the original failure (non-zero exit from the main body) would still propagate in most cases, but if the main body exits 0 (which shouldn't happen given the trap is removed at line 2934 on success) this could silently succeed with orphaned state. Fix: also set CLEANUP_FAILED=true when the rm -rf fails, mirroring the pattern used for VCS cleanup failures.

title: Fix(oy7.30): add subdirectory CWD test for worktree creation
description: Add test exercising worktree creation from a subdirectory of the repo root (e.g. src/) to confirm detect_repo_root and jj workspace add succeed when not invoked from repo root.

title: Fix(oy7.28): add VCS preamble lint test for pr-review skills
description: Add lint test loop over pr-review/skills/*/SKILL.md to verify VCS detection preamble reference, matching the existing agent lint test.

title: Fix(oy7.27): add tests for unrecognized/unknown old-jj regex patterns
description: Add two additional test cases exercising the 'unrecognized.*--name' and 'unknown.*--name' regex patterns in old jj detection.

title: Fix(oy7.36): add error message assertion to jj mkdir-fail test
description: Add assertion for 'failed to create worktree parent directory' in the jj path mkdir-fail test to match the git path test.

title: Fix(oy7.33): sanitize path in cleanup_empty_parent WARNING
description: Add sanitize_for_output to the ls failure WARNING at line 35 in worktree-helpers.sh for consistency.

title: Fix(oy7.32): guard EXIT trap rm against empty _ws_list_err
description: Guard rm -f in EXIT trap with non-empty check so rm -f '' is avoided on git path.

title: Fix(oy7.35): remove unused Bash(test *) from jujutsu SKILL.md allowed-tools
description: Remove 'Bash(test *)' from jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md allowed-tools since the skill body never uses the test command.

title: Fix(oy7.26): fix misleading error message when mktemp fails in detect_repo_root
description: In worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root(), when mktemp fails and jj_attempted is reset to false, the error path incorrectly claims 'jj not in PATH'. Fix the error message to accurately describe the mktemp failure scenario.

title: Fix(oy7.31): add _ws_list_err cleanup on success path in worktree-remove.sh
description: Add rm -f "$_ws_list_err" at line 158 alongside existing _root_err_file cleanup, so the temp file is cleaned on the success path (before trap - EXIT cancels the EXIT trap).

title: Add BATS test for jj-init step 3 failure propagation
description: jj-init.md step 3 (jj git init --colocate) failure is only covered by behavioral eval JJ-B-INIT4, not a CI-enforced BATS test. This is by design since the command is a markdown instruction set for an agent, but a future BATS test could exercise the gitignore fragment behavior when init fails. Deferred from PR #31 review finding oy7.29.
close_reason: Won't fix — BATS tests replaced by Python pytest suite

title: jj mkdir-fail test missing error message assertion (test coverage gap)
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, the test 'jj path: fails gracefully when mkdir -p fails' (around line 679) only checks that status equals 1, but does NOT check for the expected error message 'failed to create worktree parent directory'.

The equivalent git path test ('git path: fails gracefully when mkdir -p fails') correctly asserts: [[ "$output" == *"failed to create worktree parent directory"* ]]

The jj variant is asymmetric — it tests the exit code but not the diagnostic output. A regression that changed the error message would go undetected.

Fix: Add the error message assertion to the jj mkdir-fail test to match the git path test's coverage:
[[ "$output" == *"failed to create worktree parent directory"* ]]

title: Bash(test *) in jujutsu SKILL.md allowed-tools is unused (YAGNI)
description:
jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md declares 'Bash(test *)' in its allowed-tools frontmatter (line 34), but the skill body never uses the test command. The detection section uses 'jj root >/dev/null 2>&1' not 'test -d .jj'. No code snippet or instruction in the skill requires Bash(test *).

This is a YAGNI violation per CLAUDE.md project standards. Including unused tool permissions is potentially a security surface expansion.

Compare with jj/commands/jj-init.md where 'Bash(test *)' IS used (step 2: 'Run test -d .jj').

Fix: Remove 'Bash(test *)' from jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md allowed-tools.

title: cleanup_empty_parent emits unsanitized path in WARNING on ls failure
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, the cleanup_empty_parent function at line 35 emits a WARNING without sanitizing the path:

  echo "WARNING: ls failed on parent directory '$parent' — skipping rmdir" >&2

The path $parent is passed raw, without sanitize_for_output. By contrast, the WARNING at line 39 correctly uses sanitize_for_output("$parent"). This inconsistency means a parent directory path containing C0 control characters or ANSI escape sequences (e.g. from a maliciously crafted worktree path that bypassed validation) could inject terminal control sequences into the warning output.

In practice this path is called with WORKTREE_PARENT (which comes from a validated REPO_NAME), so exploitation is very unlikely. But the inconsistency with the rest of the codebase (which carefully sanitizes all output) is worth fixing for defence-in-depth.

Affected file: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, line 35.

Fix: Change to:
  echo "WARNING: ls failed on parent directory '$(sanitize_for_output "$parent")' — skipping rmdir" >&2

title: EXIT trap passes empty string to rm -f when _ws_list_err is unset (git path)
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the EXIT trap at line 62 is:
  trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}" "${_ws_list_err:-}"' EXIT

When the script takes the git path (no .jj/ present), _ws_list_err remains an empty string (initialized at line 61: _ws_list_err=""). The EXIT trap fires on error exits (before 'trap - EXIT' at line 159 cancels it on success). With _ws_list_err empty, the trap expands to: rm -f "" "", which passes literal empty-string arguments to rm. On Linux, 'rm -f ""' exits with status 1 and emits 'cannot remove' to stderr — this could produce confusing output during cleanup of an already-failed operation, or mask the original error with a secondary rm failure.

Affected file: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 62.

Fix: Guard the rm with a non-empty check, e.g.:
  trap '[[ -n "${_root_err_file:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_root_err_file"; [[ -n "${_ws_list_err:-}" ]] && rm -f "$_ws_list_err"' EXIT
Or initialize _ws_list_err to a real temp file (with error handling) at the start alongside _root_err_file.

title: no test for jj workspace add from inside a worktree subdirectory (non-root CWD)
description: All test_worktree_create.bats tests run with CWD set to REPO_ROOT (the git/jj root). In production, the WorktreeCreate hook fires with the CWD set to wherever the Claude agent is operating, which could be a subdirectory. The worktree-create.sh calls 'cd $REPO_ROOT && jj workspace add' explicitly, so it is designed to handle this, but there is no test exercising 'CWD is a subdirectory of the repo root' for either the jj or git path. A test starting from a subdirectory (e.g. REPO_ROOT/src/) would confirm that detect_repo_root and the subsequent jj workspace add still succeed when not invoked from the repo root.

title: no BATS test for jj-init.md step 3 (jj git init --colocate) failure propagation
description: test_jj_init.bats explicitly scopes to the GITIGNORE_FRAGMENT (step 4 of jj-init.md) and notes that steps 1-3 are covered by behavioral evals JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4. However, the jj-init.md command is a markdown instruction set for an agent — the evals are not automatically run in CI (they are in jj/evals/evals.json, schema-validated but not executed). The behavioral eval JJ-B-INIT4 covers the 'jj git init fails' path, but only as a manual/behavioral check, not a programmatic assertion in CI. The BATS tests have a comment acknowledging this scope limitation, which is fine as a design choice, but reviewers should be aware that the init error propagation is covered only by manual evals — not by any CI-enforced test.

title: lint test enforces VCS preamble in agents but not pr-review skills
description: test_pr_review_agents_lint.bats verifies that all pr-review agents (except review-gate) reference the VCS detection preamble. However, all three pr-review skills (address-findings, respond-to-comments, review-pr) also reference the preamble and could have it accidentally removed. There is no analogous lint test enforcing that the pr-review skills maintain this reference. A similar check loop over pr-review/skills/*/SKILL.md would prevent silent regression if a skill is refactored and the preamble reference is dropped.

title: old-jj detection regex patterns 'unrecognized' and 'unknown' are untested
description: worktree-create.sh line 98 uses a three-pattern regex to detect old jj versions that don't support --name: 'unexpected argument.*--name|unrecognized.*--name|unknown.*--name'. The existing test (test_worktree_create.bats 'jj path: rejects old jj without --name support') only exercises the first pattern via create_old_jj_mock which emits 'error: unexpected argument --name found'. The 'unrecognized.*--name' and 'unknown.*--name' branches are never exercised. If a future jj version changes its error text to match one of the other patterns (or if the regex is refactored), the untested branches could regress silently. Two additional mocks emitting those error strings would provide full coverage of the regex alternatives.

title: Fix(oy7.5): test mktemp failure path in worktree-remove
description: Add BATS test for worktree-remove.sh mktemp failure at _root_err_file creation

title: Fix(oy7.2): isolate BATS test temp dirs for parallel safety
description: Ensure BATS tests use unique temp dirs per test to prevent interference under --jobs 2

title: Fix(oy7.10): add _ws_list_err to EXIT trap
description: Update EXIT trap in worktree-remove.sh to also clean up _ws_list_err

title: Fix(oy7.6): convert automatable manual evals
description: Review behavioral evals and convert deterministic manual assertions to automated checks

title: Fix(oy7.4): lint check for VCS preamble in agents
description: Add BATS test verifying all 12 pr-review agent .md files reference vcs-detection-preamble

title: Fix(oy7.1): test detect_repo_root mktemp failure
description: Add BATS test for detect_repo_root when mktemp fails - should emit WARNING and fall through to git

title: Fix(oy7.9): handle mktemp failure in worktree-remove workspace list
description: Fix worktree-remove.sh to explicitly handle mktemp failure before jj workspace list check

title: Fix(oy7.14): add jj binary check to jj-init command
description: Add command -v jj check to jj-init.md step 1

title: Fix(oy7.8): reorder trap/exit in worktree-remove jj_forget_failed path
description: Move trap - EXIT before the jj_forget_failed exit check so EXIT trap doesnt fire after manual cleanup

title: Fix(oy7.3): test bare workspace name in worktree-remove
description: Add BATS test for worktree-remove.sh when jj workspace has bare name without worktree- prefix

title: Fix(oy7.7): guard mktemp failure in detect_repo_root
description: Fix detect_repo_root to report mktemp failure clearly instead of misleading jj root error

title: jj-init: no check for jj binary being installed before running jj git init
description:
In jj/commands/jj-init.md, step 1 checks that the current directory is a git repo using git rev-parse, but there is no explicit check that jj is installed before step 3 runs 'jj git init --colocate'. If jj is not in PATH, the failure message would be a generic 'command not found' error from the shell, which may confuse users who don't know they need to install jj first.

File: jj/commands/jj-init.md step 1

Suggested fix: Add a jj availability check to step 1, e.g.:
  'Run command -v jj to confirm jj is installed. If not found, tell the user to install jj (https://jj-vcs.github.io/jj/latest/install-and-setup/) and stop.'

title: evals.json: eval count in PR description does not match actual file contents
description:
The PR summary states 'Evals with 5 triggering + 5 behavioral tests' for jj/evals/evals.json. The actual file contains 9 triggering evals (JJ-T1 through JJ-T9 plus JJ-T-NEG2) and approximately 15 behavioral evals (JJ-B1 through JJ-B10, JJ-B-NEG1, JJ-B-UNDO1, JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4). The PR description is significantly understating the eval count.

File: jj/evals/evals.json

This is a documentation accuracy issue in the PR description, not a code bug. The eval file itself is correct. Low priority cleanup.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Fixed: updated PR description from '5 triggering + 5 behavioral tests' to '7 triggering + 3 negative + 16 behavioral tests (26 total)'

title: worktree-remove.sh: trap - EXIT unreachable on jj_forget_failed exit path
description: Closing as duplicate of fzymgc-house-skills-oy7.8 (same finding, different aspect reviewer)

title: worktree-remove.sh: _ws_list_err temp file not added to EXIT trap, leaks on unexpected exit
description:
In worktree-remove.sh, the _root_err_file temp file is registered with the EXIT trap (line 61), but _ws_list_err (created at line 121 inside the jj workspace list elif) is NOT registered in the trap. If an unexpected exit occurs after _ws_list_err is created but before the rm -f calls at lines 123/126/129, the temp file leaks in /tmp.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 121

Fix: Update the EXIT trap handler to also rm -f the _ws_list_err file if set, or track it in the same pattern as _root_err_file. Example: trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}" "${_ws_list_err:-}"' EXIT, set _ws_list_err before creating the file.

title: worktree-remove: mktemp failure in jj workspace list check produces misleading diagnostic
description:
In worktree-remove.sh line 121, the condition is:
  elif _ws_list_err=$(mktemp) && \! _jj_ws_list=$(cd ... && jj workspace list 2>"$_ws_list_err"); then

If mktemp fails: _ws_list_err is set to empty string, the && short-circuits (mktemp failure = false), making the entire elif false. Execution falls through to line 125's elif, where $_jj_ws_list is empty (never set). The grep then fails to find the workspace, producing the misleading warning 'workspace not found in jj workspace list — skipping forget'. The actual cause (mktemp failure) is never surfaced.

Additionally, rm -f "$_ws_list_err" in the line 126 branch would execute rm -f "" (empty string), which is harmless on Linux/macOS but unexpected.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 121

Fix: Pre-create the temp file before the elif chain with explicit error handling:
  _ws_list_err=$(mktemp) || { echo 'WARNING: mktemp failed — skipping jj workspace list check' >&2; }
  if [[ -n "$_ws_list_err" ]] && \! _jj_ws_list=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list 2>"$_ws_list_err"); then

title: worktree-remove: jj_forget_failed exit runs EXIT trap after manual cleanup
description:
In worktree-remove.sh lines 154-156, the ordering is:
  rm -f "$_root_err_file"        # line 154: manually delete temp file
  if $jj_forget_failed; then exit 1; fi  # line 155: exit BEFORE trap - EXIT
  trap - EXIT                      # line 156: clear trap (never reached on failure)

When jj_forget_failed is true, exit 1 at line 155 fires the EXIT trap from line 61 ('rm -f ${_root_err_file:-}'). The trap runs rm -f on the already-deleted file (harmless, but misleading). The trap should be cleared BEFORE the conditional exit, or the conditional exit should be moved after trap - EXIT.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 154-156

Fix: Reorder to clear trap before the conditional exit:
  rm -f "$_root_err_file"
  trap - EXIT
  if $jj_forget_failed; then exit 1; fi

title: jj-init behavioral evals rely heavily on manual assertion type for critical correctness checks
description: Several behavioral evals in jj/evals/evals.json use 'type: manual' for critical assertions that cannot be automated today (JJ-B1 'does-not-use-git-status', JJ-B2 'no-staging', JJ-B4 'warns-interactive', JJ-B5 'warns-no-git-mutate', JJ-B-NEG1 'no-warning-for-git-log', JJ-B6 'handles-collision'). This is acceptable for behavioral evals which are manually-run rather than CI-gated. However, JJ-B-INIT4 checks that '.gitignore' does NOT appear in output after a jj init error — this uses 'type: not_contains' with check '.gitignore', but the assertion only checks the final agent output text rather than filesystem state. An agent could fail to update .gitignore (correct) but still mention .gitignore in its response (false negative). This is a minor eval quality issue. Criticality: 3/10 — affects eval reliability, not production code.

title: worktree-remove.sh EXIT trap leaks _root_err_file on early-exit paths before mktemp cleanup is registered
description: In worktree-remove.sh, the EXIT trap 'trap rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}" EXIT' is registered AFTER the WORKTREE_PATH empty-check and directory-existence check. If WORKTREE_PATH is empty or the directory does not exist, the script exits 0 before the trap is set — no temp file was created, so no leak. However, if the path canonicalization (realpath or cd/pwd) exits early with error, that also happens before trap setup... actually examining the code more carefully: _root_err_file is created by mktemp at line 2822, then the trap is set on line 2823. The trap IS set before detect_repo_root is called. The early-return paths (empty WORKTREE_PATH, nonexistent dir) happen before mktemp, so there's no leak there. The actual untested scenario is: what happens if mktemp itself fails at line 2822? The inline error handler ('echo ERROR: mktemp failed; exit 1') fires without the trap being set. Since the file was never created, there is no leak — but no test verifies this error path. Criticality: 3/10 — mktemp failure is rare and behavior is correct (no leak), but the error path is not tested.

title: No BATS tests cover VCS detection preamble integration into actual pr-review agent startup
description: The VCS detection preamble (pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md) has been added to all 12 pr-review agents and 3 skills. The test_worktree_helpers.bats file contains 4 tests ('VCS detection preamble: outputs jj/git/none/jj-wins') that verify the abstract shell logic of 'if jj root succeeds...' detection. However, these tests copy-paste the detection snippet inline rather than testing that it is correctly embedded in the agent files. No test verifies that the agents actually contain the expected VCS detection preamble reference, nor that the preamble's instructions match what the helpers tests exercise. A regression where an agent file loses the preamble reference would go undetected. A grep-based test checking that each agent .md file references the vcs-detection-preamble would catch this. Criticality: 4/10 — the behavioral impact would be agents silently using git commands in jj repos.

title: BATS tests for worktree hooks run serially (--jobs 2) but parallel tests may share REPO_ROOT env
description: CI runs bats with --jobs 2 (check-skills.yml line 2989: 'bats --jobs 2 .claude/hooks/tests/'). The test setup in test_worktree_create.bats and test_worktree_remove.bats uses REPO_ROOT=$(mktemp -d), which is per-test and safe. However, several tests create directories relative to the bats test directory itself (e.g. line 844: 'mktemp "$BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../worktree-create-test-XXXXXX.sh"'). Multiple parallel tests creating patched scripts in the same parent directory could theoretically interfere if cleanup fails between runs. The tests also share /tmp namespace. No test teardown verifies parallel safety. This is low severity (--jobs 2 is modest concurrency), but the risk increases if parallelism is increased. Criticality: 3/10 — currently --jobs 2 with isolated REPO_ROOT prevents most races, but worth documenting the assumption.

title: No test for detect_repo_root mktemp failure branch in worktree-helpers.sh
description: In worktree-helpers.sh detect_repo_root, there is a branch at the mktemp call: _jj_err_file=$(mktemp) || { jj_rc=1; }. When mktemp fails, jj_rc is set to 1 and the function proceeds with an empty err file path, eventually reaching the error output path. This branch has no BATS test in test_worktree_helpers.bats. While mktemp failure is rare, the error path diverges from the normal jj-fails path (no temp file cleanup, jj_err will be empty). A test would mock mktemp to fail and verify the function returns non-zero with an appropriate error message. Criticality: 3/10 — mktemp failure is rare and the worst-case outcome is a slightly degraded error message, not silent failure.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Comprehensive review of PR #31 adding jj VCS support: new jj plugin, VCS detection preamble in pr-review agents/skills, worktree hooks, fix-worker VCS awareness, evals, and release config updates. Aspects: code, tests, errors.

title: Fix(ff0.3-test): add test for cleanup_empty_parent when jj_forget_failed
description: Add test verifying cleanup_empty_parent runs even when jj workspace forget fails

title: Fix(ff0.3): move cleanup_empty_parent before jj_forget_failed exit check
description: Reorder lines so cleanup_empty_parent runs before the jj_forget_failed exit 1 check

title: Fix(ff0.9): capture jj workspace list stderr in worktree-remove.sh
description: Replace 2>/dev/null with stderr capture and include in warning message

title: Fix(ff0.5): strengthen JJ-B-UNDO1 assertion check string
description: Replace weak 'once' contains check with 'op log' in JJ-B-UNDO1 eval

title: Fix(ff0.4): add git-path collision test and extend jj preexist test
description: Add tests for cleanup_on_error behavior with pre-existing worktree directories

title: Fix(ff0.1): fix exit status assertion in jj workspace forget failure test
description: Change assertion from status -eq 0 to status -eq 1 in test_worktree_remove.bats line 190

title: Fix(ff0.7): add rm -f before trap - EXIT in worktree-remove.sh
description: Add explicit rm -f _root_err_file before trap - EXIT at line 150 to prevent temp file leak on success path

title: Fix(ff0.8): replace test -d .jj with jj root detection in AGENTS.md
description: Replace test -d .jj && VCS=jj || VCS=git with proper jj root detection in AGENTS.md

title: jj workspace list stderr suppressed in worktree-remove.sh makes failure diagnosis impossible
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 121: 'elif \! _jj_ws_list=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list 2>/dev/null); then'. The 2>/dev/null suppresses all stderr from 'jj workspace list'. When it fails, the WARNING at line 122 is emitted but contains no diagnostic information about why the command failed. This is inconsistent with the rest of the file which captures stderr into variables (e.g. jj_err=$(... 2>&1)) and includes it in error messages. Fix: capture stderr separately and include it in the warning, similar to how other jj command failures are handled: if \! _jj_ws_list=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list 2>"_ws_list_err_file"); then echo "WARNING: jj workspace list failed ...: $(cat _ws_list_err_file)"

title: JJ-B-INIT evals missing 'skill' field for behavioral type
description:
Four behavioral evals in jj/evals/evals.json — JJ-B-INIT1, JJ-B-INIT2, JJ-B-INIT3, JJ-B-INIT4 — have type: "behavioral" but omit the optional "skill" field. The schema permits this omission, but the skill field communicates which skill or command is under test.

These evals test the /jj-init slash command behavior. Without the "skill" field, tooling or humans reading the eval file cannot determine what is being evaluated without reading the "expected_output" field.

Suggested fix: add "skill": "jj-init" to JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4, matching the pattern used by other behavioral evals in the same file (e.g. JJ-B1 has "skill": "jujutsu").

File: jj/evals/evals.json, eval ids JJ-B-INIT1..JJ-B-INIT4

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4 already have "skill": "jj-init" field at lines 297, 313, 335, 351 of jj/evals/evals.json.

title: JJ-B-UNDO1 eval: 'once' assertion too weak to be meaningful
description:
The behavioral eval JJ-B-UNDO1 in jj/evals/evals.json has an automated assertion:
  {"name": "explains-op-log", "type": "contains", "check": "once"}

The string "once" is too generic to be a meaningful signal. It will match any output containing phrases like "run once", "only once", "at once", "once you", etc. — none of which specifically indicate the agent explained that failed jj ops leave no op-log entry.

A stronger check would be something like "op log" or "not recorded" or "failed operation" that is more tightly scoped to the expected explanation.

File: jj/evals/evals.json, eval id JJ-B-UNDO1, assertion "explains-op-log"

title: worktree-remove.sh EXIT trap still active during jj_forget_failed exit path — correct but undocumented
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the EXIT trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}"' is set at line 61 and cleared at line 150 (trap - EXIT). When the script exits early via 'if $jj_forget_failed; then exit 1; fi' at line 145, the EXIT trap fires and deletes _root_err_file — this is correct behavior. However, line 148 'cleanup_empty_parent "$(dirname "$WORKTREE_PATH")"' is between line 145 and line 150, so when jj_forget_failed=true, cleanup_empty_parent is SKIPPED because exit 1 is called first. The parent _worktrees directory will not be cleaned up after a successful rm -rf of the worktree when jj workspace forget fails. This is a silent omission: the directory removal succeeds, the parent may be left empty, but the empty parent is not cleaned up. Fix: call cleanup_empty_parent before the jj_forget_failed exit check, or restructure as: cleanup_empty_parent ...; if $jj_forget_failed; then exit 1; fi; trap - EXIT.

title: detect_repo_root does not clean up temp file when mktemp succeeds but jj root returns non-existent directory
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, the detect_repo_root function (lines 58-68) creates a temp file via mktemp (line 59), captures jj stderr into it (line 61), then reads and removes it (lines 62-63). However, the rm -f on line 63 is inside 'if [[ $jj_rc -eq 0 ]]' block. When jj exits 0 but returns a non-existent directory path (the else branch at line 75-76), the cleanup at line 63 does execute (jj_rc is 0). But when jj exits non-zero (jj_rc \!= 0 from line 61), the block at lines 60-64 does NOT execute, which means rm -f _jj_err_file at line 63 is skipped. The temp file is leaked until the process exits. Since detect_repo_root is a function called from a long-running script, the temp file persists for the duration of the script. Low severity since the file is small and eventually cleaned up by the OS, but it violates the create-and-always-cleanup contract. Fix: add 'rm -f "$_jj_err_file"' after the outer if block (after line 68), or restructure so cleanup happens unconditionally via a trap set within the function.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — rm -f at line 63 of worktree-helpers.sh executes unconditionally inside the if block (entered when jj_rc=0 from mktemp success). Even when jj root fails at line 61, lines 62-63 still run sequentially within the block, so the temp file is always cleaned up.

title: worktree-remove.sh jj workspace list stderr merged into list output may break workspace-exists check
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 2771: _jj_ws_list=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list 2>&1). Stderr is merged into the output used for grep. If jj workspace list emits warnings to stderr (e.g., deprecation notices) alongside the workspace listing on stdout, the grep -qF 'worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:' check may match a warning line rather than a workspace entry, producing a false positive — or warnings could corrupt the output format enough to cause a false negative. Since jj workspace list exit code is already checked (the elif chain stops on non-zero exit), only the stdout is needed for the grep. Fix: capture stdout only (_jj_ws_list=$(cd "$REPO_ROOT" && jj workspace list 2>/dev/null)) and rely on the existing non-zero exit check for genuine command failures. This matches the pattern used for detect_repo_root's git rev-parse call which also uses 2>/dev/null.

title: worktree-remove.sh: explicit rm-f of temp file before trap-clear is redundant but harmless
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the script sets a trap for EXIT at line 2711:
  trap 'rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}"' EXIT

Then at the end (line 2801) it explicitly cleans up the file and clears the trap:
  rm -f "${_root_err_file:-}"
  trap - EXIT

However, the EXIT trap is only cleared AFTER the explicit rm -f. This means:
1. When the script exits 0 normally, the cleanup is fine (explicit rm -f + trap - EXIT).
2. When the script exits 1 via any of the intermediate 'exit 1' branches between lines 2723-2795, the EXIT trap fires and cleans up the temp file correctly.

The pattern is actually correct — but it's brittle. If an 'exit 1' is inserted between the 'rm -f' line and 'trap - EXIT', the trap would fire on an already-removed file (harmless since rm -f ignores missing files). More importantly, if 'trap - EXIT' were accidentally moved before 'rm -f', the temp file would be leaked.

This is low-severity but the explicit rm-f before trap-clear ordering is fragile. A cleaner pattern would be to just rely on the EXIT trap throughout and only clear it after (or not at all). The current code works correctly but is harder to reason about.

Suggested fix: Remove the explicit 'rm -f ${_root_err_file:-}' on line 2800 and just rely on the EXIT trap, or use 'trap - EXIT' first then rm -f to make intent clearer.

title: No behavioral eval for jj workspace add with pre-created worktree path
description: The bats test 'colocated jj+git: fails gracefully when worktree path already exists' (test_worktree_create.bats ~line 1245) correctly tests the worktree-create.sh code path. However, there is no eval covering the scenario where a jj agent (address-findings) tries to dispatch to a workspace that already exists — the WorktreeCreate hook would fail but the eval set (jj/evals/evals.json, pr-review/evals/evals.json) has no scenario for orchestrator behavior when WorktreeCreate returns non-zero due to a pre-existing path. This could cause a silent workflow stall. A B-WC-COLLISION eval for address-findings would close this gap. Priority is low because the hook failure would propagate as a tool error and the orchestrator should surface it, but explicit coverage would prevent regression.

title: FINDING field missing from vcs-equivalence.md fix-worker output contract table
description:
The fix-worker output contract table in pr-review/references/vcs-equivalence.md omits the FINDING field, but fix-worker.md includes it in both git and jj output format examples.

Location: pr-review/references/vcs-equivalence.md lines 7763-7770 (contract table) vs pr-review/agents/fix-worker.md output examples.

The table lists STATUS, VCS, FILES_CHANGED, DESCRIPTION, WORKTREE_BRANCH, CHANGE_ID -- but FINDING (the bead ID being fixed) is present in both output blocks in fix-worker.md. The address-findings orchestrator also references parsing FINDING from agent responses.

Suggested fix: Add FINDING to the contract table with Required=Always and Description='Bead ID of the finding being addressed'.

title: read-only .gitignore test uses bare assertions outside 'run'
description: In test_jj_init.bats, the 'gitignore: read-only .gitignore surfaces error' test (around line 626) restores permissions with 'chmod u+w .gitignore' before running the final assertion '\! grep -qF .jj/ .gitignore'. This bare grep is not wrapped in 'run', so if the assertion fails it throws a non-zero exit code that circumvents teardown and may leave the test directory undeletable or confuse subsequent tests. In BATS, assertions that can fail should either use 'run' + check $status, or be placed after teardown-safe cleanup. The chmod restore must happen unconditionally before assertions, which is already done, but the bare negated grep should be replaced with a BATS-native assertion pattern to ensure proper test isolation. File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats lines 626-634.
