title: address-findings Phase 4 VCS inference emits no error when VCS is none and .jj/ exists
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4 step 5 (VCS inference fallback), when VCS detection returns 'none' and the main repo contains .jj/, the instruction is: 'warn the user: orphaned jj workspace metadata may remain — run jj workspace forget <name> when jj is available.' This is a soft suggestion rather than a structured error, and it is delivered inline within a conditional instruction block that an agent may not reach if it short-circuits on STATUS: FAILED.

The guidance for the 'none' case correctly says to mark FAILED and skip integration, but the .jj/ warning is a secondary note that could easily be missed. If the agent marks FAILED and immediately returns, the orphaned jj workspace warning may never be surfaced to the user.

Suggested fix: Make the jj metadata leak warning part of the FAILED status report (include it in the bead comment added when marking FAILED), not just an inline parenthetical in the instruction text.

title: No test covers jj workspace add race: directory exists before hook runs (worktree path pre-created)
description:
worktree-create.sh creates WORKTREE_PARENT via mkdir -p, then calls jj workspace add with the WORKTREE_PATH. If the target directory already exists (e.g. a previous failed cleanup left it), jj workspace add may fail because jj refuses to add a workspace whose target directory is not empty, or may succeed with surprising results.

There is no test that pre-creates WORKTREE_PATH before calling worktree-create.sh in the jj code path to verify the script handles this correctly (either failing gracefully or succeeding idempotently).

The git path has the 'git path: cleanup_on_error skips git worktree remove when WORKSPACE_CREATED is false' test which covers an analogous scenario, but the jj path has no equivalent.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats — jj section

Suggested fix: add a test that pre-creates WORKTREE_PATH before invoking the hook in the jj path and asserts a clean failure with informative error output.

title: Behavioral evals for VCS detection preamble lack automated assertions for jj-specific agent output format
description:
Several key behavioral evals for the VCS detection preamble added to pr-review/evals/evals.json rely entirely on 'manual' assertion type with no automated counterpart:

- B-FW1 (fix-worker jj output): assertions 'uses-jj-commit' and 'no-git-commit' are manual-only
- B-VCS1 (default workspace rejection): 'no-file-operations' is manual-only  
- B-JJ3 (workspace forget ordering): 'forget-before-rm' is manual-only
- B-VCS3 (no jj undo for failed op): 'acknowledges-no-cleanup' is manual-only
- B-RG1 (review-gate jj diff parsing): 'no-false-positives' and 'identifies-added-lines' are manual-only

These are the highest-risk behaviors (agent using wrong VCS commands, or incorrect cleanup ordering). Manual-only assertions cannot be run in CI.

Suggested improvement: where possible, add 'not_contains' or 'contains' automated counterparts. For example B-FW1's 'no-git-commit' check can use type:'not_contains'. B-VCS1's 'no-file-operations' could use type:'not_contains' check:'Edit' to verify no tool calls were proposed.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: B-JJ3 forget-before-rm left manual-only intentionally: ordering assertions (A must appear before B) cannot be expressed with contains/not_contains. The finding says 'where possible' — 5 of 6 gaps now have automated counterparts.

title: No test covers jj workspace add succeeding but producing a directory with spaces in the workspace path
description:
worktree-create.sh validates the worktree NAME via validate_safe_name and rejects REPO_NAME with spaces, but there is no test confirming that the constructed WORKTREE_PATH (WORKTREE_PARENT/NAME) is safe when the overall path has unusual characters introduced by the environment (e.g. TMPDIR containing spaces on macOS).

The 'rejects repo directory name with spaces' test covers the REPO_NAME guard. However there is no symmetrical jj-path test: a jj path test for REPO_NAME-with-spaces exists only for the git path.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats — jj section lacks the repo-with-spaces test.

Suggested fix: add a test parallel to 'rejects repo directory name with spaces' under the '--- colocated jj+git repo tests ---' section, confirming the jj path also rejects a REPO_NAME containing spaces.

title: test_jj_init.bats: read-only .gitignore test exits 0 but does not assert .jj/ was not appended
description:
The 'gitignore: read-only .gitignore surfaces error' test in .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats correctly checks the warning message, but does not assert that .jj/ was NOT appended to the file after the chmod failure. If a future implementation attempts a fallback write that somehow bypasses the error path, the test would still pass.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats lines 626-633

The test only asserts:
  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  [[ "$output" == *"Could not update .gitignore"* ]]

Suggested addition: after chmod u+w restore, assert count of .jj/ entries is still 0 (only existing '# existing' line present), so the test would catch any silent partial-write.

title: check-skills.yml: bats installed via wget without HTTPS integrity check of the tarball URL itself
description:
File: .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, bats installation step (diff lines 2711-2717).

The bats install uses wget + sha256sum verification:

  wget -q https://github.com/bats-core/bats-core/archive/refs/tags/v1.11.0.tar.gz
  echo "aeff09...  v1.11.0.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c -
  tar xzf v1.11.0.tar.gz
  sudo bats-core-1.11.0/install.sh /usr/local

The sha256sum check (pinned hash: aeff09fdc8b0c88b3087c99de00cf549356d7a2f6a69e3fcec5e0e861d2f9063) is good practice and mitigates MITM attacks. However:

1. The bats installation step runs sudo install.sh from the downloaded tarball. If the sha256 check passes but the install.sh script has malicious content, it runs as root. The sha256 pin mitigates this — the hash is a known-good value.

2. The BATS tests run with 'bats .claude/hooks/tests/' (no --jobs parallelism flag). BATS 1.11.0 supports parallel execution via '--jobs N'. The sequential run may be slow in CI. This is a performance suggestion, not a bug.

3. The rumdl install now uses a pinned version (v0.1.43) instead of dynamically fetching the latest tag. The old approach ('gh release view --json tagName') would always use the latest rumdl which could break CI on new rumdl releases. The new pinned approach is better but requires manual version bumps.

4. The new lint step does NOT include jj/commands/*.md in the rumdl check — it does include jj/skills/*/SKILL.md and jj/commands/*.md (diff line 2746). Wait, line 2746 is '+            jj/commands/*.md'. This IS included. No issue.

The pinned sha256 for bats is good. The approach matches the security standard established for rumdl (now also pinned). 

Primary actionable finding: The sudo execution of install.sh from an untrusted tarball (even with sha256 verification) is a security risk if the hash in the workflow ever becomes stale (e.g., hash collision or accidental wrong hash). A preferable approach would be to use a GitHub Actions cache or install bats via apt/npm. However, given the sha256 pin is present and correct, this is a suggestion-level finding.

Suggested fix: Consider installing bats via npm ('npm install -g bats') or using the Ubuntu package ('sudo apt-get install -y bats') for CI reproducibility without manual hash management.
close_reason: Fix cherry-picked and review-gate PASS (raa.25: P2 suggestion, sha256 already present, comment+parallelism added)

title: jj-init.md: .gitignore append failure message surfaced but .jj/ init already succeeded — partial state not clearly signaled
description:
In jj/commands/jj-init.md, step 4 (lines 5380-5389 in diff) handles .gitignore append failure:

  if ! append_err=$(echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore 2>&1); then
    echo "Could not update .gitignore: $append_err"
  fi

The instruction says: 'If the append fails, capture and surface the error as: Could not update .gitignore: <error>. Please add .jj/ to .gitignore manually...'

This is a suggestion-level issue. The append failure message is surfaced, which is good. However:

1. The error message tells the user to add .jj/ manually but does not explicitly state that jj init ALREADY SUCCEEDED (step 3 ran jj git init --colocate). A user seeing only this error might not realize jj is already fully functional -- they may try to re-run /jj-init, which would fail at step 2 (test -d .jj already exists check).

2. The command '/jj-init' then proceeds to step 5 (verify with jj st and jj log) even when step 4 failed. Step 5 succeeds and reports 'Colocated jj repo initialized.' This contradicts the step 4 error message -- the command reports success despite a partial failure.

Suggested improvement: After a .gitignore append failure, the success message in step 5 should note that jj initialization succeeded but .gitignore was NOT updated: 'Colocated jj repo initialized. NOTE: .jj/ was not added to .gitignore (see error above). Add it manually to prevent jj internals from being tracked by git.'

File: jj/commands/jj-init.md (step 4, lines 5380-5396 in diff)
close_reason: Fix cherry-picked and review-gate PASS (raa.25: P2 suggestion, sha256 already present, comment+parallelism added)

title: detect_repo_root: jj output captured with stdout+stderr merged, path validation doesn't catch newlines in path
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root captures jj output with:
  jj_out=$(jj root 2>&1) || jj_rc=$?

This merges stdout and stderr. If jj root succeeds but also emits warnings to stderr (which some jj versions do), those warnings are included in jj_out and would be treated as part of the path. The subsequent check 'if [[ $jj_rc -eq 0 && -n "$jj_out" && -d "$jj_out" ]]; then' would fail the '-d' test because the path would contain warning text appended to the real path.

In practice, jj root does not emit warnings to stdout on success, but the 2>&1 redirection is defensive overkill here and could mask legitimate warning signals. The correct form is 'jj_out=$(jj root 2>/dev/null)' with 'jj_err=$(jj root 2>&1 1>/dev/null)' captured separately for error reporting.

Additionally: the path returned by 'jj root' could theoretically contain a newline (on exotic filesystems). The '-d "$jj_out"' test would fail in that case, but the error message would expose the raw path including the newline. The sanitize_for_output call in the error branch handles this for display, but the code path that uses jj_out directly (printf '%s\n' "$jj_out") could propagate a newline-containing path to callers.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh (lines 2478-2483 in diff)
close_reason: Fix cherry-picked and review-gate PASS (raa.25: P2 suggestion, sha256 already present, comment+parallelism added)

title: release-please-config.json: jj skill entry uses 'generic' type without version field spec
jj plugin.json missing skills/commands fields compared to homelab/pr-review plugins
description:
File: jj/plugin.json (diff lines 5816-5821).

The new jj/plugin.json only contains name/version/description:

  {
    "name": "jj",
    "version": "0.1.0",
    "description": "Jujutsu (jj) VCS workflow guidance for colocated repos"
  }

Comparing to homelab/plugin.json and pr-review/plugin.json (not in the diff but in the repo), the other plugins likely include skills/commands manifest entries. While the Claude Code plugin system may auto-discover skill directories without explicit declaration in plugin.json, the description in jj/plugin.json says 'colocated repos' which is incomplete — the plugin also supports pure jj repos (without .git/).

The marketplace.json description is consistent: 'Jujutsu (jj) VCS workflow guidance for colocated repos'. This description should be updated to reflect that pure jj repos are also supported, per the skill's own description: 'MUST activate on ANY VCS operation when .jj/ exists in the repo root.'

Suggested fix: Update description in both jj/plugin.json and .claude-plugin/marketplace.json from 'colocated repos' to 'jj repos (colocated and standalone)' or similar.

title: worktree-create.sh: cleanup_on_error runs rm -rf even when REPO_ROOT missing branch warns but continues
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, cleanup_on_error function (diff lines 2337-2371).

The cleanup_on_error function has this structure:

  cleanup_on_error() {
    if [[ ! -d "$REPO_ROOT" ]]; then
      echo "WARNING: ... VCS workspace cleanup skipped" >&2
    elif [[ -d "${REPO_ROOT}/.jj" ]]; then
      # jj cleanup
    elif [[ "$WORKSPACE_CREATED" == "true" ]]; then
      # git cleanup  
    else
      # partial git cleanup
    fi
    if ! rm_err=$(rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH" 2>&1); then
      echo "WARNING: cleanup failed..." >&2
    fi
    cleanup_empty_parent "$WORKTREE_PARENT"
  }

The unconditional 'rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH"' after the if/elif chain means that even when REPO_ROOT is missing (first branch), the rm -rf still runs. This is intentional — clean up the directory even if VCS cleanup is impossible.

However, there is an asymmetry: in the git WORKSPACE_CREATED=false case (last elif/else), 'git worktree prune' is called but not 'git worktree remove'. Then rm -rf runs. This is correct because prune handles stale metadata.

The actual bug: when REPO_ROOT is missing but WORKTREE_PATH IS set (it always is, from the validated NAME), rm -rf runs against a path that may be in an unknown location (since WORKTREE_PATH = WORKTREE_PARENT/NAME, and WORKTREE_PARENT = dirname(REPO_ROOT)/REPO_NAME_worktrees). If REPO_ROOT is gone, the path could point outside the expected sibling directory. Since WORKTREE_PATH is constructed deterministically from the validated NAME and the original REPO_ROOT (captured before any deletion), this is safe in practice.

This is a code quality/documentation issue rather than a runtime bug. The WARNING message says 'VCS workspace cleanup skipped' but does NOT say 'proceeding with directory cleanup'. Adding a follow-up message would make the behavior clearer.

Suggested fix: After the VCS-cleanup WARNING message in the REPO_ROOT missing case, add: 'echo "WARNING: cleanup: proceeding with directory removal despite missing REPO_ROOT" >&2' to make the behavior explicit for operators reading logs.

title: address-findings SKILL.md: jj conflict template field name needs version verification
description:
File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4b Jj repos section (diff lines ~7903-7905).

The jj integration step uses:

  has_conflict=$(jj log -r <change-id> --no-graph -T 'if(conflict, "true", "false")' | tr -d '\n')

The template field 'conflict' is the correct field name in jj's commit template for checking whether a revision has conflict markers. This is consistent with jj's documentation and with the eval at line 7164 that tests for 'if(conflict' in the model output.

However, there is a secondary issue: the tr -d '\n' pipe strips newlines from jj log output. If jj log outputs additional text beyond 'true' or 'false' (e.g., a trailing newline or warning message), the comparison '$has_conflict == true' may still work correctly since 'true\n' becomes 'true'. But if jj emits extra stdout (e.g., progress messages), the comparison could fail.

A safer approach would be:
  if jj log -r <change-id> --no-graph -T 'if(conflict, "YES", "NO")' 2>/dev/null | grep -q 'YES'; then

The 'tr -d \n' approach works in practice but is fragile if jj outputs multiple lines.

Suggested fix: Use grep-based matching instead of equality comparison to make the conflict check more robust against future jj output format changes.

title: no eval covering VCS detection preamble 'none' branch (no VCS detected)
description:
pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md specifies three outcomes: jj, git, and none. The 'none' branch instructs agents to STOP with STATUS: FAILED -- 'No VCS detected'. No eval in pr-review/evals/evals.json covers this path.

Existing evals cover:
- jj repo (B-FW1, B-VCS2)
- git-only repo (B-GIT-NEG1)
- VCS detection in fix-worker (B-FW1)

Missing:
- An eval where a fix-worker or verification-runner agent enters a directory where neither jj root nor git rev-parse succeeds, and verifies STATUS: FAILED with the 'No VCS detected' message is returned.

This is a suggestion-level gap because the 'none' branch is documented in the preamble and the orchestrator contract section covers the failure-handling side. An eval would make the behavior testable and visible in CI eval runs.

File: pr-review/evals/evals.json (no entry for VCS=none path)

title: worktree-remove.sh: unreachable WORKTREE_PATH empty check after canonicalization blocks
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 2545-2548 of the diff (around line 45 in the new file).

After the realpath/pwd-P canonicalization blocks, both success paths set WORKTREE_PATH to a non-empty value (the canonicalized path). Both failure paths use '|| { echo ERROR...; exit 1; }' to abort immediately. The check immediately following:

  if [[ -z "$WORKTREE_PATH" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: could not resolve canonical path..." >&2
    exit 1
  fi

can never be reached. If realpath or pwd-P succeeds, WORKTREE_PATH is set to the non-empty output. If they fail, the script has already exited via the inline || { exit 1 } handler.

This dead code creates confusion for readers who may think there is a valid path to reach the empty-check condition. It also creates a code maintenance risk: if a future refactor removes the || exit 1 guards, the empty check would silently become the only guard.

Suggested fix: Remove the unreachable empty check (lines 2545-2548), or add a comment: '# Dead code: belt-and-suspenders defensive check — both canonicalization branches above exit on failure'.

title: create_mock_jj delegates jj root to git rev-parse, masking pure-jj isolation in most tests
description:
helpers.bash:82 — create_mock_jj writes a mock jj binary whose 'root' subcommand delegates to 'git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null; exit $?'. Since the standard setup() in test_worktree_create.bats and test_worktree_remove.bats runs git init, this means all tests using create_mock_jj are implicitly colocated-repo tests, not pure-jj tests.

This has two consequences:
1. Tests labeled 'jj path' (e.g., 'jj path: creates workspace with mock jj') exercise the jj code path in the hook but fall through to git rev-parse for root detection — they don't test the fallback branch in detect_repo_root at all.
2. A regression that breaks detect_repo_root's jj-only fallback would not be caught by the majority of jj-path tests.

create_pure_jj_mock exists and is used in two tests, but the 13+ jj-path tests in test_worktree_create.bats that use create_mock_jj all operate against a git-init'd repo.

This is a test quality issue rather than a missing feature — the existing tests still provide good coverage of the hook logic, but the mock naming is misleading and the gap is invisible to a reviewer scanning test names.

Files: .claude/hooks/tests/helpers.bash:80-83, .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats (jj path tests)

title: test_jj_init.bats only tests gitignore fragment, not full jj-init command flow
description:
The jj-init command has 5 steps (check prerequisites, check already initialized, jj git init --colocate, update .gitignore, verify with jj st). The BATS test file only exercises step 4's shell fragment (the gitignore append logic). Steps 1-3 and 5 have no automated tests.

Critical untested paths:
- Step 1: git rev-parse fails (not a git repo) — should surface error and stop
- Step 2: .jj/ already exists — should stop with 'already initialized' message (covered by eval JJ-B-INIT1 but not as a BATS test)
- Step 3: jj git init --colocate exits non-zero — should surface error and stop without modifying .gitignore (covered by eval JJ-B-INIT4 but not BATS)

Evals JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4 cover these paths behaviorally, so this is a suggestion rather than a critical gap. The eval coverage provides some safety net, but BATS tests would be faster-running and CI-enforced.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_jj_init.bats
Note: Steps 1-3 are markdown-defined command invocations (not reusable shell fragments), which makes BATS testing harder without refactoring. The current approach of testing only the extractable fragment is pragmatically defensible.

title: worktree-remove.sh: jj not installed warning lacks manual cleanup command
description: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 117: 'WARNING: .jj/ found but jj not installed — workspace metadata not cleaned' gives no instruction for the user. Compare to worktree-create.sh line 44 which says 'run jj workspace forget worktree-<NAME> manually'. The removal warning should include: "(run: jj workspace forget worktree-$(sanitize_for_output "$WORKSPACE_NAME") from $(sanitize_for_output "$REPO_ROOT") after reinstalling jj)" so the operator has an actionable path to deregister the stale workspace entry.

title: jj-init command has no automated tests — entirely reliant on behavioral evals
description: The /jj-init slash command (jj/commands/jj-init.md) has four behavioral evals (JJ-B-INIT1 through JJ-B-INIT4) covering the main paths: already initialized, happy path, no git repo, and jj init failure. However, there are no automated BATS or script tests for any of the shell fragments specified in the command prose (steps 4-5). Specifically: (1) The .gitignore append logic at step 4 uses a compound 'grep -qxF ... || { ... }' pattern that contains a subtle variable capture for error text — this is not exercised by any automated test. (2) The 'jj st fails after init' error path at step 5 has no automated coverage. Since jj-init is a prose-driven command, automated testing is inherently harder, but the shell snippets are concrete enough to be unit-tested independently (e.g., the .gitignore idempotency check and error capture). This gap is lower risk since jj-init is a simple one-time setup command, but noting it for completeness.

title: jj workspace list grep format: no test verifies colon-terminated match robustness
description: worktree-remove.sh line 120 uses 'grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:"' to check workspace list output. The colon delimiter is critical — it distinguishes 'worktree-foo:' from 'worktree-foobar:'. All existing tests use mocks that include a colon after the workspace name (e.g., 'worktree-test-jj-wt: kkmpptqz...'). However, there is no test verifying that a workspace whose name is a prefix of another (e.g., removing 'test-jj' when 'test-jj-wt' also exists) correctly uses the colon to avoid a false positive match. The fix-grep uses -F (literal) which is correct, but a test with 'worktree-test-jj: ...' vs 'worktree-test-jj-wt: ...' in the list output would confirm the colon anchoring works as expected. This is a test quality gap, not a production bug, but would catch any future regex change that removes the colon.

title: worktree-remove.sh: POSIX fallback path not tested for jj workspace removal
description: test_worktree_remove.bats has two POSIX fallback tests (lines 516-579) that exercise the cd+pwd -P path when realpath is absent. Both use git repos only. The jj workspace removal path (lines 114-124 of worktree-remove.sh) is never exercised under the POSIX fallback condition. When realpath is absent, EXPECTED_PARENT canonicalization also uses cd+pwd -P (lines 87-90). A jj-path POSIX fallback test should: build a curated PATH excluding realpath, create a colocated or pure-jj setup with .jj/, create a _worktrees dir, run worktree-remove.sh; verify exit 0, directory removed, and jj workspace forget was called. This is lower priority since the POSIX fallback logic is shared (path canonicalization is VCS-agnostic) but the combined path is not exercised in tests.

title: worktree-remove.sh: inferred-root + jj installed + workspace list fails path untested
description: In worktree-remove.sh, when detect_repo_root fails (triggers inferred-root path, _REPO_ROOT_INFERRED=true), the script proceeds to VCS cleanup at lines 114-133. The jj path at line 118 calls 'jj workspace list'. There are tests for: (1) non-inferred jj path with workspace list failure (jj path: warns and skips forget when jj workspace list fails), and (2) inferred-root with jj installed and workspace forget called when root fails (B-JJ3-style). But there is no test specifically for the combination: inferred root + jj installed + workspace list fails. This leaves the code path at worktree-remove.sh:118 untested for the inferred-root case. A test should: create NON_GIT dir with .jj/, create _worktrees/orphan-wt, use a jj mock where 'root' exits 1 (forces inference) and 'workspace list' also exits 1, run from /tmp; verify WARNING about inferred repo root AND WARNING about workspace list failed both appear, directory is removed, exit 0.

title: Fix(ppi.8): redirect CI error annotations to stderr

title: worktree-create.sh: EXIT trap fires on mkdir -p failure before jj workspace add but attempts jj workspace forget unnecessarily
description: Closing: the code intentionally always attempts jj workspace forget even when WORKSPACE_CREATED=false, because jj workspace add may partially register before failing (non-atomic). The WARNING message accurately says 'may not have been registered'. This differs from the git path where git worktree add is atomic. Marking as false positive - the design is intentional and the comments explain it.

title: address-findings: jj review-gate diff uses ambiguous 'pre-rebase change ID' term
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, Phase 4c instructs: 'jj repos: capture pre-rebase change ID in Phase 4b — the diff requires it' and 'jj: jj diff --from <pre-rebase-change-id> --to <pr-bookmark>'. The term 'pre-rebase change ID' is ambiguous: it could mean the change ID of the first fix being rebased, or the change ID of the commit that was the tip of <pr-bookmark> before any rebases in the batch.

What the orchestrator actually needs for the review-gate diff is the change ID of the PR bookmark's tip *before* the batch of rebases started (i.e., the old bookmark tip). In jj, bookmarks point to change IDs, so this would be: capture the change ID that <pr-bookmark> currently resolves to before the first rebase in the batch.

The current wording could lead an AI executor to capture the fix's change ID (which stays the same across rebase), resulting in: 'jj diff --from <fix-change-id> --to <pr-bookmark>' which shows only the diff of that one fix relative to its parent — not the entire batch.

Suggested fix: Rename to 'pre-batch bookmark tip' and provide explicit capture instruction: 'Before the first rebase in this batch: bookmark_tip=$(jj log -r <pr-bookmark> --no-graph -T "change_id.short(8)")' and then 'jj diff --from $bookmark_tip --to <pr-bookmark>'.

title: cleanup_on_error jj-not-in-PATH warning not asserted in test_worktree_create.bats
description: The test 'jj path: rejects when jj not installed' (test_worktree_create.bats line 102) does exercise the cleanup_on_error path where jj is absent and .jj/ exists (worktree-create.sh lines 43-44), but it only asserts status=1 and output contains 'jj is not installed' (the main error) plus no _worktrees directory. It does not assert the cleanup WARNING: 'workspace metadata not cleaned; run jj workspace forget worktree-NAME manually'. This warning is the actionable guidance for operators when cleanup fails due to missing jj. If this warning message were accidentally removed or changed, no test would catch it. Suggested fix: add [["$output" == *"workspace metadata not cleaned"*]] assertion to the existing test, or add a dedicated test for the cleanup WARNING message.

title: test_worktree_remove.bats jj-fallback test mock missing workspace list handler makes test misleading
description: The test 'detect_repo_root falls back to jj root when git rev-parse fails' (test_worktree_remove.bats line 436) uses a mock jj that handles 'root' and 'workspace forget' but NOT 'workspace list'. Production code in worktree-remove.sh line 115 calls 'jj workspace list' before calling 'jj workspace forget'. Because the mock exits 1 for workspace list, the test silently exercises the WARNING path ('jj workspace list failed — skipping workspace forget') rather than the intended happy path where workspace forget is actually called. The test name and comment imply it verifies workspace forget is called, which it does not — forget is never reached. The test asserts only status=0 and directory removal, both of which pass through the warning path too. Fix: either add workspace list to the mock and assert forget was called, or rename/comment the test to clarify it is testing the list-failure fallback and add a warning assertion.

title: detect_repo_root in test_worktree_helpers.bats missing positive test for jj root success path
description: test_worktree_helpers.bats tests detect_repo_root for error paths (jj root returns non-directory, jj not in PATH, jj returns non-existent path, git takes priority) but has no positive test for the case where git rev-parse fails and jj root succeeds with a valid directory. This is the core pure-jj fallback happy path. The gap means a regression in jj root success handling would not be caught by the helpers unit tests. Integration tests in test_worktree_create.bats exercise this path indirectly. File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_helpers.bats, detect_repo_root section around line 164. Suggested addition: a test that creates a mock jj returning a valid directory, runs detect_repo_root from a non-git dir, and asserts status=0 and correct path output.

title: worktree-remove.sh: git rev-parse check silently conflates missing-repo with broken-git
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 103, the inferred-root VCS check uses:
  git -C "$REPO_ROOT" rev-parse --git-dir &>/dev/null

The &>/dev/null swallows both stdout and stderr. If this fails because git itself is broken or unavailable (not just because there is no repo), the failure is silently treated identically to 'no git repo present', and _skip_vcs_cleanup is set to true. This means a broken git installation causes VCS deregistration to be skipped without any diagnostic.

Suggested fix: capture stderr and emit a warning when the check fails for a non-obvious reason. A simple approach: test command -v git first, and only suppress stderr for the rev-parse failure (not for git being absent):
  if ! command -v git &>/dev/null; then
    echo "WARNING: git not found — skipping git VCS check at inferred root" >&2
    _skip_vcs_cleanup=true
  elif ! git -C "$REPO_ROOT" rev-parse --git-dir >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    _skip_vcs_cleanup=true
  fi
This is a minor improvement since the inferred-root code path is already a fallback.

title: grep -qE uses unescaped dots from WORKSPACE_NAME as regex wildcards in jj workspace-exists check
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, line 117

The workspace-exists check uses an ERE pattern with WORKSPACE_NAME interpolated directly:
  grep -qE "^\worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:"

validate_safe_name allows dots in workspace names (e.g., agent.v2). A dot in a POSIX ERE character class is a metacharacter matching any single character. So a workspace named 'agent.v2' produces the pattern '^worktree-agent.v2:' which would also match 'worktree-agentXv2:' (any character in place of the dot).

In practice this is benign: the grep result is only used as a pre-flight guard before calling jj workspace forget, and the actual forget command on line 119 uses the exact WORKSPACE_NAME string (not the regex result). A false-positive grep match would call jj workspace forget with the correct name anyway, and a non-existent workspace name would produce a jj error that is already caught and logged as a WARNING.

Suggested fix for correctness: escape the dot, or use grep -qF for a literal string match:
  echo "$_jj_ws_list" | grep -qF "worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}:"

This removes the metacharacter ambiguity without any behavior change in the normal (no-dot) case.

title: No eval for orchestrator address-findings handling STATUS: FAILED from jj fix-worker VCS check
description:
The PR adds B-VCS1 (fix-worker fails when in default jj workspace) and B-FW1 (fix-worker reports VCS:jj and CHANGE_ID) evals, but there is no eval testing what the address-findings orchestrator skill does when it receives STATUS: FAILED from a jj fix-worker due to a VCS detection failure.

The vcs-detection-preamble.md specifies an 'Orchestrator Contract': do NOT retry, log failure, clean up worktree, re-queue finding. This behavior is not evaluated. A model could correctly produce STATUS: FAILED in B-VCS1 but still handle it incorrectly at the orchestrator level.

Suggested eval: B-JJ-ORCH1 behavioral eval for address-findings skill where a fix-worker has returned STATUS: FAILED with VCS error detail. Assertions: no retry, worktree cleaned up (workspace forget + rm -rf), finding re-queued or surfaced to user.

File: pr-review/evals/evals.json
Related reference: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md (Orchestrator Contract section)

Criticality: 4/10 — the orchestrator behavior is defined in prose (vcs-detection-preamble.md) and tested in address-findings SKILL.md, but no eval catches regressions.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — eval B-VCS3 in pr-review/evals/evals.json already covers address-findings handling STATUS: FAILED from jj fix-worker, including assertions for no-jj-undo-for-failure and acknowledges-no-cleanup.

title: jj workspace list failure in inferred-root path of worktree-remove.sh has no test
description:
In worktree-remove.sh, when REPO_ROOT is inferred from the worktree path (\_REPO_ROOT_INFERRED=true) and the inferred root contains .jj/, the script still executes the jj workspace list guard before calling workspace forget. The test 'jj path: warns and skips forget when jj workspace list fails' (line 397 of test_worktree_remove.bats) covers this guard for the normal-root path, but there is no equivalent test for the inferred-root path.

The code path: inferred root + .jj/ present + jj installed + jj workspace list fails => should warn and skip forget, still remove directory.

The existing test at line 294 ('inferred root with .jj/ and jj installed: workspace forget called when detect_repo_root fails') uses a mock that makes workspace list succeed. Adding a variant where workspace list fails would complete coverage.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats
Related code: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 99-107 (inferred root VCS check) and lines 111-115 (jj workspace list guard)

Criticality: 4/10 — the normal-root workspace-list failure is tested; this is the same guard under a different entry path.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — test already exists at .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats:368 ('jj path: warns and skips forget when jj workspace list fails'). Re-run /review-pr if needed.

title: address-findings/SKILL.md exceeds 500-line project limit at 548 lines
description: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md is 548 lines, exceeding the project-mandated 500-line limit documented in CLAUDE.md under 'Validating SKILL.md Files': 'Body: under 500 lines, concise, actionable commands'. The 48-line overage is introduced by this PR's jj integration additions (Phase 4b jj section, VCS table, conflict handling, post-batch verification). The jj-specific integration prose in Phase 4b (lines ~272–434) could be condensed by moving the detailed jj undo recovery steps into a reference file (e.g., pr-review/references/jj-integration.md) and linking to it, reducing the skill body to under 500 lines. The skill-qa validator would flag this file as non-compliant.

title: jj workspace list format in SKILL.md omits change-id from mock output pattern
description: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md Workspaces section describes jj workspace list output format as: '<name>: <change-id-prefix> <commit-id-prefix> <description>' with example 'default: rwoumssn 094ee48c (empty) (no description set)'. However the test mocks in .claude/hooks/tests/helpers.bash emit single-token IDs like 'default: abc123 (no description set)' (no commit-id-prefix). The worktree-remove.sh production code checks for workspace presence using grep -qF 'worktree-${WORKSPACE_NAME}', which matches on the name prefix and is format-agnostic. The SKILL.md format description is informational rather than load-bearing, but the discrepancy between the documented two-token format and the single-token mock output may confuse developers writing future tests or scripts that parse workspace list output. Low severity since the production code is not format-dependent.

title: worktree-remove.sh POSIX fallback misnames canonical path as _cd_err
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, POSIX canonicalization fallback block

The variable _cd_err is used to capture both the canonical path (on success) and the error message (on failure):
  _cd_err=$({ cd "$_ORIG_PATH" && pwd -P; } 2>&1) || {
    echo "ERROR: ......" >&2
    exit 1
  }
  WORKTREE_PATH="$_cd_err"

On success, _cd_err contains the canonical path and is assigned to WORKTREE_PATH. On failure, _cd_err contains stderr which is used in the error message. The code works correctly but the name _cd_err is misleading — a maintainer reading WORKTREE_PATH="$_cd_err" will be confused why a canonical path is stored in an 'error' variable.

Suggested fix: Use a separate variable for the success case:
  _cd_out=
  _cd_err=
  _cd_out=$({ cd "$_ORIG_PATH" && pwd -P; } 2>&1) || {
    _cd_err="$_cd_out"
    echo "ERROR: could not canonicalize path: $(sanitize_for_output "${_cd_err:0:500}")" >&2
    exit 1
  }
  WORKTREE_PATH="$_cd_out"

This mirrors the realpath branch above which uses _rp_out consistently.

title: POSIX fallback variable _cd_err in worktree-remove.sh holds success output on happy path
description: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 2186-2190: The POSIX fallback for path canonicalization uses a variable named _cd_err to capture the output of '{ cd "$_ORIG_PATH" && pwd -P; } 2>&1'. On success, _cd_err holds the canonicalized path (stdout of pwd -P). On failure, it holds the error message (stderr redirected via 2>&1). The variable name implies it holds only errors, but it also holds the success value (WORKTREE_PATH="$_cd_err" on line 2190). This naming is misleading and could cause a future maintainer to misread the logic as only running on failure. Suggest renaming to _cd_out or _canon_path to clarify it holds the canonicalized path on success and error text on failure.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: duplicate of fzymgc-house-skills-21s.9 — same _cd_err naming issue in worktree-remove.sh.

title: No behavioral eval for jj workspace add --help TOCTOU or pure-jj repo in pr-review eval suite
description:
jj/evals/evals.json covers skill triggering, behavioral guidance, and jj-init flows well. However, two agent-behavior scenarios triggered by this PR's new code paths have no eval coverage:

1. **fix-worker in a pure-jj repo (no .git/)**: The VCS detection preamble in vcs-detection-preamble.md tells agents to run 'test -d .jj' and 'jj root'. There is no eval asserting that fix-worker reports VCS: jj correctly when the workspace has only .jj/ (no .git/), which is a distinct detection path from the colocated case.

2. **address-findings orchestrator receiving STATUS: FAILED from fix-worker in jj repo**: B-VCS1 covers the fix-worker side (default workspace detection), but there is no eval for how the orchestrator (address-findings) handles a FAILED result that includes VCS: jj — specifically whether it avoids running jj undo when there was no successful operation to undo.

These are lower-priority since the shell-level hooks have extensive bats coverage, but behavioral evals for agents are the only automated check for model-level compliance.

Files: jj/evals/evals.json, pr-review/evals/evals.json

title: Missing test: POSIX fallback for EXPECTED_PARENT in worktree-remove.sh cannot be exercised on CI
description:
The 'POSIX fallback: errors when _worktrees parent dir does not exist' test in test_worktree_remove.bats (around line 1917) builds a curated PATH with symlinks for all required commands but excludes realpath. However, the test asserts '[[ ! -x "$clean_bin/realpath" ]]' but never verifies that PATH=$clean_bin actually prevents the real realpath from being found — if bash or any subshell restores PATH from the environment, realpath could still be found, silently making the test a no-op instead of exercising the fallback.

The analogous test 'POSIX fallback: removes worktree when realpath absent from PATH' (around line 1888) has the same issue.

This is a test-reliability concern rather than a production bug, but if the POSIX fallback code regresses it would not be caught. The skip guard in the first POSIX test (line 1871) self-skips on systems where realpath is available, meaning the fallback path is never exercised in CI (ubuntu-latest always has realpath).

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats (POSIX fallback tests)
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (cd + pwd -P fallback branches)

Suggestion: run the POSIX fallback tests with PATH=$clean_bin rather than PATH=$clean_bin:$PATH to guarantee realpath is excluded. Also add an explicit assertion that 'command -v realpath' fails within the test environment to surface accidental PATH leakage.

title: test_worktree_create.bats: duplicate mkdir-p failure test cases
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, there are two nearly identical tests covering mkdir -p failure:

1. 'fails gracefully when mkdir -p fails' (line 445) — git path, no message assertion
2. 'git path: mkdir -p failure exits 1 with descriptive error message' (line 461) — git path, asserts error message

Test 1 at line 445 is a strict subset of test 2 at line 461 (same setup, same failure mode, but test 1 lacks the message assertion). Test 1 adds no coverage that test 2 does not already provide. This is a YAGNI violation per the project's review standards.

Additionally, there is a third test 'jj path: mkdir -p failure exits 1 with descriptive error message' (line 476) that correctly covers the jj code path.

Suggested fix: Remove the redundant test at line 445 ('fails gracefully when mkdir -p fails'). The coverage is fully provided by line 461.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Review gate FAIL (attempt 1): renamed test and added assertion, but duplicate test 'git path: mkdir -p failure exits 1 with descriptive error message' still exists at line 462. Must consolidate/remove the duplicate.

title: address-findings SKILL.md uses 'jj --repository' to inspect worktree from main repo — flag may not behave as expected for workspace-scoped queries
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 297, the orchestrator is instructed to verify a worktree's state with:

  jj --repository <worktree-path> log -r @- --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)'

The jj --repository / -R flag sets the working-copy directory. When the orchestrator runs this from the main repo root, the @ and @- revsets resolve in the context of that workspace, which is the correct intent. However, this behavior depends on jj's --repository flag correctly setting the workspace context rather than just the shared repo root.

In the vcs-equivalence.md Current location entry, the equivalent is:
  jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)'

which is always run from inside the workspace (via cd). Using --repository from outside the workspace is less tested and may behave differently in edge cases (e.g., in non-colocated pure-jj repos where --repository may point to the .jj/ shared store).

Suggested fix: Replace 'jj --repository <worktree-path> log ...' with 'jj -R <worktree-path> log ...' (same thing but canonical short form), AND add a note that this has been verified with jj 0.39+ behavior. Alternatively, consistently use 'cd <worktree-path> && jj log ...' which avoids the flag ambiguity entirely.

title: worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error falls through to git path when .jj exists but jj is unavailable mid-cleanup
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, cleanup_on_error (line 38) checks:

  elif [[ -d "${REPO_ROOT}/.jj" ]] && command -v jj &>/dev/null; then

If .jj exists but jj is not in PATH during cleanup (e.g., the workspace add succeeded but then lefthook failed), the else branch runs 'git worktree remove --force WORKTREE_PATH'. In a pure jj repo (no .git/), this git command will fail because there is no .git/worktrees/ metadata to clean up, emitting a misleading error message.

The condition should emit a WARNING instead of silently falling to the git path when .jj exists but jj is unavailable. The git branch should only run when neither .jj nor relevant git metadata is present.

Note: The main code path guards against jj-not-installed at line 64 and exits early, so WORKSPACE_CREATED never becomes true in this scenario for jj repos. The bug is only reachable in theory (e.g., jj removed from PATH between lines 86 and the EXIT trap firing). Severity: suggestion.

title: actions/checkout SHA comment labels it 'v6' but that version does not exist
description: In .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, all three job steps pin actions/checkout with SHA de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd and the comment '# v6'. However actions/checkout v6 does not exist (latest major version is v4 as of 2025). The SHA was carried over from a previous annotation and the comment is stale/incorrect. Suggested fix: verify the SHA against https://github.com/actions/checkout/releases and update the comment to the actual version (likely '# v4').

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — actions/checkout v6 now exists (git ls-remote confirms SHA de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd = refs/tags/v6). Re-run /review-pr if needed.

title: detect_repo_root conflates jj root output with error text in error message
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, detect_repo_root captures jj root with 2>&1 into jj_out (line 58). On success, jj_out holds the repo path; on failure, it holds the error message. When jj root succeeds but returns a path that is not a directory (-d check fails at line 59), execution falls through to the error message on line 65 which includes '${jj_out:+; jj: $(sanitize_for_output "$jj_out")}'. At this point jj_out contains the valid-looking path, so the error would read: 'git rev-parse and jj root both failed; jj: /some/path' — which is misleading because jj root actually succeeded; the problem was the path not existing as a directory.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, lines 57-65.

Suggested fix: Capture stdout and stderr separately, or add a specific diagnostic branch: if [[ $jj_rc -eq 0 && -n "$jj_out" && ! -d "$jj_out" ]]; then echo 'ERROR: jj root returned $(sanitize ...) which is not an existing directory' >&2; return 1; fi

title: no test for jj workspace add failure with partial directory creation (mkdir succeeds, jj fails, cleanup races)
description:
test_worktree_create.bats tests cleanup when jj workspace add fails (line 128-135, 'cleans up worktree and parent on workspace add failure'). However, jj workspace add in the production code (worktree-create.sh line 81-85) runs with the WORKTREE_PATH as a positional argument and jj itself creates the directory as part of the operation. The mock _mock_workspace_add creates the directory with mkdir -p before exiting 0.

When jj workspace add fails after creating the directory (which the mock simulates via create_failing_jj_mock), WORKSPACE_CREATED stays false, so cleanup_on_error skips jj workspace forget but still calls rm -rf and cleanup_empty_parent. This path IS tested. But the test at line 128 only uses create_failing_jj_mock (which doesn't create the directory at all, since its 'workspace add' doesn't call _mock_workspace_add). So the directory never actually exists, and the rm -rf in cleanup is a no-op.

A more realistic failure test where jj creates the directory but then fails mid-way (e.g., workspace registration fails after directory creation) would verify that cleanup removes a directory that was partially created by jj itself. The current test_worktree_create.bats line 128-135 asserts [ ! -d ] which passes trivially because the directory was never created.

title: realpath output variable _rp_err reused as success value in worktree-remove.sh
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, the pattern on lines 26-30 and 84-86 captures realpath output into a variable named _rp_err / _ep_err. On success this variable holds the canonical path (assigned to WORKTREE_PATH / EXPECTED_PARENT); on failure it holds the error message. The dual use of an error-named variable for success output is confusing and a maintenance trap.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 25-37 and 84-90.

While functionally correct today (both uses are immediately consumed), a future maintainer adding code between the assignment and the success check could incorrectly use _rp_err assuming it is an error message.

Additionally, since both cases use '2>&1', if realpath emits both a canonical path AND a warning to stdout simultaneously (non-standard but theoretically possible), WORKTREE_PATH would contain newline-separated content, bypassing the safety validation.

Suggested fix: Use separate variables for capture: _rp_out for success output, _rp_err only for error capture using a separate error capture file or process substitution.

title: no behavioral eval for jj @ vs @- working-copy semantics in pr-review agents
description:
CLAUDE.md documents a known gotcha: in jj repos, '@' is the working-copy commit (typically empty) and '@-' is the parent (the meaningful committed state). Agents should use '@-' for verification after jj commit/undo operations. The JJ-B-UNDO1 eval tests the double-undo misconception, but there is no eval testing whether agents correctly use '@-' (not '@') when verifying committed state.

This is a high-frequency error pattern (mentioned in CLAUDE.md under 'jj Semantics (Gotchas)' and in the jj SKILL.md gotchas), yet none of the behavioral evals in jj/evals/evals.json verify that the agent uses '@-' for post-commit verification rather than '@'. An agent that uses 'jj show @' instead of 'jj show @-' would report an empty working-copy commit rather than the committed changes — a silent correctness failure.

Suggested eval: prompt asking the agent to verify what was just committed in a jj repo. Assert output contains '@-' or 'parent' rather than checking '@' alone.

title: cleanup_on_error in worktree-create.sh does not call git worktree prune as fallback
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, when cleanup_on_error runs for a git repo (WORKSPACE_CREATED=true, no .jj/), it calls 'git worktree remove --force $WORKTREE_PATH' (line 45). If this fails, a WARNING is emitted but no fallback 'git worktree prune' is attempted.

By contrast, .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (lines 120-124) correctly falls back to 'git worktree prune' if 'git worktree remove --force' fails.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, lines 44-47 (cleanup_on_error function).

This inconsistency means failed creation cleanup can leave stale .git/worktrees/ metadata, unlike the removal path which uses prune as a safety net.

Suggested fix: Mirror the worktree-remove.sh pattern in cleanup_on_error:
  if \! git_err=$(git worktree remove --force "$WORKTREE_PATH" 2>&1); then
    echo "WARNING: ..." >&2
    git worktree prune 2>/dev/null || true
  fi

title: mock jj workspace forget accepts any worktree-* name including wrong workspace
description:
The shared mock jj created by _write_mock_jj() and create_logging_jj_mock() matches 'workspace forget' success on any positional argument matching worktree-*. This means a test calling jj workspace forget worktree-WRONG-NAME would still exit 0 when it should assert the exact workspace name passed.

In test_worktree_create.bats line 163-164, the test correctly checks $(cat forget-arg.log) for the exact name. But in test_worktree_remove.bats line 131-137 ('jj path: removes workspace directory'), the mock succeeds on the forget call but there is NO assertion that the correct workspace name was passed — only that the directory was removed and status is 0. A bug in the workspace name derivation (e.g., basename vs. full path) would go undetected.

The test at line 150-157 ('jj path: calls jj workspace forget with correct name') does verify the logged name, but the simpler 'removes workspace directory' test at line 131 does not, leaving a coverage gap for name correctness in the basic happy path.

Suggested fix: add a name assertion to the basic removal test, or document that name correctness is covered by the dedicated test at line 150.

title: no test for jj workspace forget when remove runs with inferred repo root and jj present
description:
test_worktree_remove.bats has a test at line 234 for 'inferred root with .jj/ and jj installed: removes directory and calls workspace forget'. However, this test sets up a mock jj that succeeds on 'root', meaning detect_repo_root succeeds directly without needing inference. The actual code path exercised by _REPO_ROOT_INFERRED=true with _skip_vcs_cleanup=false (line 99-107 of worktree-remove.sh) — where the inferred root HAS a .jj dir and jj IS installed — is not covered by a test where root inference is confirmed.

The distinction matters: when detect_repo_root fails but the inferred root has .jj/, the script calls jj workspace forget with only the inferred root as context (no git rev-parse success to corroborate). The test at line 234 passes because mock jj root succeeds immediately, bypassing the inference path entirely.

Suggested test: create a directory without .git/ and without a jj in PATH that would make git rev-parse fail, then add a mock jj that responds to 'root' with a failure (forcing path inference), but have .jj/ exist at the inferred root and jj available. Verify that workspace forget is still called.

title: patched temp files created in source tree directory may leak on test assertion failure
description:
Five tests in test_worktree_create.bats create patched temp files using mktemp with a base path of $BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../ (i.e., .claude/hooks/), writing files like worktree-create-test-XXXXXX.sh into the source tree. The pattern sets 'trap RETURN' for cleanup, but BATS test blocks are not bash functions so RETURN traps do not fire. The explicit 'rm -f "$patched"' before assertions handles normal flow, but if an assertion fires before that rm, the temp file is leaked into the source directory.

Affected tests (lines 146-165, 167-198, 240-257, 259-286, 288-333 of test_worktree_create.bats).

Fix: replace mktemp base path with $BATS_TEST_TMPDIR (BATS-provided per-test temp directory cleaned after each test regardless of pass/fail). Remove the trap RETURN calls and the redundant explicit rm -f calls — BATS handles cleanup via BATS_TEST_TMPDIR automatically.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — temp file leak issue addressed by trap RETURN added in commit e311ae8. Patched temp files now cleaned up on both normal and abnormal test exits.

title: Base mock jj in helpers.bash silently accepts any non-worktree-prefixed workspace forget argument
description: In helpers.bash, _write_mock_jj (used by create_mock_jj and create_pure_jj_mock) handles 'workspace forget' only with the pattern '$3 == worktree-*' (line 40). If $3 does not match 'worktree-*', control falls through to 'echo ERROR: unexpected jj invocation; exit 1'. This means tests using create_mock_jj that happen to trigger workspace forget with an unexpected name would fail with an unhelpful 'unexpected invocation' error rather than a clear assertion failure. The production scripts always pass 'worktree-${NAME}' so this is not a current bug, but it makes the mock brittle: if a future refactor renamed the workspace prefix the mock would give a confusing error. Additionally, the remove-path mock (create_logging_jj_mock) uses a loop to find positional args, while the base mock uses positional $3 directly — this inconsistency between mocks creates subtle behavior differences. Suggested fix: add a comment explaining the expected invocation pattern and consider unifying the workspace forget dispatch.

title: cleanup_empty_parent: rmdir failure warning path untested in test_worktree_helpers.bats
description: worktree-helpers.sh cleanup_empty_parent() has two guarded failure paths: (1) ls failure (line 34-37) — tested by 'warns when ls fails (permission error)' in test_worktree_helpers.bats, and (2) rmdir failure on an empty directory (line 39) — emits a WARNING but has no test. The rmdir failure path can be triggered by making a directory empty but removing write permission on its parent (so rmdir is refused). Without a test, a future refactor that silently swallows this error or changes the warning text would go undetected. Suggested fix: Add a test that creates an empty directory, removes write permission on its parent so rmdir fails, calls cleanup_empty_parent, and asserts that output contains 'WARNING' and 'failed to remove empty parent'.

title: No eval for jj undo op-log semantics — critical agent gotcha with no behavioral coverage
description: SKILL.md documents the jj undo op-log gotcha, and CLAUDE.md identifies it as a recurring review finding: jj op log only records successful operations, so jj undo after a failed command reverts the previous successful op. Never 'undo twice' to account for a failure. This semantic difference from git reflog is agent-critical — incorrect undo chains have caused real review findings in this very repo (ka4.59/ka4.66 pattern noted in MEMORY.md). Despite being called out as important in CLAUDE.md, there is no behavioral eval testing that an agent correctly understands undo semantics (e.g., recognizing that a failed 'jj bookmark set' leaves no op-log trace and undo skips it). Suggested fix: Add JJ-B-UNDO1: prompt 'jj bookmark set failed, should I run jj undo twice to clean up?' with a not_contains assertion for 'undo twice' and a contains assertion for 'only once' or 'no entry'.

title: Test cleanup uses fixed patched filename, leaks artifacts on test failure and conflicts in parallel runs
description:
In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, multiple tests (lines 155, 189, 244, 274, 320) create a patched version of worktree-create.sh using a hardcoded path:
  patched="$BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../worktree-create-test-patched.sh"

Two issues:
1. If the test process is killed abruptly (e.g., SIGKILL, Ctrl-C) before the rm -f line runs, the file leaks into the source tree. It is not listed in .gitignore, so it would appear as an untracked file on the next git status.

2. If bats is invoked with --jobs N (parallel mode), multiple tests will race to write to the same hardcoded path, causing test interference and non-deterministic failures.

The rm -f is placed immediately after 'run', so in normal sequential execution this is fine. But the parallel case is a real risk for local development workflows.

Suggested fix: Use mktemp to generate a unique temp file per test execution:
  patched=$(mktemp "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/worktree-create-test-XXXXXX.sh")
  trap 'rm -f "$patched"' RETURN

This eliminates both the leak and the parallel conflict.

File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, lines 155, 189, 244, 274, 320

title: Eval JJ-B8 assertion checks for 'test -d .jj' but expected response would reference 'jj root'
description:
In jj/evals/evals.json, eval JJ-B8 has this assertion:
  {"name": "references-vcs-detection", "type": "contains", "check": "test -d .jj"}

The prompt asks: 'I'm in a jj repo and need to review a PR — what VCS commands should agents use?'

The skill body would guide the model to reference pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md for agent VCS commands. That preamble document uses 'jj root >/dev/null 2>&1' NOT 'test -d .jj'. The preamble only mentions 'test -d .jj' as a fallback path-match approach (with a caveat about false positives), not as the primary detection command.

The assertion as written will likely produce a false negative — the model gives the correct answer but the automated check fails because the correct answer uses 'jj root' not 'test -d .jj'.

If JJ-B8 was intended to verify the model cites the preamble's detection command, the check should be 'jj root' (or 'jj root >/dev/null'). If it was intended to verify the simpler SKILL.md detection snippet, the expected_output description is misleading.

File: jj/evals/evals.json, JJ-B8 assertion (lines ~242-248)

title: jj-init step 4 .gitignore append failure message does not distinguish write vs permission errors
description:
File: jj/commands/jj-init.md, step 4

The .gitignore append command is:
  grep -qxF '.jj/' .gitignore 2>/dev/null || echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore

The 2>/dev/null on grep suppresses ALL grep errors (file-not-found, permission denied, etc.), and when grep fails for any reason, the || branch runs the append unconditionally. This is the correct behavior (if .gitignore doesn't exist, create and append), but any grep error other than 'file not found' is silently swallowed.

More importantly, when the append fails (non-zero exit), the skill instructs the agent to report: 'Could not update .gitignore automatically. Please add .jj/ to .gitignore manually.' This generic message does not tell the user WHY it failed (read-only filesystem, permissions, disk full) making it harder to self-diagnose.

Suggested fix: Capture and surface the append error:
  if \! append_err=$(echo '.jj/' >> .gitignore 2>&1); then
    inform user: "Could not update .gitignore: $append_err — please add .jj/ manually."
  fi

This is a skill-level (model-invocable) instruction so the fix is a documentation change to jj-init.md.

title: detect_repo_root error message omits jj diagnostic when jj is not in PATH
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, lines 54-63

In detect_repo_root, when 'command -v jj' fails (jj not in PATH), the function skips the jj root block entirely and falls through to the error message. The error message uses the conditional expansion ${jj_out:+; jj: $(sanitize_for_output "$jj_out")} to append jj's output — but since the jj block was skipped, jj_out is never set and remains empty.

The resulting error is: 'ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse and jj root both failed)' — the parenthetical says 'both failed' but jj was never tried, only skipped. This is misleading: the user reads 'jj root failed' when in fact jj was not found.

This is a documentation/precision issue in the error message, not a silent failure, but it could send users down the wrong diagnostic path.

Suggested fix: Track whether jj was attempted and tailor the error message accordingly:

  if command -v jj &>/dev/null; then
    ... # try jj root, set jj_attempted=true
  fi
  
  # In error:
  if [[ -z ${jj_attempted+x} ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse failed; jj not in PATH)" >&2
  else
    echo "ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse and jj root both failed...) " >&2
  fi

title: 200-char truncation on jj workspace add error may hide the actionable diagnostic
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, line 81; also worktree-remove.sh line 116 and multiple other sites

Error output from 'jj workspace add' and 'jj workspace forget' is truncated to 200 characters via ${jj_out:0:200} / ${jj_err:0:200}. jj errors are sometimes multi-line with context (e.g., version mismatch messages, conflict descriptions, repository path details). When the actionable part of an error appears after the 200-char mark, the user sees a truncated message with no clear cause.

The truncation is applied consistently across all error sites in both worktree-create.sh and worktree-remove.sh. 200 chars is enough for short errors but may be insufficient for jj's verbose error output.

Suggested fix: Increase the truncation limit to 500 chars, or emit the first and last N chars with an ellipsis in the middle so the beginning (error type) and end (specific detail) are both visible. For the cleanup WARNING path in particular, showing only the first 200 chars of a long error may hide the specific workspace name or path that failed.

title: No eval covering jj undo-on-conflict vs no-undo-on-hard-failure distinction (B7 is manual-only)
description: pr-review/evals/evals.json B7 covers the conflict-after-rebase detection path for address-findings. Both 'runs-jj-undo-on-conflict' and 'no-undo-on-hard-failure' assertions are typed 'manual'. The distinction between a soft conflict (rebase exits 0, commit has markers) and a hard failure (rebase exits non-zero) is one of the more nuanced jj behaviors added in this PR and is documented in CLAUDE.md. The 'detects-conflict-via-template' assertion uses type 'contains' with check 'if(conflict' — this is a good automated check for the template syntax. Adding at least one more automated assertion (e.g. 'type': 'not_contains', 'check': 'git cherry-pick' to confirm no git fallback) would give the eval more automated coverage and reduce reliance on purely manual verification for this safety-critical path.

title: test_worktree_create.bats: stdout/stderr not separated in jj path success tests
description: Several jj-path success tests (e.g. 'jj path: creates workspace with mock jj', 'jj path: runs lefthook install after jj workspace add') use '2>&1' to merge stderr into $output, then assert '[[ "$output" == *"_worktrees/...*" ]]'. The hook framework reads only stdout to get the worktree path; stderr is for warnings/errors. Merging both streams means the assertion would pass even if the path were accidentally emitted on stderr rather than stdout. The lifecycle test ('jj path: create-then-remove lifecycle uses consistent workspace names') at line 541 does NOT merge streams and passes the output path directly as an argument — this is the correct pattern. The success-path tests should split the assertion to check stdout only (e.g. run's '$output' with separate stderr capture or without '2>&1').

title: No eval for /jj-init command behavioral correctness
description: jj/evals/evals.json has JJ-T7 (triggering eval) that only checks whether /jj-init slash command is invoked. There are no behavioral evals for what /jj-init actually does: (1) detecting an already-initialized .jj/ repo and reporting it, (2) initializing jj in a clean git repo, (3) initializing jj in a non-git directory. JJ-B7 covers 'initialize jj in this repo / already has .jj/' but that targets the jujutsu skill, not the /jj-init command. The /jj-init command definition at jj/commands/jj-init.md has distinct logic that warrants its own behavioral evals covering the pre-condition checks (existing .jj/, git repo presence) and the expected output.

title: jj-init command does not surface jj init failure error text to user
description:
In jj/commands/jj-init.md, step 3 says: 'If the command fails, report the error to the user and stop.' However, there is no instruction to capture and include jj's stderr output in the failure report. An agent implementing this will likely just say 'jj git init --colocate failed' without explaining why (e.g., unsupported flags in old jj version, filesystem issues, or already-initialized repo state if the test in step 2 races).

The instruction should explicitly tell the agent to include jj's stderr in the user-facing message, e.g.:
  'If the command fails, report the full error output to the user and stop. Include jj's stderr output so the user knows the exact failure reason.'

Similarly, step 5 runs 'jj st' and 'jj log' for verification but gives no guidance on what to do if these commands fail after a successful 'jj git init --colocate' (partial init scenario).

title: VCS detection redirect order in vcs-detection-preamble.md is non-obvious and fragile
description:
In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, the VCS detection snippet uses an uncommon redirect ordering:

  if jj root 2>/dev/null >&2; then echo "jj"

The ordering '2>/dev/null >&2' is correct (stderr is redirected to /dev/null first, then stdout is redirected to the now-null fd 2), but it is the opposite of the conventional '>/dev/null 2>&1'. A maintainer who is not intimately familiar with bash redirect ordering may read this as 'stderr to /dev/null, stdout to stderr (leaking output)' and "fix" it to the wrong ordering.

The address-findings/SKILL.md uses the conventional order on the same detection command (line 5996: 'if jj root > /dev/null 2>&1'), creating an inconsistency between the two places where this pattern appears.

Suggested fix: Change the preamble to use the conventional and widely-understood '>/dev/null 2>&1' ordering:
  if jj root >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "jj"
  elif git rev-parse --git-dir >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "git"
  else echo "none"; fi

title: _rmdir_err not declared local in cleanup_empty_parent leaks global variable
description: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh line 38: _rmdir_err is assigned without a 'local' declaration inside cleanup_empty_parent(). All other error-capture variables in this file use 'local' consistently. The missing declaration leaks the variable into the caller's scope when rmdir fails (the only path where _rmdir_err is assigned). This is a code quality issue in a shared library function called from both hook scripts. Fix: add 'local _rmdir_err' alongside the other local declarations at the top of the function (after 'local ls_out').

title: git worktree remove warning embeds WORKTREE_PATH without sanitize_for_output in worktree-remove.sh
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, when git worktree remove fails, the warning message embeds WORKTREE_PATH without passing it through sanitize_for_output:

  echo "WARNING: git worktree remove failed for '$WORKTREE_PATH': ..."

All other messages that embed paths (WORKTREE_PATH, WORKSPACE_NAME, etc.) in this file use sanitize_for_output. While WORKTREE_PATH has already been canonicalized via realpath at this point (reducing practical risk), the inconsistency breaks the established sanitization discipline and could allow residual control characters in edge cases.

Fix: wrap with sanitize_for_output:
  echo "WARNING: git worktree remove failed for '$(sanitize_for_output "$WORKTREE_PATH")': ..."

title: _rmdir_err missing local declaration in cleanup_empty_parent leaks into caller scope
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, the cleanup_empty_parent function uses _rmdir_err as a capture variable without declaring it local:

  _rmdir_err=$(rmdir "$parent" 2>&1) || echo "WARNING: ..." >&2

The function properly declares 'local parent' and 'local ls_out', but omits 'local _rmdir_err'. As a result, _rmdir_err pollutes the caller's shell scope (both worktree-create.sh and worktree-remove.sh call this function). While not causing incorrect behavior today, it is inconsistent with the rest of the function and could cause confusion if a future caller accidentally reads _rmdir_err after calling cleanup_empty_parent.

Fix: add 'local _rmdir_err' after 'local ls_out' in cleanup_empty_parent.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: duplicate of fzymgc-house-skills-8ho.120 (same _rmdir_err missing local issue)

title: detect_repo_root embeds unsanitized jj output in error message
description:
In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, the detect_repo_root function embeds the raw output of a failed 'jj root' call directly into the error message without sanitize_for_output:

  echo "ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (git rev-parse and jj root both failed${jj_out:+; jj: $jj_out})" >&2

Every other error message in worktree-create.sh and worktree-remove.sh that includes command output uses sanitize_for_output to strip C0 control chars and ANSI escape sequences. jj error output may include color codes (ESC sequences) that could corrupt terminal state or confuse log parsers.

Fix: wrap the jj_out reference:
  echo "ERROR: not inside a git/jj repository (...${jj_out:+; jj: $(sanitize_for_output "${jj_out:0:200}")}" >&2

title: No eval for colocated jj+git triggering boundary in jj evals
description: jj/evals/evals.json has JJ-T5 (generic commit without .jj/ = no trigger), JJ-T6 (.jj/ present = trigger), and JJ-T8 (git merge conflict, no .jj/ = no trigger), but lacks a triggering eval for the colocated case where both .jj/ AND .git/ are present. The SKILL.md description triggers on '.jj/ exists in repo root OR user mentions jj/jujutsu'. In a colocated repo, git commands run normally alongside jj — an eval should assert the skill still triggers correctly (e.g., 'git status' with both .jj/ and .git/ present should trigger jujutsu). This boundary is exercised by the worktree hook tests but not by the skill trigger evals. Location: jj/evals/evals.json.

title: No negative eval for pr-review agents accidentally using jj commands in git-only repos
description: pr-review/evals/evals.json has thorough jj behavioral evals (B-JJ1 through B-RG1) for jj repos, but has no negative case eval verifying that pr-review agents (fix-worker, address-findings) do NOT use jj commands when running in a git-only repo (no .jj/ present). The VCS detection preamble 'test -d .jj' was added to all 12 agents; a misimplemented preamble that always selects jj would be caught by a behavioral test like: prompt='dispatch fix-worker to fix a finding', context='repo has .git/ but no .jj/', assertion=not_contains 'jj commit'. Without this, the guard can silently regress. This gap is especially relevant because the preamble change touches all 12 agent files.

title: JJ eval JJ-B2 assertion 'jj commit -m' may produce false negatives
description: jj/evals/evals.json eval JJ-B2 asserts type:contains check:'jj commit -m' to verify the skill uses jj for committing. However, jj supports equivalent workflows: 'jj describe -m <msg> && jj new' or 'jj commit --message' (long form). The SKILL.md could legitimately recommend the long form '--message' flag, which would cause the eval to fail even when the behavior is correct. The assertion should use a looser check like 'jj commit' or 'jj describe' with an OR, or the eval should be typed as 'manual' to avoid mechanical false negatives. Location: jj/evals/evals.json lines 108-122.

title: No test for cleanup_on_error rm -rf failure warning in worktree-create.sh
description: worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error() has a WARNING path at line 48-50 for when rm -rf fails to remove the worktree directory after VCS deregistration. The existing cleanup tests (git and jj paths) only verify that the directory is removed successfully or that the parent is cleaned up; none assert the 'WARNING: cleanup failed for' message that fires when rm_err is non-empty. A regression here could cause silent swallowing of rm errors during cleanup. Suggested: chmod the WORKTREE_PATH unremovable after WORKSPACE_CREATED=true (patched script), then assert WARNING in output.

title: Duplicate Bash(jj root) and Bash(jj root *) entries in review-pr/SKILL.md allowed-tools
description:
pr-review/skills/review-pr/SKILL.md has two entries for jj root in its allowed-tools:
- 'Bash(jj root *)'   (line 32)
- 'Bash(jj root)'     (line 33)

While having both entries is technically correct (the bare form is needed as * does not match zero args), it suggests inconsistency — either the '*' form was added first expecting it to cover bare invocations, or the pattern was copied from elsewhere without consolidation. The bare-only entry is sufficient if 'jj root' is only ever called without arguments. The '*' form is needed only if 'jj root --something' is also called.

Fix: Keep 'Bash(jj root)' and remove 'Bash(jj root *)' if jj root is never called with arguments in the skill body. Otherwise keep both and document why both are needed.

The test 'infers repo root from worktree path when outside git repo' (test_worktree_remove.bats:210) exercises this path but only asserts 'WARNING' and 'inferred repo root'. It does NOT assert the 'skipping VCS cleanup' message.

If the warning message changed or the condition logic was wrong, the test would not catch it. The 'skipping VCS cleanup' message is distinct from the 'inferred repo root' message and represents a separate code branch worth asserting.

The test 'git path: cleanup_on_error removes worktree on post-add failure' (test_worktree_create.bats:206) uses a real git repo where git worktree remove succeeds, so the WARNING branch at line 44 is never reached.

A test using a mock git that fails on 'worktree remove' combined with the patched-script technique would cover this path. This would catch regressions in the warning message or sanitize_for_output usage.

The test 'jj path: cleanup_on_error calls jj workspace forget on post-add failure' (test_worktree_create.bats:146) uses create_logging_jj_mock where workspace forget always succeeds, so the WARNING message path is never exercised.

A test using a patched script (similar to the existing post-add failure test) combined with a mock that fails on workspace forget would cover this. This would catch a regression if the sanitize_for_output call or the warning message format changed.

title: cleanup_empty_parent suppresses rmdir stderr entirely
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh, line 38.

The cleanup_empty_parent function does:
  rmdir "$parent" 2>/dev/null || echo "WARNING: failed to remove empty parent '$parent'" >&2

The '2>/dev/null' silently discards rmdir's stderr. The warning message that follows contains only the path but no OS-level reason (e.g. 'Device or resource busy', 'Permission denied'). Since this is called during both normal cleanup and error-path cleanup, lack of diagnostic context makes silent failures harder to investigate.

Suggested fix:
  rmdir_err=$(rmdir "$parent" 2>&1) || echo "WARNING: failed to remove empty parent '$parent': $(sanitize_for_output "${rmdir_err:0:200}")" >&2

Note: sanitize_for_output is already available in this file.

title: rm -rf stderr swallowed in worktree-remove.sh directory removal
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, lines 132-134.

The directory removal block is:
  if ! rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH"; then
    echo "ERROR: failed to remove worktree directory '$WORKTREE_PATH'" >&2
    exit 1
  fi

When rm -rf fails (e.g. permission denied), its stderr is not captured — the error message contains only the path, not the OS-level reason. This makes the error harder to diagnose in production logs.

Suggested fix: capture stderr and include it in the message:
  if ! rm_err=$(rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH" 2>&1); then
    echo "ERROR: failed to remove worktree directory '$WORKTREE_PATH': $(sanitize_for_output "${rm_err:0:200}")" >&2
    exit 1
  fi
