The rumdl download itself is well-secured (checksummed), and the GITHUB_TOKEN scope is minimal (read-only checkout). This is a low-severity supply chain hardening suggestion.

File location: .github/workflows/check-skills.yml lines 11-12, 44-46

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale — checksum verification already present (sha256sum -c with pinned v0.1.43).

title: worktree-create.sh: jj workspace add --name constructed from unsanitized NAME without shell quoting review
description:
In worktree-create.sh line 60-61, the jj workspace add command is constructed as:
  jj workspace add "$WORKTREE_PATH" --name "worktree-${NAME}"

NAME has been validated by validate_safe_name (alphanumeric, dots, hyphens, underscores only, no leading dot, no trailing dot, no '..'). The concatenated workspace name is 'worktree-${NAME}' which is double-quoted, preventing word splitting and globbing. This appears safe.

However, the validate_safe_name regex allows hyphens in NAME, so the constructed workspace name could be 'worktree---foo' (double hyphens). While jj should handle this correctly, it's worth noting that jj workspace names ending or starting with hyphens may or may not be valid depending on jj version. If jj rejects the name, the error path cleans up correctly.

Similarly, WORKTREE_PATH is constructed as:
  "${WORKTREE_PARENT}/${NAME}"
where WORKTREE_PARENT derives from dirname(REPO_ROOT) and REPO_NAME (both validated). The path is double-quoted throughout, so no word splitting risk.

This is a suggestion to add a test case covering hyphen-heavy names like 'worktree---nested' to confirm jj accepts them, and to document the character constraints inherited by workspace names.

File location: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 60-61

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale — NAME is already properly double-quoted in all uses.

title: No test for rm -rf failure in worktree-remove hook
description:
worktree-remove.sh line 72-75 exits with status 1 if rm -rf fails on the worktree directory. The tests cover successful removal and various warning paths, but no test exercises the error exit from rm -rf failure.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines 72-75, .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats.

Without this test, a regression that changes the rm -rf error handling (e.g., silently continuing instead of failing) would not be caught. The failure message 'failed to remove worktree directory' also goes untested.

Suggested fix: Add a test that makes the worktree path unremovable (e.g., chmod a-w on parent directory or an unremovable subdirectory), then verifies worktree-remove.sh exits with status 1 and emits the expected error message.

title: No test for cleanup_on_error preserving non-empty parent in worktree-create hook
description:
worktree-create.sh's cleanup_on_error (lines 29-34) has a conditional: it only removes the parent directory if it is empty after removing the failed worktree. Existing tests verify the empty-parent case (parent is removed on failure), but there is no test verifying that a non-empty parent is preserved when another worktree already exists.

Location: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 29-34, .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats.

If this conditional were accidentally removed, a failure creating a second worktree would delete the parent along with any existing sibling worktrees — silent data loss for any active sibling agents.

Suggested fix: Add a test that pre-creates a sibling worktree in the parent directory, triggers a jj workspace add failure, and verifies the sibling worktree is still present after cleanup_on_error runs.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: PR adds jj plugin with jujutsu skill, /jj-init command, VCS detection across all pr-review skills/agents, VCS-aware worktree hooks, and release config updates. 53 files, +5937/-189.

title: VCS detection preamble refactored into shared reference file vs inline design
description:
The jj VCS support design (docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md, section 2) specifies embedding the VCS detection preamble directly in each skill and agent file as inline markdown.

The implementation instead creates a shared reference file pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md and has each agent reference it by path. All 12 agents and 3 skills now point to this shared file.

This is a better design (DRY principle, single source of truth) but is an undocumented deviation from the spec. The jj VCS support design explicitly showed the preamble as inline content to be added to each file. No updated plan captures this architectural decision.

The jj VCS support plan (Task 6, Step 2) also says 'Insert a VCS Detection section' with inline content — the implementation uses a reference path instead.

Files: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md, all agent .md files
close_reason: By design: shared reference file is a deliberate DRY improvement over inline-per-agent

title: detect_repo_root supports non-colocated jj repos beyond design scope
description:
The jj VCS support design (docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md, Out of Scope section) explicitly states: 'Non-colocated jj repos (only colocated .jj/ + .git/ supported)'.

The implementation in .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh (detect_repo_root function) adds a fallback to 'jj root' for non-colocated repos when git rev-parse fails. This extends support beyond the documented design scope.

The worktree-remove.sh also documents this limitation: 'Non-colocated jj repos cannot create worktrees via this hook system, so they should never reach this removal path.' — suggesting the create path may fail gracefully anyway.

This enhancement is beneficial but represents an undocumented scope expansion. No design update or ADR records this decision.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh (detect_repo_root function)
close_reason: By design: non-colocated jj support is a deliberate scope expansion beyond the original design

title: release-please package key deviates from design spec
description:
The jj VCS support design (docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support.md Task 9) specifies adding a 'jj' package entry to release-please-config.json. The implementation instead uses 'jj/skills/jujutsu' as the package key (with package-name 'jj') for consistency with other skill packages.

This deviation from the design is documented inline with a comment in release-please-config.json explaining the rationale. The manifest also uses 'jj/skills/jujutsu' as the key.

This is an undocumented deviation — no updated design doc or ADR exists to record this intentional change. The inline comment is a partial mitigation but does not follow the project's pattern of keeping design docs updated.

Files: release-please-config.json, .release-please-manifest.json
close_reason: By design: release-please key jj/skills/jujutsu matches the actual directory structure for skill-level versioning

title: worktree-remove.sh comment incorrectly implies non-colocated jj repos can never create worktrees
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, comment above REPO_ROOT assignment.

The comment reads:
  '# Detect repo root — requires .git/ directory (present in colocated jj repos).
  # Non-colocated jj repos cannot create worktrees via this hook system,
  # so they should never reach this removal path.'

The claim that non-colocated repos 'cannot create worktrees via this hook system' is asserted but not enforced. worktree-create.sh uses detect_repo_root() which DOES have a jj root fallback for non-colocated repos. If a non-colocated jj repo somehow created a workspace via that path, the removal hook would fail silently at the git rev-parse step.

The comment is forward-looking documentation of intended behavior, but the 'should never reach' phrasing overstates certainty. It would be more accurate to say 'if reached from a non-colocated repo, the hook will fail cleanly and log an error.'

Fix: Change comment to: 'Detect repo root via git rev-parse (works in git repos and colocated jj repos). Non-colocated jj repos are not supported by this hook; if reached, the hook exits with an error.'

title: Design doc uses '--bookmark' long-form flag inconsistently with SKILL.md '-b' short-form for jj git push
description:
File: docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md, orchestrator phase mapping table and jj flow description.

The design doc uses 'jj git push --bookmark <pr-bookmark>' in multiple places (lines approx 2284, 2343, 2468, 2658 in the diff).

The implemented SKILL.md, jj-git-interop.md, and vcs-equivalence.md all use the short form '-b'. While '--bookmark' and '-b' are both valid flags (confirmed via jj git push --help), readers cross-referencing the design doc against the SKILL.md will see two different forms for the same flag.

This is minor since both forms are correct, but the inconsistency could cause confusion when following the design doc as a reference.

Fix: Update design doc to use '-b' consistently, matching the SKILL.md convention. Or add a note that '-b' is the short form of '--bookmark'.
close_reason: Won't fix: design docs are historical references; flag inconsistency with SKILL.md is acceptable for archival content

title: AGENTS.md uses 'jj git push --all' which may push unintended bookmarks in PR workflows
description:
File: AGENTS.md, 'Landing the Plane' section.

The mandatory push step says:
  'jj git push --all'

jj git push --all pushes ALL local bookmarks to the remote, including unrelated bookmarks. In a PR workflow, the correct command is 'jj git push -b <pr-bookmark>' to push only the PR's bookmark.

The address-findings SKILL.md (Phase 6: Ship) correctly uses 'jj git push -b <pr-bookmark>'. AGENTS.md contradicts this by recommending --all.

Risk: An agent working on a PR and following AGENTS.md could accidentally push unfinished bookmarks from other work-in-progress changes.

Fix: Change to 'jj git push -b <bookmark>' (where bookmark is the current PR branch), or use 'jj git push --tracked' to only push tracked bookmarks. Add a note that '--all' should only be used when all local bookmarks are ready to push.

title: verification-runner output spec uses weaker documentation for discriminated union than fix-worker
description:
fix-worker.md documents the VCS discriminated union output contract with explicit MUST language in a dedicated 'Discriminated union rules' section (pr-review/agents/fix-worker.md lines added in this PR around the WORKTREE_BRANCH/CHANGE_ID fields).

verification-runner.md adds the same VCS discriminant fields (VCS, WORKTREE_BRANCH, CHANGE_ID) to its output template but documents them only as inline comments ('(git only)', '(jj only)') followed by a prose note, without the MUST-level enforcement language.

The schema of the two output contracts should be documented with the same strength so orchestrators parsing both outputs follow identical omit-vs-include rules.

File: pr-review/agents/verification-runner.md (output section, lines added in diff around line 6140-6152)

Fix: Add a 'Discriminated union rules' sub-section to verification-runner's Output section matching fix-worker's explicit MUST language:
- When VCS: git — WORKTREE_BRANCH MUST be present, CHANGE_ID MUST be omitted
- When VCS: jj — CHANGE_ID MUST be present, WORKTREE_BRANCH MUST be omitted
- Omit means do not include the line at all (not an empty value)

title: CI drift-check script uses a while-read expansion loop where a direct jq query would suffice
description:
File: .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, lines ~1076-1086

The updated drift-check script resolves plugin-level config keys to skill dirs using a while-read loop with a case statement and inline path-safety check. This is valid, but the approach adds complexity for a config file that is only ever modified intentionally by maintainers.

The safety check (rejecting keys with [^a-zA-Z0-9_./-]) is justified as defense-in-depth, but wrapping it around a while-read loop makes the script harder to read at a glance.

A cleaner alternative that preserves the safety check and handles both plugin-level and skill-level keys:

  configured=$(jq -r '.packages | keys[] | select(. \!= ".")' release-please-config.json | while read -r key; do
    [[ "$key" =~ [^a-zA-Z0-9_./-] ]] && { echo "::error::Unsafe key: $key"; exit 1; }
    if [[ "$key" == */skills/* ]]; then
      echo "$key"
    else
      find "$key/skills" -name SKILL.md -exec dirname {} \; 2>/dev/null
    fi
  done | sort -u)

This is the same logic with the case statement replaced by a simpler if/else, reducing visual complexity by removing the case/esac wrapper.
close_reason: Won't fix: the while-read loop is needed to expand plugin-level keys via find; a pure jq query cannot do filesystem expansion

title: fix-worker output: WORKTREE_BRANCH field now conditional — orchestrators parsing it unconditionally will fail on jj repos
description:
File: pr-review/agents/fix-worker.md

The fix-worker output schema changes from always emitting WORKTREE_BRANCH to a discriminated union: git repos emit WORKTREE_BRANCH, jj repos emit CHANGE_ID. The VCS field is new and required to disambiguate.

The address-findings orchestrator handles the missing VCS field via an inference fallback (lines 6599-6606 of diff). This is good defensive coding. However, the orchestrator fallback also needs to handle the case where VCS is present but WORKTREE_BRANCH is absent (jj path) — it does handle this correctly by checking CHANGE_ID instead.

No actual breakage for existing git-only consumers because:
1. The VCS inference fallback correctly recovers when VCS is omitted
2. All agent files are updated atomically in this PR

This is a suggestion to document the migration contract explicitly: any external orchestrators (outside this repo) that parse fix-worker output will need to handle the new discriminated union or will fail when VCS=jj. The current fix is correct but the contract change should be noted in a changelog or migration note.
close_reason: Acknowledged: orchestrator already handles both VCS modes correctly with fallback inference

title: AGENTS.md duplicates CLAUDE.md beads workflow content verbatim
description:
File: AGENTS.md (new file, lines 1-168)

AGENTS.md was added to provide beads onboarding and workflow instructions to agents. However, the bulk of its content (lines 1199-1326, marked <!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION --> through <!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->) duplicates content that is also present in .beads/README.md.

More importantly, the beads onboarding content (bd workflow, types, priorities, auto-sync rules) is boilerplate generated by 'bd init' and is already available to any agent that runs 'bd onboard'. Committing it into AGENTS.md creates a second copy that will drift from the canonical source.

The unique content in AGENTS.md (the 'Non-Interactive Shell Commands' section and 'Landing the Plane' workflow) is genuinely useful. The duplicated bd reference material could be replaced with a single line: 'For bd command reference, run bd onboard or see .beads/README.md.'

This reduces AGENTS.md from 168 lines to roughly 50, making the unique guidance more visible.

title: Seven implementation plan docs committed to main branch after feature is complete
description:
Files: docs/plans/2026-03-06-worktree-isolation-fix-design.md, docs/plans/2026-03-06-worktree-isolation-fix.md, docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md, docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support.md, docs/plans/2026-03-07-pr31-review-findings-fix.md, docs/plans/2026-03-07-pr31-review-fixes.md, docs/plans/2026-03-08-pr31-turn3-review-fixes.md

Seven planning documents (design docs + implementation plans) are committed in this PR. These documents served their purpose during development but now add noise to the repository and could mislead future readers who may not realize the plans are already executed.

The implementation plans in particular (worktree-isolation-fix.md, jj-vcs-support.md, and the pr31-review-* variants) contain step-by-step instructions with explicit 'commit after each step' directives — intended as execution guides, not reference documentation.

Suggestion: Either move completed plans to a docs/plans/archive/ subdirectory to signal they are historical, or simply omit them from the PR. The design docs (2026-03-0{6,7}-*-design.md) have more archival value as ADRs and could be renamed to docs/adr/ if kept.
close_reason: Won't fix: design docs serve as architectural decision records; removing them would lose context for future maintainers

title: beads metadata.json committed with project UUID - low-sensitivity but irreversible
description: In .beads/metadata.json (committed to the repo), the file contains a project_id UUID: 9a7f7f3d-0591-461f-98b4-63067e4fba78 and database name fzymgc_house_skills. These are non-secret configuration values and .beads/ is intentionally committed (the .beads/.gitignore excludes only runtime/DB files). However, the project_id UUID is a persistent identifier that could be used to correlate Dolt sync activity. This is low risk as it is not a secret, but it is an identifier that cannot be rotated without breaking existing synced clones. The BEADS README also includes a curl | bash install pattern (line 131: curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash) — this is a documentation recommendation to users and not an active workflow step, but should be noted as a pattern the repo is endorsing to collaborators who read AGENTS.md.
close_reason: Won't fix: metadata.json is committed by bd init and is normal beads behavior; UUID is low-sensitivity

title: BATS test_worktree_remove.bats 'jj path: calls jj workspace forget' inlines a one-off mock instead of using helpers
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats, lines ~768-785

The test 'jj path: calls jj workspace forget with correct name' inlines another one-off jj mock that writes its args to a log file. This is the same pattern as the inline mock in test_worktree_create.bats (forward --name test).

Both tests exist for argument-logging purposes. A shared create_logging_jj_mock helper in helpers.bash that writes args to '${REPO_ROOT}/jj-args.log' would serve both. The current duplication means two separate places to update if the mock interface changes.

title: validate_safe_name regex allows names starting with a dot-non-dot sequence
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-helpers.sh line 6, the validate_safe_name regex is: [[ "$name" =~ [^a-zA-Z0-9_.-] || "$name" == .* || "$name" == *".."* ]]. This correctly rejects names starting with dot and names containing double-dot. However, the condition "$name" == .* uses glob matching in bash which tests if the name STARTS with a literal dot. Due to operator precedence in the compound [[ ]] expression, the OR chain evaluates left-to-right without parentheses: the regex match for bad chars, then dot-prefix, then double-dot. This is correct as written but is subtle. More concerning: the regex character class [^a-zA-Z0-9_.-] uses a literal hyphen at the end of the class which is correct, but a name of exactly '-' (a single hyphen) would pass the regex check (hyphen is in the allowed set), pass the .* check (doesn't start with dot), and pass the *..* check (no double dot). A single hyphen is harmless as a basename for mkdir/git, but documents the boundary case. Recommend adding an explicit test for single-hyphen and single-underscore names to document the acceptance, and add a test for a name that is exactly '-' to confirm it is handled correctly by downstream git/jj commands.
close_reason: Fixed in 2d2.12: validate_safe_name now rejects leading dots and trailing dots

title: BATS test 'jj path: forwards --name flag' inlines a one-off mock instead of using create_mock_jj from helpers
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats, lines ~506-528

The test 'jj path: forwards --name flag to jj workspace add' writes an inline jj mock script instead of using create_mock_jj() from helpers.bash. The inline mock has the same --help/workspace-add structure as create_mock_jj, but additionally logs args and uses mkdir -p.

The logger line is the only unique part. This could be extracted as a create_logging_jj_mock helper in helpers.bash, eliminating ~12 lines of duplicated mock scaffolding per similar inline mock.

Suggested fix: Add create_logging_jj_mock to helpers.bash that also writes '$@' to a log file, then use it in the test.

title: Behavioral evals B4 and B6 in jj/evals/evals.json have zero automated assertions
description: jj/evals/evals.json evals B4 ('split commit warns interactive') and B6 ('workspace name collision') each contain only a single assertion of type 'manual'. Manual assertions are invisible to automated eval runners — a regression in either behavior produces no CI signal. B4 could add an output_contains check for 'interactive' or 'cannot' to verify the warning text is present. B6 could add an output_contains check for 'already exists' or 'conflict' or 'unique'. Even a weak automated assertion is better than none, since it will catch complete failures (e.g., skill silently doing the wrong thing instead of warning). The fully manual check can remain as a secondary assertion for human review.
close_reason: By design: B4 and B6 have manual assertions appropriate for behavioral evals (interactive operations and collision handling cannot be auto-verified)

title: CI rumdl download uses pinned version but no signature verification
description: In .github/workflows/check-skills.yml lines 56-62, the rumdl binary is downloaded from GitHub Releases using a pinned version tag (v0.1.43) and sha256 checksum verification is performed (sha256sum -c). This is a significant improvement over the prior approach (which fetched the latest tag dynamically). However, the sha256 file itself is downloaded from the same GitHub Releases endpoint — if that release was compromised, both the binary and its hash would be attacker-controlled. There is no GPG/Sigstore signature verification. For a developer tooling workflow this is acceptable risk, but consider: (1) pinning the SHA256 inline in the workflow file so it cannot be swapped, or (2) using a GitHub Actions hash pinning approach (cosign/sigstore). Also: actions/checkout@v4 is used without SHA pinning — consider pinning to a specific commit SHA for supply chain hardening.
close_reason: Won't fix: rumdl doesn't publish GPG signatures; sha256 checksum verification is already present and sufficient

title: BATS test uses git -c commit.gpgsign=false which bypasses commit signing but is not portable
description: In .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats and test_worktree_remove.bats, test setup uses 'git -c commit.gpgsign=false commit --allow-empty -m init'. This is a valid workaround for environments that enforce GPG signing, but it would be cleaner and more portable to use 'GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL=/dev/null git commit --allow-empty -m init' or set GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM=1. Minor issue -- the current approach works correctly but the -c flag pattern is non-idiomatic. Not a blocking issue.

title: Empty parent dir cleanup not tested for jj worktree removal path
description: worktree-remove.sh lines 77-81 clean up the empty _worktrees parent directory after removal. The git path test 'cleans up empty parent directory after removal' verifies this behavior. However, there is no equivalent test for the jj path. The jj removal tests (test_worktree_remove.bats lines 760-803) verify that the workspace directory is removed but do not assert that an empty _worktrees parent is also cleaned up. A missing rmdir in the jj path (if a future refactor accidentally branched before the cleanup) would be undetected. Add a test that uses setup_jj_worktree + create_mock_jj and asserts that REPO_ROOT_worktrees no longer exists after removal.

title: worktree-remove.sh non-colocated jj repo falls through to git rev-parse with no actionable guidance
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, repo root detection uses only git rev-parse (no jj fallback), with the comment 'Non-colocated jj repos cannot create worktrees via this hook system, so they should never reach this removal path.' If this assumption is ever violated (e.g. a user manually creates a workspace in a non-colocated jj repo, then invokes the remove hook), the error message is: 'ERROR: could not determine repo root (git rev-parse failed)' with no mention of jj. A user seeing this on a jj-only repo has no idea what went wrong or how to manually clean up the workspace. The error message should surface the jj assumption: e.g. 'ERROR: could not determine repo root — non-colocated jj repos are not supported by this hook; run jj workspace forget <name> manually'.

title: No BATS test for jj branch mkdir failure in worktree-create.sh
description: worktree-create.sh lines 55-58 handle mkdir failure in the jj code path (ERROR: failed to create worktree parent directory). The git path has a symmetric block and is tested by the 'fails gracefully when mkdir -p fails' BATS test. However, that test does not set up .jj/, so it only exercises the git branch. The jj branch mkdir failure is not covered. A test should: (1) call setup_jj, (2) make the sibling parent dir unwritable, (3) verify exit 1 and the ERROR message. Criticality is lower because the logic is structurally identical to the tested git path, but the jj branch is a distinct shell branch.

title: rm -rf in worktree-remove.sh suppresses error message with 2>/dev/null before checking exit code
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh around line 1042 (diff context), the directory removal is: if \! rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH" 2>/dev/null; then echo ERROR ... exit 1; fi. The 2>/dev/null discards the system-level error message (e.g. 'Permission denied', 'Device busy') before the exit code is checked. When rm -rf does fail, the error output printed to the operator is generic ('failed to remove worktree directory') with no indication of why. Fix by removing 2>/dev/null and letting stderr flow naturally, or capturing it: if \! rm_err=$(rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH" 2>&1); then echo "ERROR: failed to remove worktree directory '$WORKTREE_PATH': $rm_err" >&2; exit 1; fi. Same pattern applies to rm -rf in cleanup_on_error in worktree-create.sh line 878.

title: Redundant file-type guard in VCS detection preamble
description: In pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md line 6 (step 1), the detection command is: 'if test -d .jj && [ \! -f .jj ]; then echo jj'. The condition 'test -d .jj' already proves .jj is a directory; '[ \! -f .jj ]' is then always true (a path cannot simultaneously be a directory and a regular file). The guard is therefore dead code that can never change the outcome. Suggested fix: remove the '[ \! -f .jj ]' clause entirely. The comment explaining it ('prevents a regular file named .jj from being mistaken for a jj repository directory') is also misleading because 'test -d .jj' already handles that case.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Comprehensive review of PR #31: New jj plugin with jujutsu skill, /jj-init command, VCS detection preamble in all pr-review skills/agents, worktree hooks VCS-aware, vcs-equivalence reference, evals

title: respond-to-comments allowed-tools uses broad Bash(jj *) vs review-pr's specific git patterns
description: respond-to-comments/SKILL.md line 13 adds 'Bash(jj *)' as a broad wildcard while also having 'Bash(git *)' as a broad wildcard (line 12). This is consistent with address-findings which uses the same pattern, but contrasts with review-pr which uses 'Bash(jj *)' broad alongside very specific git subcommands (git diff *, git log *, git status, etc.). There is no API contract breakage here since allowed-tools is an additive permission list, but the inconsistency in granularity across skills is worth noting: respond-to-comments and address-findings use broad 'Bash(git *)' while review-pr uses narrow per-subcommand git permissions. If the intent was to apply least-privilege, respond-to-comments and address-findings should enumerate specific jj subcommands (jj st, jj log, jj diff, jj bookmark, jj git push, etc.) rather than the wildcard. Files: pr-review/skills/respond-to-comments/SKILL.md line 13, pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md line 16, pr-review/skills/review-pr/SKILL.md line 26.

title: BATS tests inline mock jj binaries that duplicate the shared helpers
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats lines 92-111, 149-163, 165-176, 235-250, 252-263
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_remove.bats lines 100-116, 127-138

The helpers.bash file defines create_mock_jj and create_failing_jj_mock to avoid duplicating mock binary creation. However, multiple tests still write their own inline mock binaries:

In test_worktree_create.bats:
- L89: 'jj path: forwards --name flag' — custom mock with arg-logging
- L149: 'jj path: rejects old jj without --name support' — duplicates create_failing_jj_mock's --help response but with different workspace add output
- L165: 'jj path: exits with error when jj workspace add --help fails' — standalone mock
- L235, L252: orphan tests also define custom mocks

In test_worktree_remove.bats:
- L99: 'jj path: calls jj workspace forget with correct name' — custom mock
- L127: 'jj path: handles workspace forget failure' — standalone mock

Some of these are genuinely unique (arg-logging mock). Others could extend create_failing_jj_mock by accepting parameters or could be moved into helpers.bash as named variants (e.g., create_old_jj_mock, create_broken_jj_mock) to reduce the scattered inline heredocs.

title: review-gate accepts jj diff format but has no jj-specific validation in its output contract
description: review-gate.md Environment section (lines 22-25) documents that it accepts VCS diff input in either git-style or jj-style format, and refers readers to vcs-equivalence.md for interpretation. However, the output contract (lines 70-74) is unchanged — it only returns per-finding PASS/FAIL lines with no VCS field. This is intentional (review-gate is read-only and doesn't report VCS identifiers). However, the address-findings Phase 4c orchestrator (SKILL.md line 400-401) generates the diff using VCS-conditional commands, but the prompt template shows 'Generate the diff:' inline within the prompt block, meaning the orchestrator must already have computed the diff before dispatching review-gate. This is consistent but the dependency on the orchestrator pre-computing a jj diff using 'jj diff --from <pre-rebase-change-id> --to <pr-bookmark>' creates a new failure mode: if the pre-rebase change-id was not captured before the rebase operation, the review-gate cannot receive the correct diff. The pre-rebase snapshot requirement should be explicitly documented as a contract precondition. File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md lines 388-402 and pr-review/agents/review-gate.md lines 19-25.

title: worktree-create.sh: jj version probe runs on every invocation when a simpler version check suffices
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/../worktree-create.sh lines 45-58

The jj version guard is three sequential steps:
1. command -v jj (check installed)
2. jj workspace add --help (run the binary)
3. grep -- '--name' (check flag presence in output)

Steps 2-3 can fail independently, so they have separate error messages. However, steps 1 and 2 are partially redundant: if 'command -v jj' succeeds, 'jj workspace add --help' can still fail — but the only thing the help-probe provides (beyond the existence check) is the --name flag presence check.

A simpler alternative is to check the jj version number directly with 'jj version' and compare against the minimum that added --name support (0.18.0, released 2024-02). This would collapse the two probe steps into one and provide a clearer user-facing error ('jj 0.18+ required, found 0.X.Y') rather than the opaque '--name flag not found in help output' message.

Note: the current approach is defensively correct (it works without knowing the exact version boundary). This is a suggestion, not a bug. The inline comment at line 49 ('Runtime version probe: jj is user-installed...') does document the rationale. The question is whether that rationale justifies the three-step probe versus a single version check.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Fixed: The inline comment at worktree-create.sh lines 42-45 documents the rationale for runtime probing over version checking. Also added TOCTOU acknowledgment note (w6x.3). Current approach is defensively correct.

title: BATS: three orphan directory leak tests duplicate the version/help check tests
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats lines 225-263

The 'orphan directory leak tests' section (lines 225-263) adds three tests:
- 'jj path: cleans up WORKTREE_PARENT when jj not installed' (L227)
- 'jj path: cleans up WORKTREE_PARENT when jj version too old' (L235)
- 'jj path: cleans up WORKTREE_PARENT when jj --help fails' (L252)

Each of these corresponds to an existing test that already asserts failure (exit code 1). The existing tests at lines 73, 149, and 165 assert the same error conditions but do not check whether the worktree parent directory was cleaned up.

Rather than duplicate the entire test body, the cleanup assertion ([ ! -d "${REPO_ROOT}_worktrees" ]) could be added to the existing tests. This would eliminate ~40 lines (three full mock setups plus invocations) and keep each error condition covered by a single test.

Suggested fix: add the orphan directory assertion to the three existing error tests (L73-78, L149-163, L165-176) and remove the orphan directory leak section.

title: BATS: two jj cleanup tests cover the same code path and can be merged
description:
File: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats lines 113-119 and 178-185

Two tests cover the jj cleanup-on-failure path using the same create_failing_jj_mock:

- Line 113: 'jj path: cleans up on jj workspace add failure' — checks worktree dir is removed
- Line 178: 'jj path: removes empty WORKTREE_PARENT on jj workspace add failure' — checks parent dir is also removed

Both tests run the identical script invocation with the same mock. They differ only in which path they assert no longer exists. Merging them into one test halves the mock setup overhead and removes the implicit suggestion that these are distinct behaviors.

Suggested fix: consolidate into a single test named 'jj path: cleans up worktree and parent on workspace add failure' with both assertions.

title: Design plan doc uses non-existent jj workspace root command
description: docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md line 96 shows 'jj workspace root' as the jj equivalent for 'Workspace identity'. This command does not exist in jj. The correct approach (as implemented in vcs-equivalence.md) is 'jj log -r @ --no-graph -T change_id.short(8)...' or similar. While this is a historical design doc, it may mislead future readers. The implementation in pr-review/references/vcs-equivalence.md correctly uses the jj log template form.

title: jujutsu SKILL.md TOC omits the See Also section
description: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md has a Contents TOC (lines 17-28) that lists 10 sections but omits the final 'See Also' section (line 301). The TOC entry '[See Also](#see-also)' is missing. Suggested fix: add '- [See Also](#see-also)' as the last item in the Contents list.

title: worktree-create.sh installs lefthook for jj workspaces — spec restricts it to git-only
description:
The implementation plan (Task 8, Step 2) shows lefthook installation inside the 'else' (git) branch only:

  else
    git worktree add ...
    if [[ -f lefthook.yml ]]; then
      (cd worktree && lefthook install)
    fi
  fi

The implementation runs lefthook install AFTER the if/else block (lines 82-87 of .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh), so it runs for both git AND jj workspaces. The inline comment says 'lefthook works in both VCS modes', which is correct — lefthook is VCS-agnostic.

This is a beneficial improvement (agents in jj workspaces also get hooks), but it deviates from the spec without documentation.

File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh lines 82-87

Suggested fix: Document this decision in the design spec or add a comment in the hook explaining why lefthook is installed for both VCS types. No functional change needed.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Fixed: The inline comment at worktree-create.sh line 76 ('lefthook works in both VCS modes') already documents this intentional deviation. lefthook is VCS-agnostic.

title: VCS detection preamble refactored to shared reference file — undocumented architectural deviation
description:
The design spec (Section 2) and implementation plan (Task 6, Step 2 and Task 7, Step 1) specify that VCS detection preamble content be embedded inline in each skill and agent's Environment block. The implementation instead created a shared reference file at pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md and has all 12 agents and 3 skills point to it via 'Follow the procedure in pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md'.

This is a beneficial architectural improvement (DRY, single source of truth for the detection procedure), but it is not documented in the design spec or plan.

Files affected: All 12 pr-review/agents/*.md, all 3 pr-review/skills/*/SKILL.md
New file: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md

Suggested fix: Update the design doc (docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md) to note the shared preamble file approach, or add a comment in the implementation plan marking this as a refinement. No functional change needed.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Fixed: The shared reference file approach (vcs-detection-preamble.md) is self-documenting — the file itself and CLAUDE.md's Worktree Layout section explain the architecture. No additional documentation needed.

title: release-please package key deviates from spec: 'jj/skills/jujutsu' used instead of 'jj'
description:
The implementation plan (Task 9, Step 1) specifies adding the jj package with key 'jj' and manifest entry 'jj': '0.1.0'. The implementation uses 'jj/skills/jujutsu' as the package key in both files.

File: release-please-config.json line 86
File: .release-please-manifest.json line 10

The key determines where release-please writes CHANGELOG.md. With 'jj/skills/jujutsu', the changelog will be at jj/skills/jujutsu/CHANGELOG.md rather than jj/CHANGELOG.md. The 'jj/skills/jujutsu' key is more consistent with other skill packages (homelab/skills/grafana, pr-review/skills/review-pr), which is a reasonable refinement, but it deviates from the spec without documentation.

Suggested fix: Document the rationale in the design spec or an ADR comment. The 'jj/skills/jujutsu' key is arguably more consistent with the established skill-directory pattern.

title: B-VCS1 and B-VCS2 evals are near-duplicates with no distinguishing scenario
description: pr-review/evals/evals.json evals B-VCS1 and B-VCS2 test the same behavioral contract: an agent running in a jj default workspace reports STATUS: FAILED and mentions 'default workspace'. The prompts differ (B-VCS1 is generic, B-VCS2 references a specific bead ID) but the assertions are identical. One of these should be replaced with a distinct scenario — e.g., an agent in a named worktree workspace (the success case), or the fix-worker reporting VCS: git in a jj repo (mismatch handling). As-is, running both evals provides no incremental coverage signal.

title: B-JJ3 eval does not verify jj workspace forget precedes rm -rf
description: pr-review/evals/evals.json eval B-JJ3 checks that 'jj workspace forget' and 'rm -rf' both appear in the output, but uses separate output_contains assertions with no ordering constraint. The critical requirement is that workspace metadata is cleaned up BEFORE the directory is removed (otherwise jj workspace forget would fail on a missing path). The eval should either use a manual assertion explicitly checking ordering, or add a note that the two operations must be sequential. As written, a broken agent that calls rm -rf first and then fails jj workspace forget would still pass both output_contains assertions if it emits both strings.

title: No negative-trigger eval for jujutsu skill (git-specific prompts)
description: jj/evals/evals.json has two negative-trigger evals (T5: generic commit, T7: /jj-init slash command) but no eval verifying the skill does NOT trigger for a git-specific workflow prompt with no jj context (e.g., 'help me resolve this git merge conflict' with no .jj/ directory). This gap means if the skill's description over-matches on VCS keywords, there is no eval to catch the regression. A T8 eval like: prompt='help me with this git merge conflict', context='no .jj/ directory', expected_skill=null would close this gap.

title: git worktree prune failure path has no test coverage
description: worktree-remove.sh lines 68-70 contain a double-fallback path: when git worktree remove --force fails, the script falls back to git worktree prune, and if that also fails it emits a second WARNING. No test in test_worktree_remove.bats covers this nested failure case. The existing test 'falls back to rm when git worktree remove fails' only tests the outer fallback and does not trigger the prune step. A test mocking both git commands to fail would verify the second WARNING is emitted and the script still completes successfully.

title: address-findings VCS fallback omits error handling when VCS detection commands fail
description:
File: pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md, lines 272-279

The fallback logic executed when fix-worker omits the VCS field uses a simplified detection pattern:
  test -d .jj && echo jj || echo git

Unlike the canonical vcs-detection-preamble.md, this simplified form:
1. Does not handle the 'none' case (no .jj/ and no .git/) — it silently defaults to 'git' in any non-jj directory, including non-repo directories
2. Does not verify the subsequent VCS commands (jj workspace list, git branch --show-current) succeeded before using their output
3. The inferred VCS is used to drive git cherry-pick vs jj rebase integration — a wrong inference would cause a silent integration failure that corrupts the PR branch

Suggested fix: Either reference the canonical preamble procedure inline, or add a 'none' guard:
  if test -d .jj; then INFERRED_VCS=jj; elif test -d .git; then INFERRED_VCS=git; else log error and skip integration; fi

title: VCS detection uses test -d .jj without guarding against .jj being a regular file
description:
Files: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md (line 8), .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh (line 43), .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh (line 57)

All VCS detection uses the pattern: test -d .jj (or [[ -d ... ]])
This silently returns false if .jj exists as a regular file rather than a directory, causing code to fall through to git logic in a jj repo. In worktree-create.sh and worktree-remove.sh, subsequent git rev-parse and git worktree commands will fail with confusing errors that don't mention the real cause.

Suggested fix: Add an explicit check for the file case:
  if [[ -d "${REPO_ROOT}/.jj" ]]; then
    # jj path
  elif [[ -e "${REPO_ROOT}/.jj" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: .jj exists but is not a directory" >&2
    exit 1
  else
    # git path
  fi

The preamble reference document should also be updated to match.

title: TOCTOU race between jj version probe and workspace creation
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh (lines 51-58), the script first probes 'jj workspace add --help' to check if --name is supported, then makes a separate invocation to actually run 'jj workspace add'. Between these two calls, jj could be replaced on the PATH (e.g., via a compromised PATH entry or a race during concurrent tool updates). In a developer workstation context the practical risk is very low, but the check could be simplified by running workspace add directly and interpreting failure — or by capturing the version once and using it for both checks. No sanitization issue; error output from jj is correctly captured in  and printed to stderr only.

title: Review: PR #31 — feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support
description: Comprehensive review of PR #31: New jj plugin with Jujutsu VCS support, VCS detection preamble in all pr-review skills/agents, VCS-aware worktree hooks, and related config updates. 55 files changed, 6004+/183-.

title: jj/evals/evals.json skill_name 'jj' does not match registered skill name 'jujutsu'
description:
File: jj/evals/evals.json line 2.

Both jj/evals/evals.json and pr-review/evals/evals.json correctly use 'check' as the assertion value field — no inconsistency there.

However, jj/evals/evals.json line 2 has 'skill_name': 'jj' which does not match the registered skill name 'jujutsu' (as declared in jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md frontmatter 'name: jujutsu').

If the eval framework uses 'skill_name' to look up or filter evals by skill, passing 'jj' instead of 'jujutsu' will cause evals to be miscategorized or skipped. The xpy.39 finding fixed 'jj-plugin' to 'jj' but should have gone further to 'jujutsu'.

Suggested fix: Change line 2 of jj/evals/evals.json from 'skill_name: jj' to 'skill_name: jujutsu' to match the skill name in SKILL.md frontmatter.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — jj/evals/evals.json already has skill_name: jujutsu at HEAD (366dff8).

title: VCS detection implemented as separate reference file instead of design-specified inline preamble
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md (new file); pr-review/skills/*/SKILL.md; pr-review/agents/*.md

Design doc (docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support-design.md, Section 2 'VCS Detection Preamble') specifies each skill and agent should receive an inline '## VCS Detection' section with the detection logic embedded directly.

Actual implementation created a separate reference file pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md and all skills/agents reference it with: 'Follow the procedure in pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md to detect VCS...'

This is a better DRY architecture than the spec'd inline approach. The separate file eliminates 12 copies of duplicated content and allows updates in one place. However it deviates from the documented design.

No fix required — the implementation is architecturally superior. This is noted for design doc completeness; consider updating the design doc to reflect the actual pattern.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: intentional design choice. A shared reference file (vcs-detection-preamble.md) is better than inlining VCS detection in each of 12 agent files — reduces duplication and ensures consistency when the detection logic changes.

title: Release-please config key 'jj/skills/jujutsu' deviates from implementation plan's specified 'jj' key
description:
File: release-please-config.json, .release-please-manifest.json, .github/workflows/check-skills.yml

The implementation plan (docs/plans/2026-03-07-jj-vcs-support.md, Task 8, Step 1-2) explicitly specifies: add 'jj' package key to release-please-config.json and '"jj": "0.1.0"' to .release-please-manifest.json.

Actual implementation uses 'jj/skills/jujutsu' as the key in both files. This is architecturally consistent with the homelab skill-level pattern (homelab/skills/grafana, etc.) but deviates from the plan.

Additional consequence: The CI drift-check was updated with a code path handling 'plugin-level keys (e.g., jj)' (check-skills.yml lines ~970-980), but since no plugin-level key was added, that expansion branch is dead code that will never execute.

Suggested fix: Remove the dead-code plugin-level expansion branch from the drift-check, and either document the key format choice or align it with the plan.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: intentional deviation. Using skill-level key 'jj/skills/jujutsu' is consistent with the homelab pattern (homelab/skills/grafana, etc.) and the check-skills.yml drift check handles both plugin-level and skill-level keys correctly.

title: release-please-config.json package-name 'jj' conflicts with plugin directory naming convention
description:
File: release-please-config.json lines 86-99.

The jj plugin is registered with package-name 'jj' but the config key is 'jj/skills/jujutsu'. This inconsistency means the package-name in release notes would say 'jj' while the CHANGELOG would be generated under jj/skills/jujutsu/. The homelab/pr-review plugins use skill-level keys matching their actual package names (e.g., 'grafana', 'terraform').

Additionally, the extra-files list at lines 90-94 bumps jj/plugin.json version via jsonpath $.version, but the jj plugin-level entry does NOT exist in release-please-config.json as a separate package (no 'jj' top-level key for plugin.json bumping separate from the skill). This is unlike pr-review which has a separate 'pr-review' package entry that bumps pr-review/plugin.json.

The fix plan (xpy.30) identified this and proposed moving to 'jj/skills/jujutsu' as the key (already done). No breaking change currently, but the package-name 'jj' vs key 'jj/skills/jujutsu' divergence may confuse release-please version bump targeting.

Suggested fix: Either align package-name to 'jujutsu' (matching the skill name and directory) or add a separate top-level 'jj' package entry for plugin.json version bumping, matching the pr-review pattern.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: finding is stale — release-please-config.json has separate 'jj' top-level entry (package-name: jj) matching the pr-review pattern. Both exist at HEAD (366dff8).

title: fix-worker output contract changed: VCS field added, orchestrator fallback recovery required
description:
File: pr-review/agents/fix-worker.md, Output section; pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 4 step 5.

The fix-worker agent output format is a breaking change from the prior version. Previously it returned only WORKTREE_BRANCH; now it returns a discriminated union requiring a VCS field:
- When VCS: git — WORKTREE_BRANCH MUST be present, CHANGE_ID MUST be omitted
- When VCS: jj — CHANGE_ID MUST be present, WORKTREE_BRANCH MUST be omitted

The orchestrator (address-findings SKILL.md Phase 4 step 5) has a fallback recovery path if VCS field is missing, but this adds complexity and implicit behavior that could mask agent bugs.

The contract is well-documented in fix-worker.md and vcs-equivalence.md (discriminated union rules are explicit). However, any external orchestrator or automation that consumed the old WORKTREE_BRANCH-only format will break silently (no VCS field = fallback inference).

Note: The PR plan doc marked xpy.35/xpy.42 as 'by design / informational' and closed them. Raising as a COMPATIBLE suggestion to ensure the output format change is surfaced in release notes or changelog.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: not applicable (duplicate of fpr.17). VCS discriminant is part of initial implementation — no prior agents to maintain backward compat with.

title: Empty-parent directory check duplicated verbatim in worktree-create.sh and worktree-remove.sh
description:
The pattern for removing an empty parent directory is copy-pasted between the two hook scripts:

worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error() lines 38-40:
  if [[ -d "$WORKTREE_PARENT" ]] && [[ -z "$(ls -A "$WORKTREE_PARENT")" ]]; then
    rmdir "$WORKTREE_PARENT" 2>/dev/null || echo "WARNING: failed to remove empty parent..." >&2
  fi

worktree-remove.sh lines 80-82:
  if [[ -d "$PARENT" ]] && [[ -z "$(ls -A "$PARENT")" ]]; then
    rmdir "$PARENT" 2>/dev/null || true
  fi

The two copies also have different error handling: create.sh warns on failure, remove.sh silently ignores it (|| true). The inconsistency is a subtle maintenance hazard.

An alternative idiom that is shorter and avoids spawning a subshell for ls:
  rmdir --ignore-fail-on-non-empty "$PARENT" 2>/dev/null || true

However this flag is not available on macOS (only GNU coreutils). The current approach is correct for portability; the issue is purely the duplication and inconsistent error handling between the two copies.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. cleanup_empty_parent() was already extracted to worktree-helpers.sh and is sourced by both scripts.

title: PR #31 API contract review
description: API contract review findings for PR #31 feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: all children resolved.

title: review-gate.md uses inline VCS detection instead of referencing vcs-detection-preamble.md like all other agents
description:
All 10 worktree-isolated review agents (api-contract-checker, code-reviewer, code-simplifier, comment-analyzer, fix-worker, pr-test-analyzer, security-auditor, silent-failure-hunter, spec-compliance, type-design-analyzer, verification-runner) use the standardized indirection pattern in their Environment section:

  'You are running in an isolated worktree. Follow the startup procedure
  in `pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md` to detect VCS
  and verify your location before proceeding.'

review-gate.md instead has inline VCS detection steps:

  '1. Detect VCS: `test -d .jj && echo jj || echo git`
   2. Use the detected VCS for interpreting diffs (jj diffs vs git diffs).
      Consult `pr-review/references/vcs-equivalence.md` for command equivalents.'

This is inconsistent with all other agents. It also notes a separate issue (vcs-equivalence vs vcs-detection-preamble.md), but the simplification concern is purely the divergent pattern.

File: pr-review/agents/review-gate.md, Environment section (lines ~5250-5255 in the diff)

Suggested fix: Replace the Environment section with the standard 2-line reference to vcs-detection-preamble.md used by all other agents. Note that review-gate may not need full startup procedure since it is not worktree-isolated (it receives diffs from orchestrator), but the inconsistency should be resolved either by aligning with the standard pattern or by making review-gate's special status explicit.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. review-gate.md now says 'you receive the VCS diff as input' and references vcs-equivalence.md only for interpreting diff syntax.

title: Duplicated name-validation regex in hook scripts should be extracted to a helper function
description:
The same 3-condition regex for name safety appears verbatim four times across two files:

- .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh line 16: NAME validation
- .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh line 28: REPO_NAME validation
- .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 21: WORKSPACE_NAME validation
- .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 38: REPO_NAME validation

Pattern repeated each time:
  [[ "$VAR" =~ [^a-zA-Z0-9_.-] || "$VAR" == .* || "$VAR" == *..* ]]

A validate_safe_name() helper at the top of each script would eliminate this within-file duplication and centralize the error message format. The scripts are not sourced from each other so the function must exist in both, but within each file the two call-sites would share it:

  validate_safe_name() {
    local val=$1 label=$2
    if [[ "$val" =~ [^a-zA-Z0-9_.-] || "$val" == .* || "$val" == *..* ]]; then
      echo "ERROR: $label '\' contains unsafe characters" >&2; return 1
    fi
  }

This also makes it easier to update the allowed-character set in one place per script if requirements change.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. validate_safe_name() was already extracted to worktree-helpers.sh and is called from both scripts.

title: PR #31 code simplification review
description: Code simplification findings for PR #31 (feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support). Covers clarity, redundancy, and maintainability issues in the changed code.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: all children resolved.

title: vcs-equivalence.md 'Workspace identity' row uses overly verbose jj template that differs from Quick Reference in SKILL.md
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-equivalence.md, Command Mapping table (diff line ~5747).

The 'Workspace identity' row has jj command: jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8) ++ " " ++ description.first_line()'

Meanwhile jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md Quick Reference has 'Current location': jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)'

These two tables document the same conceptual operation with different templates. The vcs-equivalence.md version includes description.first_line() but SKILL.md does not. An agent using vcs-equivalence.md to check workspace identity gets a richer output, but an agent using SKILL.md gets a terser one. Neither is wrong, but the inconsistency means agents using different reference files will produce different output formats for the same operation.

Also the 'Current location' row in the same vcs-equivalence.md table (line 5736) uses yet a third variant: jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.short(8)' — which matches SKILL.md but differs from the Workspace identity row in the same file.

Suggested fix: Standardize on one template across both files for 'current location/identity'. If description is useful, add it to both. If not, remove it from vcs-equivalence.md.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale — vcs-equivalence.md already uses consistent change_id.short(8) template.

title: worktree-create.sh comment 'Runtime version probe' is slightly misleading — it happens per invocation not per session
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh, jj version probe comment (diff line ~843).

The comment reads: 'Runtime version probe: jj is user-installed and can be updated independently, so we check --name support on each invocation.'

The word 'invocation' is accurate here, but the comment claims the reason for the probe is that jj 'can be updated independently.' This is true but it implies the check is guarding against version changes between runs — when the real reason is that the hook has no compile-time dependency on jj's version so it must probe at runtime.

The comment is not wrong, but it could cause a maintainer to think: 'if we pin jj to a specific version, we can remove this check.' In reality the check should remain even with a pinned jj version because the hook has no way to verify the version at install time.

Suggested fix: Update to: 'Runtime capability probe: jj is user-managed and may not be in PATH or may predate --name support. Check before each workspace creation rather than assuming a minimum version is present.' This makes the guard's purpose (presence + capability check) more clear.

title: CLAUDE.md Worktree Layout gotcha still says 'Do NOT manually create worktrees inside the sibling directory' but intended prohibition is creation outside the hooks
description:
File: CLAUDE.md, Worktree Layout gotcha section (diff line ~1248).

The section says: 'Do NOT manually create worktrees inside the <repo>_worktrees/ sibling directory.'

This wording is ambiguous. The intended meaning is 'do not manually create worktrees at all — use the hook system.' But the literal reading could be interpreted as 'the sibling directory is off-limits' rather than 'manual creation is off-limits.' An agent could comply literally by creating worktrees somewhere else (like inside the main repo), which is exactly what the sibling layout was designed to prevent.

Also, the old text (pre-PR) said 'Do NOT manually create worktrees inside .claude/worktrees/' — the reference directory has changed in the new text, but the old directory name is still mentioned in the docs/plans/2026-03-06-worktree-isolation-fix-design.md file (Task 7, Step 1 template), which can cause confusion.

Suggested fix: Reword to 'WorktreeCreate/WorktreeRemove hooks handle worktree creation automatically. Do NOT manually create worktrees — always use the hook system or `jj workspace add`/`git worktree add` via the hooks.' This makes the prohibition about manual creation, not about the directory.

title: jj-git-interop.md workspace section says 'forget does NOT delete files' but is inconsistent with removal workflow
description:
File: jj/skills/jujutsu/references/jj-git-interop.md, Workspaces section.

The comment for jj workspace forget says: 'De-register workspace (does NOT delete files)'.

This is accurate in isolation, but the surrounding removal workflow in address-findings SKILL.md and the worktree-remove.sh hook both run rm -rf AFTER jj workspace forget. A developer reading jj-git-interop.md in isolation will learn 'forget does NOT delete files' but will not understand that callers are EXPECTED to delete the directory manually after forget.

The omission creates a knowledge gap: someone unfamiliar with the two-step pattern (forget + rm -rf) might call jj workspace forget and consider the cleanup complete, leaving orphaned directories.

The same description appears in jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md Quick Reference: 'Remove workspace: jj workspace forget <name>' — no mention of rm -rf.

Suggested fix: Add a parenthetical or note: 'De-register workspace metadata (does NOT delete the directory — run rm -rf <path> separately).' Optionally add a complete two-step example to the Workspaces section.

title: jujutsu SKILL.md describes jj split as unavailable but omits jj restore --from alternative for splitting
description:
File: jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md, Refining Commits section.

The note says: 'jj split is interactive and not safe for agents. To split a commit manually, create a new change, move specific files, then squash the rest.'

This is accurate but incomplete. The suggested workflow ('create a new change, move specific files, then squash') is non-trivial and requires restoring specific files from a prior revision — which uses jj restore --from <rev> <path>. The restore command IS listed in the Abandon/Undo/Restore section, but the connection between that command and the 'how to split' workaround is not made explicit.

An agent reading only the 'jj split is not safe' note would have to independently discover that 'move specific files' means 'use jj restore --from'. The connection should be spelled out.

Also, the Agent Environment Rules table lists 'jj split' as interactive, but does not list 'jj log -r' with a pager-triggering flag. The 'always use --no-pager' note in rules is good but the constraint table only covers split/resolve/squash -i.

Suggested fix: Update the split note to read: 'To split a commit without jj split: (1) create a new empty change on top with jj new, (2) restore specific files with jj restore --from @- <path>, (3) run jj squash on the remainder.' This makes the workaround concrete and references the restore command already documented in the file.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale — addressed by dr0 fixes in current HEAD.

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md: 'none' VCS branch silently proceeds if check is omitted by agent
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md lines 8-10

Step 1 says: 'If the result is none, STOP and report STATUS: FAILED'. This is a prompt instruction to an LLM agent, not enforced code. An agent that skips or misreads the detection step will proceed with VCS operations in a directory with no VCS, leading to confusing git/jj command failures downstream with no diagnostic context.

This is inherent to the prompt-based agent design (not a code bug), but the preamble could be hardened by making the stop condition more prominent or by adding a shell snippet: 'test -d .jj || test -d .git || { echo "STATUS: FAILED -- No VCS detected"; exit 1; }'. This converts the prose instruction into something agents can copy-paste to enforce deterministically.

Suggested fix (minor): add an explicit shell snippet for the 'none' case to make it harder for agents to accidentally skip the check.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: not applicable. This is inherent to prompt-based agent design. The preamble already provides clear STOP instructions. Adding shell enforcement would require modifying agent architecture beyond this PR's scope.

title: worktree-remove.sh comment claims non-colocated jj repos cannot reach removal path but has no gate
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh, comment near REPO_ROOT detection (diff line ~900).

The comment reads: 'Detect repo root — requires .git/ directory (present in colocated jj repos). Non-colocated jj repos cannot create worktrees via this hook system, so they should never reach this removal path.'

The comment documents a correctness assumption ('they should never reach this removal path') but provides no enforcement mechanism. The assumption is only true because worktree-create.sh also uses git rev-parse — but a non-colocated jj repo could have an orphaned worktree directory from manual creation, and that would silently fall through to the 'could not determine repo root' error rather than getting a clear explanation.

The comment also says 'Non-colocated jj repos cannot create worktrees via this hook system' — this is an assertion about a causal relationship that a reader cannot verify without reading worktree-create.sh too. The comment would be more accurate if it explicitly linked the precondition to worktree-create.sh.

Suggestion: Update comment to: 'Detect repo root — requires git rev-parse (.git/ directory). In colocated jj repos (.jj/ + .git/), this succeeds. Pure jj repos without .git/ cannot reach this path because worktree-create.sh also uses git rev-parse and would have failed during creation.'

title: worktree-remove.sh: rmdir on empty parent swallows errors via '|| true'
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 81

'rmdir "$PARENT" 2>/dev/null || true' silently swallows failures. While this is deliberately lenient (rmdir succeeds when directory is truly empty; the '|| true' handles the case where it isn't empty because another worktree is still present), there is no distinction between a benign 'directory not empty' failure and a permissions-based failure that would be useful to diagnose.

The worktree-create.sh cleanup_on_error already upgraded from '2>/dev/null || true' to a warning message pattern. Consistency suggests worktree-remove.sh should log a WARNING instead of silently suppressing all rmdir errors.

Suggested fix: rmdir "$PARENT" 2>/dev/null || echo "WARNING: could not remove parent directory '$PARENT' (may not be empty)" >&2

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. cleanup_empty_parent() in worktree-helpers.sh uses 'rmdir ... || echo WARNING' — errors are logged, not silently swallowed.

title: worktree-remove.sh: rm -rf WORKTREE_PATH runs unconditionally with no error capture
description:
File: .claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh line 76

'rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH"' has no error capture. With set -euo pipefail active, a failure here (permissions issue, read-only filesystem) will cause the script to exit with a non-zero status and no diagnostic message. The comment says rm -rf exits 0 if the path doesn't exist, which is correct, but a permissions failure would exit non-zero and produce no actionable output beyond the bare shell error. This is a minor hardening gap — the rm failure would surface via the shell exit code, but the missing log message makes debugging harder.

Suggested fix (minor): wrap in a conditional with a warning message:
  rm -rf "$WORKTREE_PATH" || echo "WARNING: rm -rf failed for '$WORKTREE_PATH'" >&2

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. worktree-remove.sh lines 82-85 now have 'if \! rm -rf; then echo ERROR; exit 1; fi' for both git and jj paths.

title: PR #31 error handling review
description: Silent failure and error handling audit for PR #31 feat(jj): add Jujutsu VCS support

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: all children resolved.

title: jj evals missing negative-trigger for explicit git-only request in git repo
description: jj/evals/evals.json has one negative-trigger eval (T5: generic commit should not trigger jujutsu). There is no negative eval for an explicit git-only request in a git repo — e.g. prompt 'git commit these changes' with no .jj/ present should not activate the jujutsu skill. Without this, false-positive skill activation in pure-git repos is untested. Suggested fix: add eval T-NEG2 with prompt 'git commit these changes' (no .jj/ context), expected_skill: null, assertion type: skill_not_triggered for jujutsu.

title: No test for double git worktree failure (remove + prune both fail) in worktree-remove.sh
description: worktree-remove.sh git path falls back to git worktree prune when git worktree remove fails, and emits a second WARNING if prune also fails (.claude/hooks/worktree-remove.sh lines ~940-945). The existing test 'git path: surfaces warning when git worktree remove fails' covers only the first failure. There is no test for the double-failure path where prune also fails. Suggested fix: add a BATS test with a mock git that fails on both 'worktree remove' and 'worktree prune', verifying both WARNING lines appear and status is still 0.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: fixed. Test 'git path: exits non-zero when both git worktree remove and prune fail' was added in the P1 fix commit.

title: No test for mkdir failure in worktree-create.sh (jj and git paths)
description: worktree-create.sh has an explicit error branch for mkdir -p WORKTREE_PARENT failing (lines ~832-835), but no BATS test covers this path for either the jj or git code path. A read-only parent directory would trigger this. File: .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh. Tests file: .claude/hooks/tests/test_worktree_create.bats. Suggested fix: add a test that makes the intended parent unwritable and verifies exit 1 with 'failed to create worktree parent directory' in output.

title: PR #31 test coverage review

title: vcs-detection-preamble.md references '(current)' marker that may not match jj workspace list output
description:
File: pr-review/references/vcs-detection-preamble.md lines 9-14

The preamble instructs agents: 'Output shows one workspace per line: <name>: <path> with (current) marking the active workspace.'

The actual jj workspace list output format is '<name>: <path>' with the working copy workspace indicated differently depending on jj version. Agents relying on '(current)' as a literal string marker may fail to identify their active workspace correctly.

The SKILL.md at jj/skills/jujutsu/SKILL.md says 'jj workspace list shows all workspaces with their path (format: <name>: <path>)' without mentioning any marker, which is more defensible.

Suggested fix: Either remove the '(current)' marker reference and instruct agents to verify workspace identity by matching pwd against the path in each workspace line, or verify the exact jj output format and update accordingly. The instruction 'Verify the current workspace name starts with worktree-' using pwd matching is more reliable.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. vcs-detection-preamble.md lines 24-25 now say 'Do NOT rely on jj workspace list output; jj 0.39+ does not emit a (current) marker.' The detection uses path matching instead.

title: release-please-config.json jujutsu skill package-name is 'jj' instead of 'jujutsu'
description:
File: release-please-config.json line 88

The jj/skills/jujutsu package entry has 'package-name': 'jj', using the plugin name. All other skill-level packages use the skill name as package-name (e.g., grafana, terraform, skill-qa, review-pr, address-findings, respond-to-comments). The jujutsu skill should use 'package-name': 'jujutsu' for consistency.

This affects release-please PR titles and changelog entries, which will say 'jj' instead of 'jujutsu'.

Fix: Change 'package-name': 'jj' to 'package-name': 'jujutsu' in the jj/skills/jujutsu package entry in release-please-config.json.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale — addressed by dr0 fixes in current HEAD.

title: beads README.md: install script uses curl | bash (untrusted code execution)
description: In .beads/README.md line 128, the Quick Start example shows: 'curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash'. This is a classic curl-pipe-bash anti-pattern that executes unreviewed remote code from the 'main' branch (not a pinned commit or tagged release). If the remote script is compromised or the URL is hijacked, any developer following this doc would execute malicious code. This is documentation in a committed file, so it represents endorsed guidance. Recommendation: either (a) pin to a specific commit hash in the URL, (b) recommend downloading and inspecting before executing, or (c) link to the beads GitHub releases page for manual download. Note: this is a docs file, and the README is auto-generated by 'bd init', so it may need to be fixed upstream in the beads tool itself.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: not applicable. beads is an external dependency — its README is not part of this repo. The install pattern is standard for CLI tools.

title: worktree-create.sh: WORKTREE_PARENT created before VCS checks, leaving orphan on validation failure
description: In .claude/hooks/worktree-create.sh line 43, 'mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT"' is called before the jj version guard (lines 56-63). If jj is installed but too old (missing --name support), the script exits on line 61 after already having created the WORKTREE_PARENT directory. The cleanup_on_error function (line 36-41) attempts to remove WORKTREE_PATH and an empty WORKTREE_PARENT, but the jj version guard exits with 'exit 1' on line 62 WITHOUT calling cleanup_on_error. This leaves the empty WORKTREE_PARENT directory on disk. While not a security vulnerability, it is a correctness issue. Fix: move 'mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT"' to just before the VCS-specific workspace creation commands (after all validation passes), or ensure all early-exit error paths call cleanup_on_error.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. All jj guard failures call cleanup_on_error() which removes WORKTREE_PATH and calls cleanup_empty_parent() on WORKTREE_PARENT.

title: CI: actions/checkout pinned to mutable tag (@v4) not SHA digest
description: All three jobs in .github/workflows/check-skills.yml use 'actions/checkout@v4' (a mutable semver tag). If the tag is moved via supply-chain compromise, the CI runner executes arbitrary code with GITHUB_TOKEN permissions. The change in this PR introduced @v4 (replacing non-existent @v6), so the pattern is in scope. File: .github/workflows/check-skills.yml, lines 11, 37, 48. Recommendation: pin to SHA digest, e.g., 'actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683 # v4.2.2'. Low-risk for a private repo but a standard hardening gap per GitHub Actions security best practices.

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: stale. Commit a63162d already pinned actions/checkout to SHA (34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4).

title: jj fix config generator: suggest fix.tools config for a repo
description: Create a skill or command that inspects a repo's file types, existing linter configs (ruff, prettier, eslint, gofmt, clang-format, etc.), and generates a recommended [fix.tools.*] config block for .jj/repo/config.toml. Should detect: pyproject.toml/ruff.toml → ruff format, package.json with prettier → prettier, go.mod → gofmt, .clang-format → clang-format. Output a ready-to-paste TOML block. Could be a /jj-fix-config slash command or a model-invoked skill.

title: Unquoted $BOOKMARK variable in address-findings jj checkout snippet
description:
In pr-review/skills/address-findings/SKILL.md Phase 1 Step 3, the jj checkout code reads:
  BOOKMARK=$(gh pr view <number> --json headRefName --jq .headRefName)
  jj edit $BOOKMARK

The variable $BOOKMARK is unquoted. If a PR branch name contains spaces or shell metacharacters (unlikely but possible), this would break. The assignment also lacks error checking — if 'gh pr view' fails, BOOKMARK will be empty and 'jj edit' will receive no argument.

Fix: quote the variable and add error checking:
  BOOKMARK=$(gh pr view <number> --json headRefName --jq .headRefName) || { echo 'ERROR: gh pr view failed'; exit 1; }
  jj edit "$BOOKMARK"

comments.author: seanb4t
comments.text: Closed: subsumed by h6b.31 — that finding already includes quoting $BOOKMARK and error checking

value: jj-fix-command: jj fix runs configured formatters (ruff, prettier, etc.) across mutable commit history. Configure via [fix.tools.<name>] with command + patterns in config.toml. Key: deterministic tools only, deduplicates across commits, never creates new conflicts, auto-propagates to descendants. Unlike jj absorb (routes working-copy changes to ancestors by blame), jj fix pipes file content through tools and rewrites commits in place.

jj-rebase-onto-flag: jj rebase destination flag is --onto / -o (NOT -d / --destination). The -d flag was deprecated. All jj rebase commands must use -o for destination. Updated across all skills/agents 2026-03-14.

jj-skip-emptied-semantics: --skip-emptied on jj rebase abandons commits that BECOME empty after rebase (their content is already in the new parent). Does NOT abandon commits that were ALREADY empty before rebase. Never skips merge commits with multiple non-empty parents. This is the critical flag for squash-merge reconciliation workflows.

jj-tug-alias: The tug alias (jj bookmark move --from 'heads(::@ & bookmarks())' --to @) solves the most common jj footgun: bookmarks don't auto-advance. Workflow becomes: edit → jj tug → jj git push. Configured in Sean's user config alongside sync and landed aliases.

jj VCS rule: When jj is available (jj root succeeds), MUST use jj commands for ALL VCS operations — commits, workspaces, rebases, status, etc. Never use mutating git commands in jj repos (including colocated). Read-only git commands and gh CLI are safe. This applies to workspace creation too: jj workspace add, never git worktree add.

jj-workspace-git-limitation: Secondary jj workspaces (created with jj workspace add) do NOT get a .git/ directory — only .jj/. Tools requiring .git/ (IDEs, npm, nix, gh CLI) may not work in secondary workspaces. PR jj-vcs/jj#4644 tracks this. The primary workspace in a colocated repo has both .jj/ and .git/.
